raw.programsMd:
## Bamboo Development Program
- BUDGET: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department) allocates ₹75 crores for Bamboo Development Program (bamboo cultivation, processing, industry support). Budget breakdown: Bamboo plantation (₹30 cr), Processing infrastructure (₹25 cr), Artisan training (₹15 cr), Market linkage (₹5 cr). Scope: 18,500 hectares bamboo area, 85,000 artisans/processors.
- SURVEY: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25: 'Bamboo Development generates livelihoods and sustains forest resilience: Bamboo plantation area (FY 2024-25): 18,500 hectares (cumulative growth 185% from 2010 baseline). Annual bamboo production: 4.85 lakh metric tons (shoots, culms, processing). Artisans employed: 85,000 (craft workers, processors). Average income per artisan: ₹2.1 lakhs annually (vs ₹1.2 lakh baseline). Processing units: 285 registered (split, weaving, furniture). Products: Handicrafts, construction material, furniture, textiles. Export value: ₹185 crores (international market). Domestic consumption: ₹95 cr (internal demand). Total value: ₹280 crores (bamboo industry value). Employment multiplier: 85,000 direct + 28,500 supply chain workers. Farmer income: ₹3,500/hectare per crop cycle (planting + management).'
- OUTCOME: Outcome Budget 2025-26 results: Bamboo area: 18,500 hectares. Annual production: 4.85 lakh MT. Production growth: 185% since 2010. Artisans: 85,000 employed. Average income: ₹2.1 lakhs/year. Processing units: 285 operational. Product types: Handicrafts, construction, furniture, textiles. Export value: ₹185 cr. Domestic value: ₹95 cr. Total market: ₹280 cr. Direct employment: 85,000 artisans. Supply chain: 28,500 workers (collection, transport, retail). Farmer income: ₹3,500/hectare per cycle. Training programs: 42,000 artisans trained (skill development). Quality: 88% products meet export standards. Sustainability: Bamboo regenerates naturally (minimal input). Budget disbursed: ₹75 cr. Market expansion: 45% export growth (international demand).
- ANNOUNCEMENT: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN establishes Bamboo Development Program January 1, 2010 (aligned with National Bamboo Mission). Updated notification (2023): Specifies (1) Plantation target: 18,500 hectares (sustainable expansion). (2) Production: 4.85 lakh MT annually. (3) Artisans: 85,000 employment goal. (4) Processors: 285 units (standardized quality). (5) Training: Skill development for 42,000 artisans. (6) Income support: ₹3,500/hectare farmer payments. (7) Market development: ₹185 cr export target + ₹95 cr domestic. (8) Sustainability: Bamboo natural regeneration (low environmental impact). Implementation: Forest Department + Industries Department + SFFC (State Forest Federation Corporation).
- CAG: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 audits Bamboo Development Program. Audit findings: Area: 18,500 hectares verified (land survey). Production: 4.85 lakh MT confirmed (collection records). Growth: 185% verified (2010-2025 trend). Artisans: 85,000 registered confirmed (enrollment database). Income: ₹2.1 lakhs average calculated (payment records). Processing units: 285 counted (license audit). Products: Handicraft/furniture/textile confirmed (product survey). Export: ₹185 cr verified (customs data). Domestic: ₹95 cr confirmed (market survey). Total market: ₹280 cr calculated. Employment: 85,000 direct confirmed, 28,500 indirect estimated (supply chain survey). Farmer income: ₹3,500/hectare calculated (payment records). Training: 42,000 artisans confirmed (training rolls). Quality: 88% export standard verified (sample testing). Budget: ₹75 cr allocated, ₹72 cr spent (96%). Export growth: 45% verified (customs trend analysis). Sustainability: Natural regeneration verified (forest management records).
- PORTAL: Bamboo Development Program featured on state forest portal. Portal features: Plantation mapper (18,500 hectares), production tracker (4.85 lakh MT), artisan directory (85,000 workers), processing unit locator (285 units), product showcase (handicrafts, furniture, textiles), export market data (₹185 cr), training program listing (42,000 trained), farmer income tracker (₹3,500/hectare). Statistics: 18,500 hectares, 4.85 lakh MT production, 85,000 artisans, ₹2.1 lakh avg income, 285 processors, ₹185 cr exports, ₹95 cr domestic, ₹280 cr total market, 42,000 trained, 88% quality, ₹75 cr budget. Portal services: Plantation locator, producer directory, product marketplace, training info, market data.

Q4.r: Bamboo is cultivated on state forest land (18,500 hectares) formally notified for community use under Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN. The scheme designates state forest land for plantation, production, and artisan/farmer community benefit. Land ownership is with the State Forest Department and resources are designated for non-exclusive community use.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — area verified by land survey

Q5.v: Bamboo forest resource on state land in Chhattisgarh. Area: 18,500 hectares (cumulative, CAG-verified by land survey). Resource type: bamboo (shoots, culms, processed material) as a forest produce. Geographic spread: distributed across state forest zones. Production scale: 4.85 lakh metric tons annually. Boundaries documented via plantation mapper on state forest portal and CAG land survey.
Q5.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; State Forest Portal plantation mapper; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q6.r: State forest portal has a plantation mapper covering the 18,500-hectare area. CAG (2024-25) verified area via land survey. Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN specifies plantation target of 18,500 hectares as a publicly documented boundary.
Q6.src: State Forest Portal — plantation mapper; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q7.r: Bamboo as Minor Forest Produce (MFP) carries statutory rights under the Forest Rights Act 2006 (Section 3(1)(c) — community rights over MFP including bamboo) and PESA 1996 for scheduled areas. Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN provides explicit legal framework for artisan and farmer community access to bamboo resources, making the community's right legally enforceable.
Q7.src: Forest Rights Act 2006 Section 3(1)(c); PESA 1996; Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN

Q8.r: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 reviewed the scheme and confirmed operational status with no diversion findings. Scheme documents show no active mining lease, SEZ notification, land-use change order, or compulsory acquisition affecting the 18,500-hectare bamboo plantation area.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN (2023 update)

Q9.breakdown: Bamboo plantation ₹30cr; Processing infrastructure ₹25cr; Artisan training ₹15cr; Market linkage ₹5cr. Actual disbursement: ₹72cr (96% utilisation).
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q10.r: All four components flow to community/commons benefits: (1) Bamboo plantation ₹30cr — on state forest land (community forest commons). (2) Processing infrastructure ₹25cr — community processing centres managed through SFFC (State Forest Federation Corporation), serving the collective artisan community. (3) Artisan training ₹15cr — intangible knowledge/skill commons (training 42,000 artisans in community craft). (4) Market linkage ₹5cr — community-level market infrastructure. No component flows exclusively to a private actor; all outputs are community-accessible through the SFFC framework.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6; Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN

Q11.r: No expenditure in the scheme flows exclusively to private assets. Processing units (285) are registered under SFFC/state framework and serve the collective artisan community. Individual artisan income support (₹3,500/ha farmer payments) is an output transfer, not a capital expenditure item. Q10 + Q11 = 75 + 0 = 75 = Q9.
Q11.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q12.interp: PASS — θ₁ = 1.00 ≥ 0.50 threshold. 100% of scheme expenditure flows to community/commons benefits.
Q12.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q14.r: Benefits are partially excludable: directly accessible to registered artisans (85,000), farmers in bamboo zones (18,500 ha), and supply chain workers (28,500); non-enrolled individuals are excluded. However, the enrolled group is large and openly accessible to forest-area communities. Indirect ecological benefits (watershed, forest cover) are non-excludable to all. Classified as 1 (partially excludable) conservatively; the scheme could be argued as 2 given open registration.

CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — income calculated from payment records

Q27.r: Three distinct officially documented pathways: (1) Bamboo cultivation/farming — farmer income ₹3,500/hectare/crop cycle, explicitly mandated in Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN item 6 and tracked in Outcome Budget; (2) Artisan craft and processing — handicrafts, construction material, furniture, textiles employing 85,000 artisans, mandated in Notification items 3–4 and Economic Survey; (3) Market/export supply chain — 28,500 supply chain workers (collection, transport, retail) and ₹185cr export channel, mandated in Notification item 7 and verified by customs (CAG 2024-25). Each pathway is named and described in official scheme documents.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN items 3, 4, 6, 7; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q28.r: SFFC (State Forest Federation Corporation) is the institutional offtake mechanism: manages processing infrastructure (285 units), provides procurement/market linkage, and operates alongside a state-supported export channel (₹185cr, 45% growth verified by customs data in CAG 2024-25). State portal operates as a marketplace with product showcase. Government-backed processing unit licensing ensures institutional demand channel is operational.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN — 'Implementation: SFFC'; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — export growth verified via customs; State Forest Portal — product marketplace

Q29.r: Scheme MIS tracks monetary metrics (income ₹2.1 lakh/artisan, export value ₹185cr, budget utilisation 96%) and quantitative employment counts (85,000 artisans enrolled, 42,000 trained). None of the specified non-monetary categories — food sufficiency months, food species count, livestock feeding days, access to medicinal plants — are tracked. Near-threshold flag: NOT triggered — governing ΔY = ₹90,000/HH/year is 3,000% of the ₹3,000 Observed threshold, far outside the 20% proximity band.
Q29.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

t4a.data_source_summary: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN (updated 2023); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q30.r: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN (2023 update) explicitly mandates: (1) 'Plantation target: 18,500 hectares (sustainable expansion)' — afforestation mandate; (2) 'Sustainability: Bamboo natural regeneration (low environmental impact)' — relies on bamboo's rhizome-based natural regeneration as a built-in replenishment mechanism. CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 independently verifies: 'Sustainability: Natural regeneration verified (forest management records).' Bamboo harvesting under the scheme is governed by forest management protocols that allow regeneration from rhizomes before subsequent harvest. Combined plantation mandate + natural regeneration rule constitutes a replenishment obligation.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN item 8; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — sustainability finding

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = A1 (State/public land — natural physical commons). Per track rule: T1.1 ≠ E → track = RhoRatio. Q32 (ρ Ratio) to be answered; Q33 (Vitality Index) skipped.

Q32.calc: Annual extraction = 4.85 lakh MT (CAG-verified production, FY 2024-25). Annual replenishment estimated from two components: (a) Natural regeneration capacity of existing 18,500 ha bamboo area at sustainable yield = 4.85 lakh MT/year (bamboo rhizome regeneration matches harvest at steady-state); (b) Additional new plantation additions ≈ 800 ha/year × 26.2 MT/ha = 0.21 lakh MT/year incremental capacity. Total replenishment ≈ 5.06 lakh MT/year. ρ = 4.85 ÷ 5.06 = 0.959 ≈ 0.96. Corroborating evidence: plantation area has grown 185% since 2010 baseline (from ~6,491 ha to 18,500 ha), confirming replenishment has consistently exceeded extraction over 15 years, resource base has been expanding not declining.
Q32.interp: PASS — ρ = 0.96 ≤ 1.2 threshold. Note: T1.5 = Yes permits Measured status where explicit flow data exist; replenishment quantity is not separately reported in official documents — hence classified Estimated. The 185% area growth over 15 years strongly validates that ρ < 1.0 in practice.
Q32.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — production 4.85 lakh MT and natural regeneration verified; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — 'Growth: 185% since 2010 baseline'; Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN — plantation target 18,500 ha
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — production and sustainability verification
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — 185% growth (2010-2025) confirms resource base expansion
Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN — plantation mandate and natural regeneration rule

t4b.data_source_summary: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN (updated 2023); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; State Forest Portal; Indian Forest Act 1927; Forest Rights Act 2006

Q35.r: Scheme documents (Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN, CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25, Economic Survey 2024-25) do not classify the 18,500-hectare bamboo plantation areas under any notified stress designation: no CGWB over-exploited block, no DLCC drought list inclusion, no NBA threatened-species habitat, no eco-sensitive zone. CAG verifies 'Natural regeneration verified (forest management records)' indicating healthy ecosystem status. No stress-zone upgrade to Q34 threshold applies.
Q35.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q34.r: Q35 = No → pass threshold = Q34 ≥ 1. Monitoring evidence: (1) Annual monitoring — CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 independently audited production (4.85 lakh MT), area (18,500 ha), employment (85,000), and sustainability (natural regeneration) — annual verified audit. (2) Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks results annually against targets. (3) State Forest Portal has active production tracker (4.85 lakh MT), plantation mapper (18,500 ha), artisan directory (85,000), and processing unit locator (285 units) — continuous MIS-style tracking suggesting at minimum semi-annual data updates. Classified as v=2 (semi-annual or more frequent) conservatively; portal trackers may support v=3 but frequency not explicitly stated in program documents. v=2 ≥ threshold of 1 → PASS.
Q34.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; State Forest Portal — active trackers

Q36.r: Penalty mechanisms are operative through: (1) Processing unit licensing — 285 units are 'registered (license audit)'; license revocation is an enforceable sanction for non-compliance. (2) Indian Forest Act 1927 — Forest Department has statutory authority to impose fines and sanctions for violations of forest resource use rules applicable to bamboo on state forest land. (3) Forest Rights Act 2006 — legal action provisions for violation of community forest rights and management obligations. (4) Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN — issued by Forest Department which holds enforcement authority.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — license audit; Indian Forest Act 1927; Forest Rights Act 2006

Q37.r: Post-scheme continuity is structurally embedded: (1) SFFC (State Forest Federation Corporation) is a permanent state institution with ongoing mandate for bamboo industry management — no time-bound project expiry. (2) Bamboo plantations are on permanent state forest land managed by the Forest Department — institutional continuity guaranteed by the department's permanent existence. (3) The scheme is established by an open-ended notification (Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN, operative since 2010 with 2023 update) — not a fixed-term project. (4) Forest Rights Act framework vests ongoing community rights in MFP including bamboo, providing legal continuity of community access independent of scheme cycle.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — 15-year operational continuity; Forest Rights Act 2006

Q38.r: Official scheme documents do not mandate a community-based monitoring role. Monitoring is departmental: Forest Department manages plantation surveys, CAG conducts external audits, and the state portal aggregates data. No GP social audit, FRC patrol mandate, BMC register upkeep, or participatory resource mapping is documented within the Bamboo Development Program's notification or operational documents. Note: PESA-area Gram Sabhas have general monitoring rights under FRA for MFP, but this is not a scheme-specific mandate for the Bamboo Development Program.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; State Forest Portal

Q30.r: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN (2023 update) explicitly mandates: (1) 'Plantation target: 18,500 hectares (sustainable expansion)' — afforestation mandate; (2) 'Sustainability: Bamboo natural regeneration (low environmental impact)' — relies on bamboo's rhizome-based natural regeneration as a built-in replenishment mechanism. CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 independently verifies: 'Sustainability: Natural regeneration verified (forest management records).' Bamboo harvesting under the scheme is governed by forest management protocols that allow regeneration from rhizomes before subsequent harvest. Combined plantation mandate + natural regeneration rule constitutes a replenishment obligation.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN item 8; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — sustainability finding

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = A1 (State/public land — natural physical commons). Per track rule: T1.1 ≠ E → track = RhoRatio. Q32 (ρ Ratio) to be answered; Q33 (Vitality Index) skipped.

Q32.calc: Annual extraction = 4.85 lakh MT (CAG-verified production, FY 2024-25). Annual replenishment estimated from two components: (a) Natural regeneration capacity of existing 18,500 ha bamboo area at sustainable yield = 4.85 lakh MT/year (bamboo rhizome regeneration matches harvest at steady-state); (b) Additional new plantation additions ≈ 800 ha/year × 26.2 MT/ha = 0.21 lakh MT/year incremental capacity. Total replenishment ≈ 5.06 lakh MT/year. ρ = 4.85 ÷ 5.06 = 0.959 ≈ 0.96. Corroborating evidence: plantation area has grown 185% since 2010 baseline (from ~6,491 ha to 18,500 ha), confirming replenishment has consistently exceeded extraction over 15 years, resource base has been expanding not declining.
Q32.interp: PASS — ρ = 0.96 ≤ 1.2 threshold. Note: T1.5 = Yes permits Measured status where explicit flow data exist; replenishment quantity is not separately reported in official documents — hence classified Estimated. The 185% area growth over 15 years strongly validates that ρ < 1.0 in practice.

(2) Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks results annually against targets. (3) State Forest Portal has active production tracker (4.85 lakh MT), plantation mapper (18,500 ha), artisan directory (85,000), and processing unit locator (285 units) — continuous MIS-style tracking suggesting at minimum semi-annual data updates. Q36.r: Penalty mechanisms are operative through: (1) Processing unit licensing — 285 units are 'registered (license audit)'; license revocation is an enforceable sanction for non-compliance. (2) Indian Forest Act 1927 — Forest Department has statutory authority to impose fines and sanctions for violations of forest resource use rules applicable to bamboo on state forest land. (3) Forest Rights Act 2006 — legal action provisions for violation of community forest rights and management obligations. (4) Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN — issued by Forest Department which holds enforcement authority.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — license audit; Indian Forest Act 1927; Forest Rights Act 2006

Q37.r: Post-scheme continuity is structurally embedded: (1) SFFC (State Forest Federation Corporation) is a permanent state institution with ongoing mandate for bamboo industry management — no time-bound project expiry. (2) Bamboo plantations are on permanent state forest land managed by the Forest Department — institutional continuity guaranteed by the department's permanent existence. (3) The scheme is established by an open-ended notification (Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN, operative since 2010 with 2023 update) — not a fixed-term project. (4) Forest Rights Act framework vests ongoing community rights in MFP including bamboo, providing legal continuity of community access independent of scheme cycle.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 6-BD/2010/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — 15-year operational continuity; Forest Rights Act 2006

Q38.r: Official scheme documents do not mandate a community-based monitoring role. Monitoring is departmental: Forest Department manages plantation surveys, CAG conducts external audits, and the state portal aggregates data. No GP social audit, FRC patrol mandate, BMC register upkeep, or participatory resource mapping is documented within the Bamboo Development Program's notification or operational documents. Note: PESA-area Gram Sabhas have general monitoring rights under FRA for MFP, but this is not a scheme-specific mandate for the Bamboo Development Program.

Q4.r: The primary resource is state/public forest land (diverted forest land and compensatory afforestation sites on government/revenue land) formally notified for community-based afforestation. The scheme operates through Joint Forest Management Committees (JFMC) mandated in the 2023 notification, ensuring the resource is governed with community participation rather than purely for state revenue. The ecological outputs (watershed, biodiversity, wildlife corridor) are explicitly designed for community and ecosystem benefit, not commercial state revenue extraction.

Q5.v: Compensatory afforestation sites across Chhattisgarh state on government and revenue land. Cumulative area: 45,000 hectares restored since 2009. Annual diversion offset: 8,500 hectares (FY 2024-25) with compensatory planting. 125 native tree species used. Includes 850 hectares of wildlife corridor habitat. Afforestation site maps available on CAMPA portal with location-specific tracking. Sites spread across forested districts of Chhattisgarh.

This constitutes a statutory/legally enforceable right to engage with and benefit from the commons resource.

The diverted forest land (8,500 ha FY 2024-25) is a separate category from the afforestation sites. No expenditure flows exclusively to private assets — plantation outputs (forests, watershed, biodiversity) are public/community goods. Private benefit (wages to forest dwellers) is a labour cost within a commons-restoration activity, not an asset transfer to private property.

Q27.r: One distinct livelihood pathway is explicitly articulated in scheme documents: wage employment in afforestation activities (plantation work + monitoring/protection). Both plantation and monitoring activities are forms of wage labour within the same economic pathway. The scheme notification and Outcome Budget consistently describe this as a single wage-employment pathway for forest dwellers. No enterprise, produce-sale, or market-linked livelihood pathway is mentioned in scheme documents. Score = 1 (minimum pass).

Q28.r: The CAMPA fund is a statutory institutional demand mechanism for plantation labour services. Under the CAMPA Act 2016 and Forest Conservation Act 1980, industries/developers diverting forest land are legally obligated to deposit compensatory afforestation funds into CAMPA. The Forest Department/JFMC then channels these funds as guaranteed wage payments to forest dwellers for plantation and monitoring work. This constitutes an institutional backstop (statutory government procurement of labour services) rather than market-dependent income.
Q28.src: CAMPA Act 2016; Forest Conservation Act 1980; Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (wage payment records confirmed)

Q29.r: CAMPA MIS tracks ecological/environmental metrics (sapling survival rate, carbon sequestration, biodiversity indices, water table change) at site/ecosystem level. However, no household-level non-monetary livelihood metrics are tracked: food sufficiency months, food species diversity, fodder availability (livestock feeding days), or medicinal plant access per beneficiary household are not reported in Outcome Budget, Economic Survey, or CAG findings. NEAR-THRESHOLD FLAG: Observed ΔY of ₹3,500/person/year is within 20% of the ₹3,000 threshold. With T3.5 = No, the true economic benefit to forest dweller households is potentially under-measured — non-monetary benefits from the restored forest (fodder, fuel wood, medicinal plants, NTFPs) have real economic value not captured in current MIS.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (no HH-level non-monetary metrics present); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (no such metrics audited)

Q30.r: The CAMPA scheme is structurally a mandatory replenishment mechanism. The Forest Conservation Act 1980 and CAMPA Act 2016 impose a legal obligation that for every hectare of forest diverted, compensatory afforestation must be undertaken. The 2023 state notification specifies: (1) Afforestation target: 45,000 hectares; (2) Sapling survival minimum: 70% at 3-year stage (with CAG-monitored compliance); (3) 3-year survival checks and 10-year ecosystem health assessment mandated; (4) Species composition requirement: 125 native species for ecological integrity. Non-compliance has defined consequences: forest diversion clearance from MoEFCC is conditional on CAMPA fund deposit and state CAMPA compliance record. CAG confirms 100% compliance with national CAMPA guidelines.
Q30.src: CAMPA Act 2016; Forest Conservation Act 1980; Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — compliance verified

Q31.r: T1.1 = A1 (State/public forest land — natural/physical commons). This is a subtractable natural commons (forest biomass, land cover). The ρ Ratio track applies. Track VitalityIndex (Q33) is SKIP.
Q31.src: T1 output carry_for_later_tests.T1.1 = A1; SOC Framework Section 4, T4.2 Track A selection rule

Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction (forest diverted, enabled by CAMPA clearance pathway, FY 2024-25) ÷ Annual replenishment (new afforestation from CAMPA budget FY 2024-25). Extraction: 8,500 ha diverted FY 2024-25 (CAG/Economic Survey). Replenishment: ₹95 cr (afforestation component) ÷ ₹4.1 lakh/ha (scheme-verified cost) = 2,317 ha new planting. ρ = 8,500 ÷ 2,317 = 3.67.
Q32.interp: FAIL — ρ = 3.67 > 1.2 threshold. Annual forest diversion enabled by CAMPA clearance mechanism (8,500 ha) significantly exceeds annual compensatory afforestation planted (est. ~2,317 ha) in the same FY. METHODOLOGY NOTE: T1.5 = Yes (site mapping exists), so ρ could in principle be Measured; however, direct annual extraction/replenishment volume data is not reported separately in available documents — budget-derived estimation was used. IMPORTANT CAVEAT: CAMPA operates on a multi-year restoration cycle. The 45,000 ha cumulative restoration since 2009 (at ~2,813 ha/yr average) compared with average annual diversion unknown — if average annual diversion is ≤2,813 ha over the 16-year period, ρ(cumulative) could approach 1.0. FY 2024-25 diversion of 8,500 ha appears atypically high (large infrastructure year). Neither interpretation brings ρ below 1.2 for the reported FY 2024-25 data. RECOMMENDATION: Compute ρ using 5-year rolling average extraction and replenishment data from Forest Department annual returns for a more representative assessment.
Q32.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (8,500 ha diversion; ₹175 cr spent); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (8,500 ha diverted, ₹4.1 lakh/ha cost); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (₹95 cr afforestation component); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — land diversion 8,500 ha verified
Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 — ₹95 cr afforestation component
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — ₹4.1 lakh/ha cost parameter
Outcome Budget 2025-26 — cumulative 45,000 ha afforestation since 2009

Q34.r: CAMPA monitoring operates at multiple levels: (1) Annual government monitoring: CAG State Accounts Report annually audits plantation records, survival rates, expenditure, and compliance — constituting annual government reporting. (2) Accessible public portal: CAMPA portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/) provides a publicly accessible dashboard with current-year statistics including sapling survival tracker (72%), carbon sequestration data, species distribution, land diversion statistics, and afforestation site maps — qualifying as a 'scheme portal accessible to community.' (3) Formal field cycles: 3-year survival checks mandated by the 2023 notification. The combination of an accessible public portal with current-year data AND annual CAG audit verification meets the Score 2 criterion (semi-annual or more frequent monitoring accessible to community via portal). NOTE: Q35 = Yes (climate-stress zone) — threshold upgraded to ≥ 2. T4.3 = 2 just meets this threshold. The formal field monitoring cycle (triennial) is less frequent than ideal for a high-pressure zone; ongoing portal accessibility compensates. Specific quarterly update documentation for the portal is not cited in available data.
Q34.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — 3-year survival checks, 10-year ecosystem assessment; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — annual compliance audit; CAMPA Portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/) — publicly accessible dashboard with sapling survival tracker, land statistics

Q35.r: Chhattisgarh's forested districts where CAMPA operates face documented high deforestation pressure. The scheme itself records 8,500 ha of forest diverted in FY 2024-25 alone from mining, industrial, and infrastructure projects — a high-pressure forest stress indicator. Per ISFR (India State of Forest Report) data, Chhattisgarh (particularly Bastar, Surguja, and Raigarh divisions) is classified as high deforestation-pressure zone, with active mining belt encroachment. Additionally, several Chhattisgarh districts (Jashpur, Surguja) are in NDMA drought-prone lists. The forest resource is thus in a climate and anthropogenic stress zone, triggering the T4.7 conditional tightening: Q34 pass threshold upgrades from ≥ 1 to ≥ 2.
Q35.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (8,500 ha diverted from mining/industrial projects); ISFR (India State of Forest Report) — Chhattisgarh deforestation pressure classification; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (land diversion verified)

Q36.r: Multiple documented penalty mechanisms exist: (1) Forest Conservation Act 1980: forest diversion without legal clearance is a criminal offence. CAMPA compliance is a prerequisite for MoEFCC clearance — non-compliance blocks future clearances and triggers legal action. (2) CAMPA Act 2016: establishes the statutory framework with Fund oversight by Steering and Executive Committees; non-utilization or misutilization has defined administrative consequences. (3) CAG-verified compliance: CAG's annual compliance audit creates an accountability mechanism where deficits are reported to the state legislature, triggering administrative corrective action. (4) Sapling survival mandate: minimum 70% survival rate at 3 years; failure triggers additional planting obligations (replanting mandate) as a form of non-monetary penalty.
Q36.src: Forest Conservation Act 1980 (Section 3A penalty clause); CAMPA Act 2016 (Fund oversight and accountability provisions); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — survival minimum with replanting obligation; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: CAMPA has multiple post-scheme continuity mechanisms: (1) Statutory perpetuity: CAMPA is not a time-bound scheme but a statutory fund under the CAMPA Act 2016, designed to operate as long as forest diversions occur in India. It has no sunset clause. (2) JFMC as standing institutional vehicle: JFMC is constituted as a permanent community institution under Forest Department orders — it continues beyond individual planting cycles. (3) Multi-year maintenance obligation: The 2023 notification mandates 3-year survival monitoring and 10-year ecosystem health assessment, ensuring institutional engagement extends 10 years beyond initial planting. (4) Rolling fund mechanism: CAMPA funds are held in the state CAMPA account and allocated progressively — they do not lapse at scheme year end. These provisions collectively ensure post-planting continuity of governance.
Q37.src: CAMPA Act 2016 (statutory perpetual fund); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — 10-year ecosystem health assessment; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (rolling fund allocation); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q38.r: No community-led monitoring mechanism is documented in CAMPA's available scheme data. Monitoring is conducted exclusively by government agencies: Forest Department field teams (survival checks), CAG (annual compliance audit), and portal data managed by state government. The CAMPA portal is publicly accessible for reading but no community participation in monitoring, measurement, or data entry is described. No social audit mandate, Village Ecological Register, FRC patrol system, or participatory resource assessment is specified in the 2023 notification or any supporting document. GOVERNANCE GAP FLAG: T4.6 = 0 is the primary structural gap in CAMPA's sustainability lock. Government-only monitoring for a high-pressure forest zone (T4.7 triggered) creates a risk of delayed or suppressed disclosure of ecological deterioration. Community-led monitoring (FRC patrols, JFMC survival recording, participatory tree-count methods) would strengthen the replenishment lock.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — no community monitoring clause; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — government-led audit only; CAMPA Portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/) — government-managed, no community data entry

schemes.objectiveText: Compensatory afforestation sites across Chhattisgarh state on government and revenue land. Cumulative area: 45,000 hectares restored since 2009. Annual diversion offset: 8,500 hectares (FY 2024-25) with compensatory planting. 125 native tree species used. Includes 850 hectares of wildlife corridor habitat. Afforestation site maps available on CAMPA portal with location-specific tracking. Sites spread across forested districts of Chhattisgarh.

raw.programsMd:
## CAMPA (Compensatory Afforestation)
- BUDGET: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department) allocates ₹185 crores for CAMPA (Compensatory Afforestation Management and Planning Authority). Budget breakdown: Afforestation on diverted forest land (₹95 cr), Sapling procurement and maintenance (₹50 cr), Monitoring and protection (₹25 cr), Capacity building (₹15 cr). Scope: 45,000 hectares diverted forest land target for restoration.
- SURVEY: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25: 'CAMPA ensures environmental offset for development activities: Land diverted (FY 2024-25): 8,500 hectares (mining, industrial, infrastructure projects). Compensatory afforestation: 45,000 hectares cumulatively restored (CAMPA fund utilization since 2009). Sapling survival rate: 72% (3-year monitoring). Green cover restoration: 185 lakh trees planted. Carbon sequestration: 3.25 lakh metric tons CO2 equivalent annually (from CAMPA-afforested areas). Biodiversity: 125 native tree species used in restoration. Cost per hectare: ₹4.1 lakhs (sapling + maintenance + protection).'
- OUTCOME: Outcome Budget 2025-26 results: Cumulative afforestation: 45,000 hectares (1989 trees/hectare average). Total saplings planted: 185 lakh (since 2009). Sapling survival: 72% verified (post-3-year monitoring). Carbon sequestration: 3.25 lakh MT CO2/year. Tree species: 125 native species (diversity-focused restoration). Land restored: 45,000 hectares (ecosystem recovery achieved). Wildlife habitat: 850 hectares with enhanced wildlife corridors. Water table improvement: 25 cm rise in CAMPA-restored areas (5-year survey). Biodiversity indicators: Bird species +18%, vegetation density +35%. Budget disbursed: ₹185 cr (FY 2024-25). Employment: 12,500 person-years generated (plantation + maintenance). Local livelihood: Forest dwellers engaged in plantation + monitoring (₹3,500 per person annual).
- ANNOUNCEMENT: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN establishes CAMPA May 1, 2009 (aligned with national CAMPA guidelines). Updated state notification (2023): Specifies (1) Afforestation target: 45,000 hectares on diverted forest lands. (2) Tree species: 125 native species selection (ecosystem-appropriate). (3) Sapling survival: Minimum 70% at 3-year stage. (4) Cost: ₹4.1 lakhs/hectare (sapling + maintenance + protection). (5) Implementation: Joint Forest Management Committee + District Forest Officer coordination. (6) Monitoring: 3-year survival checks, 10-year ecosystem health assessment. (7) Employment: Forest dwellers prioritized for plantation work. (8) Carbon benefit: Official CO2 sequestration tracking. Implementation: Forest Department + Ministry of Environment Forest.
- CAG: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 audits CAMPA fund utilization. Audit findings: Land diversion: 8,500 hectares (FY 2024-25) verified (mining dept coordination). Afforestation: 45,000 hectares cumulative verified (plantation records). Saplings: 185 lakh planted confirmed (nursery records). Survival: 72% verified (3-year monitoring field survey). Carbon sequestration: 3.25 lakh MT CO2/year estimated (biomass calculation verified). Tree species: 125 species confirmed (nursery inventory). Habitat restoration: 850 hectares wildlife corridor verified (assessment reports). Water table: 25 cm rise measured (borewell survey). Employment: 12,500 person-years confirmed (wage payment records). Budget: ₹185 cr allocated, ₹175 cr spent (95%). Cost per hectare: ₹4.1 lakhs verified (project cost analysis). Compliance: 100% with national CAMPA guidelines verified.
- PORTAL: CAMPA featured on state forest portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/). Portal features: Afforestation site mapping (45,000 hectares), sapling survival tracker (72% rate), carbon sequestration calculator, species distribution dashboard, land diversion statistics (8,500 hectares FY 2024-25), employment records (12,500 person-years), habitat restoration status. Statistics: 45,000 hectares afforested, 185 lakh saplings, 72% survival, 3.25 lakh MT CO2/year, 125 species, ₹185 cr budget, 12,500 employment. Portal services: Site locator, survival status, carbon benefit calculator, species finder.

Q3.src: programs.md — ANNOUNCEMENT, CAG sections

Q4.r: The primary resource is state/public forest land (diverted forest land and compensatory afforestation sites on government/revenue land) formally notified for community-based afforestation. The scheme operates through Joint Forest Management Committees (JFMC) mandated in the 2023 notification, ensuring the resource is governed with community participation rather than purely for state revenue. The ecological outputs (watershed, biodiversity, wildlife corridor) are explicitly designed for community and ecosystem benefit, not commercial state revenue extraction.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — Implementation clause; CAMPA Forest Portal site mapping; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q5.v: Compensatory afforestation sites across Chhattisgarh state on government and revenue land. Cumulative area: 45,000 hectares restored since 2009. Annual diversion offset: 8,500 hectares (FY 2024-25) with compensatory planting. 125 native tree species used. Includes 850 hectares of wildlife corridor habitat. Afforestation site maps available on CAMPA portal with location-specific tracking. Sites spread across forested districts of Chhattisgarh.
Q5.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; CAMPA Portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/)

Q6.r: CAMPA portal shows afforestation site mapping for all 45,000 hectares with location-specific tracking. CAG audit verified plantation records with site-level data. The 2023 notification specifies afforestation targets per site. Each site is identified through Forest Department compartment maps and plantation records verified by CAG. Sufficient boundary documentation exists in public records.
Q6.src: CAMPA Portal afforestation site mapping; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (plantation records verified)

Q7.r: JFMC (Joint Forest Management Committee) involvement is mandated by the 2023 state notification under the scheme. JFMC is constituted under state Forest Department orders and has statutory recognition. Forest dwellers' right to participate and benefit from plantation work is mandated in the scheme guidelines. This constitutes a statutory/legally enforceable right to engage with and benefit from the commons resource.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — Implementation clause: 'Joint Forest Management Committee + District Forest Officer coordination'; Outcome Budget 2025-26 — employment mandate

Q8.r: The compensatory afforestation sites are government/revenue land earmarked specifically for ecological restoration. The diverted forest land (8,500 ha FY 2024-25) is a separate category from the afforestation sites. No mining lease, SEZ notification, or change-of-land-use order is active on the compensatory afforestation sites themselves. CAG audit confirmed compliance with national CAMPA guidelines with no encumbrance flags.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — Compliance: 100% with national CAMPA guidelines verified; Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN

Q9.breakdown: Afforestation on diverted forest land: ₹95 cr; Sapling procurement and maintenance: ₹50 cr; Monitoring and protection: ₹25 cr; Capacity building: ₹15 cr
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (₹175 cr spent of ₹185 cr allocated)

Q10.r: All expenditure components are on public/community forest resource restoration: afforestation on state forest/government land (₹95 cr), sapling procurement for common land planting (₹50 cr), monitoring of common forest assets (₹25 cr), and capacity building for community/JFMC engagement (₹15 cr). No expenditure flows exclusively to private assets — plantation outputs (forests, watershed, biodiversity) are public/community goods. Private benefit (wages to forest dwellers) is a labour cost within a commons-restoration activity, not an asset transfer to private property.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q11.r: No expenditure component in CAMPA creates or transfers assets to private land or exclusively private benefit. All physical outputs (planted trees, restored forests, wildlife corridors, watersheds) are on government/common land. Wages paid to forest dwellers are compensation for labour on common land, not investment in private property.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (budget breakdown); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (implementation scope)

Q12.interp: PASS — θ₁ = 1.00 ≥ 0.50. All scheme expenditure is on common/public resources (forest land restoration, ecosystem services). No private asset expenditure identified.
Q12.src: Derived from Q9 and Q10; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q14.r: CAMPA's primary outputs are ecological commons goods: carbon sequestration (3.25 lakh MT CO2/year), watershed improvement (25 cm water table rise in restored areas), biodiversity enhancement (bird species +18%, vegetation density +35%), wildlife corridor habitat (850 ha). These ecosystem services are non-excludable — they benefit the entire region without practical exclusion of any community member. The forest resource itself (afforested land) remains state/community land open to all. No fee or membership mechanism restricts access to these benefits.
Q14.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

The 10 km radius and GP/block definitions both apply.

Q19.r: Chhattisgarh's CAMPA afforestation districts (Bastar, Surguja, Bilaspur divisions) have ST population concentrations of 40–70% (Census 2011). 22a.r: JFMC (Joint Forest Management Committee) is the formally constituted community representative body. Type: statutory community forest management body constituted under Forest Department orders. Mandated in CAMPA 2023 notification: 'Implementation: Joint Forest Management Committee + District Forest Officer coordination.'

22b.r: The 2023 CAMPA state notification explicitly mandates JFMC's coordinating role in plantation implementation — not advisory. JFMC approves plantation sites, supervises labour recruitment, and coordinates with District Forest Officer. Role is integral to scheme operation, not consultative.

22c.r: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms '100% compliance with national CAMPA guidelines' — CAMPA guidelines require JFMC to hold regular coordination meetings for implementation oversight. Plantation activity continuity (45,000 ha cumulative, 12,500 person-years FY 2024-25) is operationally consistent with functional committee activity. NOTE: Specific JFMC meeting minutes (≥2 in prior 12 months with resolutions) are not individually cited in available documents; compliance is inferred from CAG 100% compliance certification and scheme operational output volumes.

Q25.r: The 2023 CAMPA notification explicitly mandates: 'Employment: Forest dwellers prioritized for plantation work.' The Outcome Budget 2025-26 records employment as a mandatory output indicator: 12,500 person-years generated (plantation + maintenance), with Forest dwellers engaged in plantation + monitoring at ₹3,500 per person annually. CAG confirmed wage payment records. This constitutes a mandatory livelihood pathway (wage employment) explicitly articulated in scheme design documents.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — Employment clause; Outcome Budget 2025-26 — employment results; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — wage payment records confirmed

observed.calc: CAG-verified wage payment records: 12,500 person-years at ₹3,500/person/year. Treating per-person annual figure as per-participating-household-year (forest dweller households engage one primary worker in the scheme). Observed and independently audited.
observed.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26: 'Local livelihood: Forest dwellers engaged in plantation + monitoring (₹3,500 per person annual)'; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25: 'Employment: 12,500 person-years confirmed (wage payment records)'

projected.calc: Not applicable — observed data governs

Q26.r: Observed ΔY of ₹3,500/person/year is available from CAG-verified wage payment records and Outcome Budget. Observed data governs per framework rule. ΔY = ₹3,500 is 16.7% above the ₹3,000 observed threshold — passes but is near-threshold (within 20%). Note: figure is per-person, not explicitly per-household; if multiple scheme workers exist per household, true HH-level ΔY would be higher.
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — wage payment records (12,500 person-years)
Outcome Budget 2025-26 — livelihood output section (₹3,500/person/year)
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — employment generation data

Q27.r: One distinct livelihood pathway is explicitly articulated in scheme documents: wage employment in afforestation activities (plantation work + monitoring/protection). Both plantation and monitoring activities are forms of wage labour within the same economic pathway. The scheme notification and Outcome Budget consistently describe this as a single wage-employment pathway for forest dwellers. No enterprise, produce-sale, or market-linked livelihood pathway is mentioned in scheme documents. Score = 1 (minimum pass).
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — 'Employment: Forest dwellers prioritized for plantation work'; Outcome Budget 2025-26 — 'Forest dwellers engaged in plantation + monitoring'

Q28.r: The CAMPA fund is a statutory institutional demand mechanism for plantation labour services. Under the CAMPA Act 2016 and Forest Conservation Act 1980, industries/developers diverting forest land are legally obligated to deposit compensatory afforestation funds into CAMPA. The Forest Department/JFMC then channels these funds as guaranteed wage payments to forest dwellers for plantation and monitoring work. This constitutes an institutional backstop (statutory government procurement of labour services) rather than market-dependent income.
Q28.src: CAMPA Act 2016; Forest Conservation Act 1980; Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (wage payment records confirmed)

Q29.r: CAMPA MIS tracks ecological/environmental metrics (sapling survival rate, carbon sequestration, biodiversity indices, water table change) at site/ecosystem level. However, no household-level non-monetary livelihood metrics are tracked: food sufficiency months, food species diversity, fodder availability (livestock feeding days), or medicinal plant access per beneficiary household are not reported in Outcome Budget, Economic Survey, or CAG findings. NEAR-THRESHOLD FLAG: Observed ΔY of ₹3,500/person/year is within 20% of the ₹3,000 threshold. With T3.5 = No, the true economic benefit to forest dweller households is potentially under-measured — non-monetary benefits from the restored forest (fodder, fuel wood, medicinal plants, NTFPs) have real economic value not captured in current MIS.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (no HH-level non-monetary metrics present); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (no such metrics audited)

pass_verification.enh_T3.5_flag: FLAG: Observed ΔY of ₹3,500 is within 20% of the ₹3,000 threshold AND T3.5 = No. Non-monetary forest benefits (fodder, fuel wood, medicinal plants, NTFPs from restored forest) are not tracked at household level. True economic benefit likely under-measured. Recommend adding HH-level non-monetary tracking in MIS.

sources_verified: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6

Q30.r: The CAMPA scheme is structurally a mandatory replenishment mechanism. The Forest Conservation Act 1980 and CAMPA Act 2016 impose a legal obligation that for every hectare of forest diverted, compensatory afforestation must be undertaken. The 2023 state notification specifies: (1) Afforestation target: 45,000 hectares; (2) Sapling survival minimum: 70% at 3-year stage (with CAG-monitored compliance); (3) 3-year survival checks and 10-year ecosystem health assessment mandated; (4) Species composition requirement: 125 native species for ecological integrity. Non-compliance has defined consequences: forest diversion clearance from MoEFCC is conditional on CAMPA fund deposit and state CAMPA compliance record. CAG confirms 100% compliance with national CAMPA guidelines.
Q30.src: CAMPA Act 2016; Forest Conservation Act 1980; Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — compliance verified

Q31.r: T1.1 = A1 (State/public forest land — natural/physical commons). This is a subtractable natural commons (forest biomass, land cover). The ρ Ratio track applies. Track VitalityIndex (Q33) is SKIP.
Q31.src: T1 output carry_for_later_tests.T1.1 = A1; SOC Framework Section 4, T4.2 Track A selection rule

Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction (forest diverted, enabled by CAMPA clearance pathway, FY 2024-25) ÷ Annual replenishment (new afforestation from CAMPA budget FY 2024-25). Extraction: 8,500 ha diverted FY 2024-25 (CAG/Economic Survey). Replenishment: ₹95 cr (afforestation component) ÷ ₹4.1 lakh/ha (scheme-verified cost) = 2,317 ha new planting. ρ = 8,500 ÷ 2,317 = 3.67.
Q32.interp: FAIL — ρ = 3.67 > 1.2 threshold. Annual forest diversion enabled by CAMPA clearance mechanism (8,500 ha) significantly exceeds annual compensatory afforestation planted (est. ~2,317 ha) in the same FY. METHODOLOGY NOTE: T1.5 = Yes (site mapping exists), so ρ could in principle be Measured; however, direct annual extraction/replenishment volume data is not reported separately in available documents — budget-derived estimation was used. IMPORTANT CAVEAT: CAMPA operates on a multi-year restoration cycle. The 45,000 ha cumulative restoration since 2009 (at ~2,813 ha/yr average) compared with average annual diversion unknown — if average annual diversion is ≤2,813 ha over the 16-year period, ρ(cumulative) could approach 1.0. FY 2024-25 diversion of 8,500 ha appears atypically high (large infrastructure year). Neither interpretation brings ρ below 1.2 for the reported FY 2024-25 data. RECOMMENDATION: Compute ρ using 5-year rolling average extraction and replenishment data from Forest Department annual returns for a more representative assessment.
Q32.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (8,500 ha diversion; ₹175 cr spent); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (8,500 ha diverted, ₹4.1 lakh/ha cost); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (₹95 cr afforestation component); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN

sources_verified: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6

Q35.r: Chhattisgarh's forested districts where CAMPA operates face documented high deforestation pressure. The scheme itself records 8,500 ha of forest diverted in FY 2024-25 alone from mining, industrial, and infrastructure projects — a high-pressure forest stress indicator. Per ISFR (India State of Forest Report) data, Chhattisgarh (particularly Bastar, Surguja, and Raigarh divisions) is classified as high deforestation-pressure zone, with active mining belt encroachment. Additionally, several Chhattisgarh districts (Jashpur, Surguja) are in NDMA drought-prone lists. The forest resource is thus in a climate and anthropogenic stress zone, triggering the T4.7 conditional tightening: Q34 pass threshold upgrades from ≥ 1 to ≥ 2.
Q35.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (8,500 ha diverted from mining/industrial projects); ISFR (India State of Forest Report) — Chhattisgarh deforestation pressure classification; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (land diversion verified)

Q34.r: CAMPA monitoring operates at multiple levels: (1) Annual government monitoring: CAG State Accounts Report annually audits plantation records, survival rates, expenditure, and compliance — constituting annual government reporting. (2) Accessible public portal: CAMPA portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/) provides a publicly accessible dashboard with current-year statistics including sapling survival tracker (72%), carbon sequestration data, species distribution, land diversion statistics, and afforestation site maps — qualifying as a 'scheme portal accessible to community.' (3) Formal field cycles: 3-year survival checks mandated by the 2023 notification. The combination of an accessible public portal with current-year data AND annual CAG audit verification meets the Score 2 criterion (semi-annual or more frequent monitoring accessible to community via portal). NOTE: Q35 = Yes (climate-stress zone) — threshold upgraded to ≥ 2. T4.3 = 2 just meets this threshold. The formal field monitoring cycle (triennial) is less frequent than ideal for a high-pressure zone; ongoing portal accessibility compensates. Specific quarterly update documentation for the portal is not cited in available data.
Q34.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — 3-year survival checks, 10-year ecosystem assessment; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — annual compliance audit; CAMPA Portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/) — publicly accessible dashboard with sapling survival tracker, land statistics

Q36.r: Multiple documented penalty mechanisms exist: (1) Forest Conservation Act 1980: forest diversion without legal clearance is a criminal offence. CAMPA compliance is a prerequisite for MoEFCC clearance — non-compliance blocks future clearances and triggers legal action. (2) CAMPA Act 2016: establishes the statutory framework with Fund oversight by Steering and Executive Committees; non-utilization or misutilization has defined administrative consequences. (3) CAG-verified compliance: CAG's annual compliance audit creates an accountability mechanism where deficits are reported to the state legislature, triggering administrative corrective action. (4) Sapling survival mandate: minimum 70% survival rate at 3 years; failure triggers additional planting obligations (replanting mandate) as a form of non-monetary penalty.
Q36.src: Forest Conservation Act 1980 (Section 3A penalty clause); CAMPA Act 2016 (Fund oversight and accountability provisions); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — survival minimum with replanting obligation; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: CAMPA has multiple post-scheme continuity mechanisms: (1) Statutory perpetuity: CAMPA is not a time-bound scheme but a statutory fund under the CAMPA Act 2016, designed to operate as long as forest diversions occur in India. It has no sunset clause. (2) JFMC as standing institutional vehicle: JFMC is constituted as a permanent community institution under Forest Department orders — it continues beyond individual planting cycles. (3) Multi-year maintenance obligation: The 2023 notification mandates 3-year survival monitoring and 10-year ecosystem health assessment, ensuring institutional engagement extends 10 years beyond initial planting. (4) Rolling fund mechanism: CAMPA funds are held in the state CAMPA account and allocated progressively — they do not lapse at scheme year end. These provisions collectively ensure post-planting continuity of governance.
Q37.src: CAMPA Act 2016 (statutory perpetual fund); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — 10-year ecosystem health assessment; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (rolling fund allocation); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q38.r: No community-led monitoring mechanism is documented in CAMPA's available scheme data. Monitoring is conducted exclusively by government agencies: Forest Department field teams (survival checks), CAG (annual compliance audit), and portal data managed by state government. The CAMPA portal is publicly accessible for reading but no community participation in monitoring, measurement, or data entry is described. No social audit mandate, Village Ecological Register, FRC patrol system, or participatory resource assessment is specified in the 2023 notification or any supporting document. GOVERNANCE GAP FLAG: T4.6 = 0 is the primary structural gap in CAMPA's sustainability lock. Government-only monitoring for a high-pressure forest zone (T4.7 triggered) creates a risk of delayed or suppressed disclosure of ecological deterioration. Community-led monitoring (FRC patrols, JFMC survival recording, participatory tree-count methods) would strengthen the replenishment lock.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — no community monitoring clause; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — government-led audit only; CAMPA Portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/) — government-managed, no community data entry

pass_verification.C1_T4.1: PASS (from T4a — mandatory replenishment rule exists under CAMPA Act 2016 and state notification)
pass_verification.C2_rho_or_VI: FAIL (from T4a — ρ = 3.67 >> 1.2 threshold; annual forest diversion 8,500 ha greatly exceeds estimated annual CAMPA afforestation ~2,317 ha in FY 2024-25)
pass_verification.C3_T4.3: PASS (T4.3 = 2; meets upgraded threshold ≥ 2 triggered by Q35 climate-stress classification; publicly accessible portal + annual CAG audit)
pass_verification.C4_T4.4_or_T4.5: PASS (Q36 = Yes AND Q37 = Yes — both penalty mechanism and post-scheme continuity are present)
pass_verification.enh_T4.6_flag: GOVERNANCE GAP FLAG: Community-led monitoring (T4.6 = 0) is absent. This is the primary structural upgrade lever. For a high-deforestation-pressure zone (T4.7 triggered), government-only monitoring is a significant risk. Recommend: mandate JFMC participation in survival recording, integrate FRC patrol reports into CAMPA MIS, and establish a community-accessible monitoring dashboard with data-entry capability.
pass_verification.overall_T4: FAIL — C2_rho_or_VI = FAIL is a hard gate. ρ > 1.2 means annual forest extraction enabled by CAMPA clearance mechanism (8,500 ha diverted) far exceeds annual CAMPA-mandated replenishment (~2,317 ha planted) in FY 2024-25. This represents an EXTRACTION-REPLENISHMENT IMBALANCE and constitutes a structural sustainability failure. CLASSIFICATION IMPLICATION: T1=PASS, T2=UNVERIFIED (contextually likely PASS), T3=PASS, T4=FAIL → Provisional Near-SOC (Structural). T4 failure requires redesign: CAMPA afforestation targets must be recalibrated to match or exceed annual diversion volumes on a rolling annual basis, not only cumulatively. Upgrade pathway: (1) Increase annual afforestation budget to match 1:1 extraction ratio (would require ~₹349 cr additional or reduction in permissible annual diversion); (2) Mandate annual ρ computation and public disclosure; (3) Activate community-led monitoring (T4.6). A 5-year rolling ρ calculation is recommended as FY 2024-25 diversion (8,500 ha) may be atypically high; however, even with cumulative averaging, available data does not demonstrate ρ ≤ 1.2.

Q4.r: The primary resource is state/public forest land (diverted forest land and compensatory afforestation sites on government/revenue land) formally notified for community-based afforestation. The scheme operates through Joint Forest Management Committees (JFMC) mandated in the 2023 notification, ensuring the resource is governed with community participation rather than purely for state revenue. The ecological outputs (watershed, biodiversity, wildlife corridor) are explicitly designed for community and ecosystem benefit, not commercial state revenue extraction.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — Implementation clause; CAMPA Forest Portal site mapping; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q5.v: Compensatory afforestation sites across Chhattisgarh state on government and revenue land. Cumulative area: 45,000 hectares restored since 2009. Annual diversion offset: 8,500 hectares (FY 2024-25) with compensatory planting. 125 native tree species used. Includes 850 hectares of wildlife corridor habitat. Afforestation site maps available on CAMPA portal with location-specific tracking. Sites spread across forested districts of Chhattisgarh.

Sufficient boundary documentation exists in public records.

Q7.r: JFMC (Joint Forest Management Committee) involvement is mandated by the 2023 state notification under the scheme. JFMC is constituted under state Forest Department orders and has statutory recognition. Forest dwellers' right to participate and benefit from plantation work is mandated in the scheme guidelines. This constitutes a statutory/legally enforceable right to engage with and benefit from the commons resource.

Q8.r: The compensatory afforestation sites are government/revenue land earmarked specifically for ecological restoration. The diverted forest land (8,500 ha FY 2024-25) is a separate category from the afforestation sites. No mining lease, SEZ notification, or change-of-land-use order is active on the compensatory afforestation sites themselves. CAG audit confirmed compliance with national CAMPA guidelines with no encumbrance flags.

Q10.r: All expenditure components are on public/community forest resource restoration: afforestation on state forest/government land (₹95 cr), sapling procurement for common land planting (₹50 cr), monitoring of common forest assets (₹25 cr), and capacity building for community/JFMC engagement (₹15 cr). No expenditure flows exclusively to private assets — plantation outputs (forests, watershed, biodiversity) are public/community goods. Private benefit (wages to forest dwellers) is a labour cost within a commons-restoration activity, not an asset transfer to private property.

Q11.r: No expenditure component in CAMPA creates or transfers assets to private land or exclusively private benefit. All physical outputs (planted trees, restored forests, wildlife corridors, watersheds) are on government/common land. Wages paid to forest dwellers are compensation for labour on common land, not investment in private property.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (budget breakdown); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (implementation scope)

Q12.interp: PASS — θ₁ = 1.00 ≥ 0.50. All scheme expenditure is on common/public resources (forest land restoration, ecosystem services). No private asset expenditure identified.

These ecosystem services are non-excludable — they benefit the entire region without practical exclusion of any community member. The forest resource itself (afforested land) remains state/community land open to all. No fee or membership mechanism restricts access to these benefits.

22a.r: JFMC (Joint Forest Management Committee) is the formally constituted community representative body. Type: statutory community forest management body constituted under Forest Department orders. Mandated in CAMPA 2023 notification: 'Implementation: Joint Forest Management Committee + District Forest Officer coordination.'

22b.r: The 2023 CAMPA state notification explicitly mandates JFMC's coordinating role in plantation implementation — not advisory. JFMC approves plantation sites, supervises labour recruitment, and coordinates with District Forest Officer. Role is integral to scheme operation, not consultative.

22c.r: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms '100% compliance with national CAMPA guidelines' — CAMPA guidelines require JFMC to hold regular coordination meetings for implementation oversight. Plantation activity continuity (45,000 ha cumulative, 12,500 person-years FY 2024-25) is operationally consistent with functional committee activity. NOTE: Specific JFMC meeting minutes (≥2 in prior 12 months with resolutions) are not individually cited in available documents; compliance is inferred from CAG 100% compliance certification and scheme operational output volumes.

Q25.r: The 2023 CAMPA notification explicitly mandates: 'Employment: Forest dwellers prioritized for plantation work.' The Outcome Budget 2025-26 records employment as a mandatory output indicator: 12,500 person-years generated (plantation + maintenance), with Forest dwellers engaged in plantation + monitoring at ₹3,500 per person annually. CAG confirmed wage payment records. This constitutes a mandatory livelihood pathway (wage employment) explicitly articulated in scheme design documents.

Q27.r: One distinct livelihood pathway is explicitly articulated in scheme documents: wage employment in afforestation activities (plantation work + monitoring/protection). Both plantation and monitoring activities are forms of wage labour within the same economic pathway. The scheme notification and Outcome Budget consistently describe this as a single wage-employment pathway for forest dwellers. No enterprise, produce-sale, or market-linked livelihood pathway is mentioned in scheme documents. Score = 1 (minimum pass).
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — 'Employment: Forest dwellers prioritized for plantation work'; Outcome Budget 2025-26 — 'Forest dwellers engaged in plantation + monitoring'

Q28.r: The CAMPA fund is a statutory institutional demand mechanism for plantation labour services. Under the CAMPA Act 2016 and Forest Conservation Act 1980, industries/developers diverting forest land are legally obligated to deposit compensatory afforestation funds into CAMPA. The Forest Department/JFMC then channels these funds as guaranteed wage payments to forest dwellers for plantation and monitoring work. This constitutes an institutional backstop (statutory government procurement of labour services) rather than market-dependent income.
Q28.src: CAMPA Act 2016; Forest Conservation Act 1980; Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (wage payment records confirmed)

Q29.r: CAMPA MIS tracks ecological/environmental metrics (sapling survival rate, carbon sequestration, biodiversity indices, water table change) at site/ecosystem level. However, no household-level non-monetary livelihood metrics are tracked: food sufficiency months, food species diversity, fodder availability (livestock feeding days), or medicinal plant access per beneficiary household are not reported in Outcome Budget, Economic Survey, or CAG findings. NEAR-THRESHOLD FLAG: Observed ΔY of ₹3,500/person/year is within 20% of the ₹3,000 threshold. With T3.5 = No, the true economic benefit to forest dweller households is potentially under-measured — non-monetary benefits from the restored forest (fodder, fuel wood, medicinal plants, NTFPs) have real economic value not captured in current MIS.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (no HH-level non-monetary metrics present); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (no such metrics audited)

Q30.r: The CAMPA scheme is structurally a mandatory replenishment mechanism. The Forest Conservation Act 1980 and CAMPA Act 2016 impose a legal obligation that for every hectare of forest diverted, compensatory afforestation must be undertaken. The 2023 state notification specifies: (1) Afforestation target: 45,000 hectares; (2) Sapling survival minimum: 70% at 3-year stage (with CAG-monitored compliance); (3) 3-year survival checks and 10-year ecosystem health assessment mandated; (4) Species composition requirement: 125 native species for ecological integrity. Non-compliance has defined consequences: forest diversion clearance from MoEFCC is conditional on CAMPA fund deposit and state CAMPA compliance record. CAG confirms 100% compliance with national CAMPA guidelines.
Q30.src: CAMPA Act 2016; Forest Conservation Act 1980; Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — compliance verified
