Q34.r: CAMPA monitoring operates at multiple levels: (1) Annual government monitoring: CAG State Accounts Report annually audits plantation records, survival rates, expenditure, and compliance — constituting annual government reporting. (2) Accessible public portal: CAMPA portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/) provides a publicly accessible dashboard with current-year statistics including sapling survival tracker (72%), carbon sequestration data, species distribution, land diversion statistics, and afforestation site maps — qualifying as a 'scheme portal accessible to community.' (3) Formal field cycles: 3-year survival checks mandated by the 2023 notification. The combination of an accessible public portal with current-year data AND annual CAG audit verification meets the Score 2 criterion (semi-annual or more frequent monitoring accessible to community via portal). NOTE: Q35 = Yes (climate-stress zone) — threshold upgraded to ≥ 2. T4.3 = 2 just meets this threshold. The formal field monitoring cycle (triennial) is less frequent than ideal for a high-pressure zone; ongoing portal accessibility compensates. Specific quarterly update documentation for the portal is not cited in available data.
Q34.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — 3-year survival checks, 10-year ecosystem assessment; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — annual compliance audit; CAMPA Portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/) — publicly accessible dashboard with sapling survival tracker, land statistics

Q36.r: Multiple documented penalty mechanisms exist: (1) Forest Conservation Act 1980: forest diversion without legal clearance is a criminal offence. CAMPA compliance is a prerequisite for MoEFCC clearance — non-compliance blocks future clearances and triggers legal action. (2) CAMPA Act 2016: establishes the statutory framework with Fund oversight by Steering and Executive Committees; non-utilization or misutilization has defined administrative consequences. (3) CAG-verified compliance: CAG's annual compliance audit creates an accountability mechanism where deficits are reported to the state legislature, triggering administrative corrective action. (4) Sapling survival mandate: minimum 70% survival rate at 3 years; failure triggers additional planting obligations (replanting mandate) as a form of non-monetary penalty.
Q36.src: Forest Conservation Act 1980 (Section 3A penalty clause); CAMPA Act 2016 (Fund oversight and accountability provisions); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — survival minimum with replanting obligation; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: CAMPA has multiple post-scheme continuity mechanisms: (1) Statutory perpetuity: CAMPA is not a time-bound scheme but a statutory fund under the CAMPA Act 2016, designed to operate as long as forest diversions occur in India. It has no sunset clause. (2) JFMC as standing institutional vehicle: JFMC is constituted as a permanent community institution under Forest Department orders — it continues beyond individual planting cycles. (3) Multi-year maintenance obligation: The 2023 notification mandates 3-year survival monitoring and 10-year ecosystem health assessment, ensuring institutional engagement extends 10 years beyond initial planting. (4) Rolling fund mechanism: CAMPA funds are held in the state CAMPA account and allocated progressively — they do not lapse at scheme year end. These provisions collectively ensure post-planting continuity of governance.
Q37.src: CAMPA Act 2016 (statutory perpetual fund); Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — 10-year ecosystem health assessment; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (rolling fund allocation); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q38.r: No community-led monitoring mechanism is documented in CAMPA's available scheme data. Monitoring is conducted exclusively by government agencies: Forest Department field teams (survival checks), CAG (annual compliance audit), and portal data managed by state government. The CAMPA portal is publicly accessible for reading but no community participation in monitoring, measurement, or data entry is described. No social audit mandate, Village Ecological Register, FRC patrol system, or participatory resource assessment is specified in the 2023 notification or any supporting document. GOVERNANCE GAP FLAG: T4.6 = 0 is the primary structural gap in CAMPA's sustainability lock. Government-only monitoring for a high-pressure forest zone (T4.7 triggered) creates a risk of delayed or suppressed disclosure of ecological deterioration. Community-led monitoring (FRC patrols, JFMC survival recording, participatory tree-count methods) would strengthen the replenishment lock.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 6-CAMPA/2009/CGN (2023 update) — no community monitoring clause; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — government-led audit only; CAMPA Portal (https://forest.cg.gov.in/campa/) — government-managed, no community data entry

upgradePathway: Add enforceable replenishment and monitoring mechanisms to lock sustainability.

Q1.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN, July 1 2015 (updated 2024)

Q2.v: Forest Department, Government of Chhattisgarh; implementation through District Forest Officers
Q2.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6

Q3.v: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (establishing notification and 2024 update); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6, Forest Department); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25
Q3.src: programs.md — BUDGET, SURVEY, OUTCOME, ANNOUNCEMENT, CAG, PORTAL entries

Q4.r: The scheme explicitly and exclusively targets private landholdings. Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN specifies 'Target: 3.5 lakh trees/year on private land.' Eligibility requires min 0.5 ha (fruit) / 2 ha (timber) private landholding. Economic Survey 2024-25 confirms 'tree plantations on private land.' CAG 2024-25 confirms 42,000 registered farmers with private holdings. All plantation activity, incentive payments, and sapling distribution flow to and remain on private property. This is definitively Type C — private land. AUTOMATIC TEST 1 FAIL triggered.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q5.v: The primary resource is private agricultural/forestry land held by 42,000 registered farmers across Chhattisgarh state. Approximate coverage: 22,500 hectares (calculated at 1,867 trees/hectare, cumulative since 2015). Minimum holding eligibility: 0.5 hectare (fruit tree plantation) or 2 hectare (timber plantation). Species planted: 185 approved varieties (fruit, timber, fodder). No fixed geographic boundary — scheme is state-wide, operating across districts. Individual parcels are private property with no commons demarcation.
Q5.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q6.r: No commons resource exists to demarcate. The scheme operates on private land parcels. Individual farmer parcels are registered in the scheme portal (42,000 enrolled), but this constitutes private land records, not a public commons boundary document. No GP resolution, watershed boundary map, survey number for a commons resource, or equivalent public commons demarcation document exists or is applicable.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; State Forest Portal

Q7.r: Farmers hold statutory freehold property rights to their private land under applicable state revenue law and registration records. Score = 2 (statutory/legally enforceable right). NOTE: This is moot — T1.1 = C (private land) triggers automatic T1 FAIL regardless of T1.2 score. The T1.2 pass/fail assessment is inapplicable to private land under the SOC framework, which requires community access to a commons. Recorded for completeness.
Q7.src: State revenue law (private land title); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (eligibility for landholders)

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition, or equivalent override process affecting the scheme's operational land exists. The land is private property; no evidence of active legal encumbrance found in available records. CAG 2024-25 confirms continued scheme operation without disruption. However, this pass is moot given T1.1 = C automatic fail.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN

Q9.breakdown: Tree sapling provision: ₹45 cr; Plantation incentive (₹1,000/tree × 3.5 lakh trees): ₹35 cr; Monitoring: ₹15 cr
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department)

Q10.r: All scheme expenditure flows to private land and private actors. (1) Sapling provision (₹45 cr): saplings are sourced from certified nurseries and distributed to private farmers for planting on private land — outputs flow exclusively to private assets. (2) Plantation incentive (₹35 cr): direct cash transfer to individual private landholders at ₹1,000/tree. (3) Monitoring (₹15 cr): administrative overhead for tracking survival on private land — no commons benefit created. No expenditure component creates or maintains a commons resource accessible to the broader community. Per the framework, expenditure whose outputs flow exclusively to private actors is excluded from the numerator even if physically located near public land.
Q10.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q11.r: Total scheme expenditure of ₹95 cr (FY 2025-26) constitutes private-benefit spend: ₹45 cr saplings distributed to private farmers for private land planting; ₹35 cr cash incentive to private landholders; ₹15 cr monitoring of private plantations. Q10 = 0, Q11 = 95, sum = 95 = Q9. Rationale for Q11 = 100% of Q9: scheme design is explicitly a private land greening incentive with no commons component.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN

Q12.interp: FAIL — θ₁ = 0.00, well below the 0.50 threshold. Entire scheme expenditure benefits private land assets.
Q12.src: Derived from Q9 and Q10 above

docs: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (July 1 2015, updated 2024) — scheme guidelines, eligibility, incentive structure
Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department) — ₹95 cr budget breakdown
CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — ₹420 cr cumulative disbursed, 42,000 farmers, expenditure verification
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — program performance data
Outcome Budget 2025-26 — verified targets and achievements

Q14.r: Benefits are fully excludable and accrue only to the individual private landholder. The tree (physical asset), the cash incentive (₹1,000/tree), and the maintenance allowance (₹500/tree/year) all accrue exclusively to the registered farmer on whose private land the plantation occurs. No other community member can access these benefits. While CO2 sequestration is a diffuse environmental benefit, it is not the scheme's primary resource benefit and is not operationalised as a community livelihood or access right. Score = 0 (fully excludable). Automatic T1 FAIL additionally triggered by this sub-parameter.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (eligibility: individual landholders); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (individual farmer incentive payments)

Q15.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (42,000 farmers registered confirmed, enrollment database); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (42,000 farmers); Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q16.src: All 42,000 registered beneficiaries are private landholders residing in Chhattisgarh who plant on their own land. Scheme eligibility requires physical land ownership (min 0.5 ha fruit / 2 ha timber) — landowners are by definition resident in or proximate to their private holdings. No non-resident/distant actors are eligible under scheme guidelines. Source: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (eligibility criteria); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (enrollment database)

Q17.v: Settled_communities — within district/GP of their private landholding across Chhattisgarh state
Q17.r: The scheme has no bounded commons area to define 'local' against. Since T1.1 = C (private land), the applicable definition is settled communities: each beneficiary farmer resides within the GP/district where their private landholding is situated. All 42,000 enrolled farmers are Chhattisgarh residents holding land within the state. This is an inherent consequence of the scheme's private-land model: the landholder is co-located with the plantation site. Definition applied: Settled_communities (GP/district of private land holding).
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (private land eligibility); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.00, well above 0.70 threshold. All registered beneficiaries are Chhattisgarh-resident private landholders. NOTE: This pass is structurally trivial for a private-land scheme — the locality condition is automatically met by scheme eligibility design, not by commons-area proximity.
Q18.src: Derived from Q15 and Q16; Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q19.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No SC/ST/women-headed/landless household breakdown of the 42,000 enrolled farmers is provided in any available source (programs.md, Outcome Budget, Economic Survey, CAG Report, or Portal data). The scheme eligibility requires minimum landholding (0.5–2 ha), which structurally excludes landless households — one of the key marginalised groups under the SOC framework definition.>>
Q19.r: Available sources confirm total enrollment (42,000 farmers) and physical outcomes but do not disaggregate beneficiary caste/category or household type. Chhattisgarh state demographics indicate ~31% ST and ~13% SC population share, but this cannot be applied directly to scheme beneficiaries without scheme-specific disaggregated data. The minimum landholding requirement (0.5 ha fruit / 2 ha timber) excludes landless households from eligibility, which structurally suppresses marginalised inclusion for that sub-group.
Q19.src: <<UNVERIFIED>>

Q20.v: <<UNVERIFIED: θ₃ cannot be computed — Q19 (marginalised beneficiary count) is unverified. No disaggregated beneficiary data available.>>
Q20.calc: θ₃ = Q19 ÷ Q15 = <<UNVERIFIED>> ÷ 42,000 = <<UNVERIFIED>>
Q20.interp: UNVERIFIED — cannot assess PASS/FAIL without marginalised beneficiary count

22a.r: No formally constituted community representative body (Gram Sabha, User Group, JFM Committee, Village Water Committee, SHG federation, BMC, or equivalent) exists in the scheme's governance structure. Implementation is through District Forest Officers and a state-run online portal. The scheme is administered as an individual farmer-level transaction: farmer registers online, books saplings, plants, and receives incentive via direct bank transfer. No collective community body is constituted or even mentioned in the official notification or any available scheme document.

22b.r: No community body's role is mandated in scheme guidelines, since no such body is constituted. Implementation mandate rests with District Forest Officers and the state Forest Department.

22c.r: No community body exists to be functional. No meeting records, resolutions, or community governance evidence are present or applicable.

Q22.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no mention of community governance body); State Forest Portal (farmer self-service model); Outcome Budget 2025-26 (implementation via DFO)

Q23.r: No community governance body exists (see Q22). There is therefore no governance body to assess women's representation in. Women-headed households may be beneficiaries (some of the 42,000 farmers could be women landholders) but no disaggregated data on women's participation is available, and no governance structure exists where representation can be assessed. Governance gap flagged.
Q23.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; <<UNVERIFIED for women beneficiary share>>

Q24.r: No formal grievance or voice mechanism for non-beneficiaries is mandated in the scheme design. The state forest portal provides a farmer registration and incentive tracker service, but this is a beneficiary-facing administrative tool, not a grievance mechanism for those excluded from benefits. No social audit provision, Ombudsman reference, MIS grievance portal, or Gram Sabha complaint process is specified in the scheme guidelines (Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN) or referenced in any available evaluation document. Governance gap flagged.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; State Forest Portal; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q25.r: The scheme's official notification (No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN, updated 2024) explicitly mandates income output indicators: (1) Cash incentive of ₹1,000/tree as a mandatory income transfer to farmers, (2) Maintenance allowance of ₹500/tree/year for 3 years as a mandated livelihood support payment, (3) Employment generation in nursery and distribution (8,500 person-years) explicitly stated as a scheme output in the Outcome Budget 2025-26. The scheme articulates a direct cash-income pathway from the plantation activity to individual farmer households. T3.1 = 1. PASS.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (updated 2024); Outcome Budget 2025-26

observed.calc: Average 83 trees/HH (CAG-confirmed enrollment: 42,000 farmers / 42 lakh trees cumulative) × ₹1,000 incentive/tree = ₹83,000 cash incentive in planting year. Plus ₹41,500/year maintenance allowance (83 trees × ₹500/tree/year) for 3 years. Year-1 ΔY = ₹83,000 + ₹41,500 = ₹124,500; Years 2–3 ΔY = ₹41,500/year each. 3-year average ΔY = (₹124,500 + ₹41,500 + ₹41,500) ÷ 3 = ₹69,167/HH/year. Incentive payment records verified by CAG (₹420 cr total disbursed, 42,000 farmers, payment records audited).
observed.interp: PASS — ₹69,167/HH/year (3-year average) well above ₹3,000 observed threshold; ₹124,500 even in conservative year-1-only calculation exceeds threshold by 40×
observed.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (payment records audited); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (rate schedule); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (avg 83 trees/HH)

projected.calc: Observed data governs; projected modelling not separately published in DPR

Q26.r: Observed ΔY governs. CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms payment records for ₹420 cr cumulative disbursed (incentive portion) to 42,000 farmers. Observed ΔY (3-year avg) ≈ ₹69,167/HH/year, far exceeding the ₹3,000/HH/year observed threshold. Planting-year ΔY = ₹124,500/HH. No separate DPR projection published; CAG-verified actuals are sufficient.
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — ₹420 cr cumulative incentive payment verification
Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (2024 update) — ₹1,000/tree incentive + ₹500/tree/year maintenance rate schedule
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — average 83 trees/HH, ₹420 cr total incentive disbursed

Q27.r: Two distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) CASH INCENTIVE PATHWAY — Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN mandates ₹1,000/tree cash transfer at planting plus ₹500/tree/year maintenance allowance for 3 years, providing direct farm income to private landholders (83 trees avg → ₹83,000 planting + ₹41,500/year maintenance). (2) NURSERY WAGE EMPLOYMENT PATHWAY — Outcome Budget 2025-26 explicitly records 8,500 person-years of employment in sapling nursery establishment and distribution as a scheme output, constituting a distinct wage income stream for nursery workers and distributors. Note: Long-term timber/fruit income from 185 approved species is a natural consequence but is NOT explicitly mandated or tracked as a livelihood output indicator in scheme documents — excluded from count per counting rule.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q28.r: The Government of Chhattisgarh (Forest Department) functions as the institutional buyer/offtake mechanism for tree plantation services. The scheme guarantees ₹1,000/tree upon verified planting + ₹500/tree/year maintenance upon survival confirmation (78% survival verified). This is analogous to the MGNREGS wage payment model — government institutional demand creates a guaranteed offtake for the activity (plantation service + survival maintenance). Budget commitment: ₹35 cr/year plantation incentive (Demand for Grants No. 6, FY 2025-26). CAG-audited: ₹420 cr paid cumulatively, confirming the institutional demand is real and sustained over 10 years. Saplings are sourced from certified government nurseries (quality-assured procurement channel). This constitutes a state government procurement scheme for farmer plantation services.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q29.r: The scheme MIS and evaluation framework track: (1) sapling survival rate (78% at 3-year monitoring) — an operational metric, not a livelihood indicator; (2) species diversity (185 varieties) — a biodiversity metric, not a beneficiary livelihood metric; (3) CO2 sequestration potential (185 lakh tons projected) — an environmental metric, not a household livelihood metric. None of the tracked metrics qualify as non-monetary livelihood indicators per the framework definition (food sufficiency months, livestock feeding days, medicinal plant access, food species count accessed by households). NOTE: ΔY (₹69,167/HH/year avg) is approximately 23× the ₹3,000 threshold — no near-threshold concern. T3.5 absence does not raise potential under-measurement risk at this income level.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; State Forest Portal

Q30.r: The scheme contains no legally-binding or scheme-guideline-mandated obligation to replenish, restore, maintain, or sustain a commons resource. The 3-year survival monitoring requirement (78% target) and the maintenance allowance (₹500/tree/year × 3 years) are incentive mechanisms applied to private tree survival on private land — they are not replenishment obligations for a commons resource. The scheme's cumulative plantation target (42 lakh trees by 2026) is a state green-cover output goal, not a commons regeneration mandate. Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN specifies no afforestation ratio on commons, no recharge structure mandate, no rotational grazing protocol, no seed-bank renewal obligation, no knowledge-transmission component — none of the qualifying replenishment/continuity rules per the SOC framework apply. No consequence mechanism for non-compliance with a replenishment obligation exists because no replenishment obligation exists. AUTOMATIC T4 FAIL triggered.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (2024 update — no commons replenishment clause found); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q31.r: T1.1 (carried from t1_output.json) = 'C' (private land). Track selection rule: T1.1 ≠ E → track = RhoRatio. Q32 (ρ Ratio) is answered; Q33 (Vitality Index) is SKIP.

Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction enabled by scheme ÷ Annual replenishment mandated by scheme. Extraction: The scheme mandates tree planting on private land with no commons harvest component; no extraction from commons is enabled or tracked. Extraction from scheme activity = 0 units/year. Replenishment: Plantation survival = 3.5 lakh trees planted × 78% survival rate = 2.73 lakh trees surviving per year (Outcome Budget 2025-26). ρ = 0 ÷ 2.73 lakh = 0.00. NOTE: Concept is structurally inapplicable — there is no commons resource from which extraction occurs. The ρ ratio is computed using the only available data (plantation survival as a proxy for private-land green cover replenishment), yielding ρ = 0.00 technically within PASS threshold (≤1.2). This calculation is analytically vacuous: a ρ of 0 does not reflect a sustainable commons — it reflects the absence of any commons resource to extract from. Marked Estimated per T1.5 = No.
Q32.interp: PASS — ρ = 0.00 ≤ 1.2 (technical/formal PASS). However, this result is structurally meaningless: the scheme operates on private land with no commons resource extraction. C1_T4.1 = FAIL overrides this result — T4a partial PASS requires both C1_T4.1 = PASS AND ρ ≤ 1.2. Since C1_T4.1 = FAIL (Q30 = No), T4a partial result is FAIL regardless of ρ.
Q32.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (3.5 lakh trees planted, 78% survival); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no commons harvest mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25
docs: Outcome Budget 2025-26 — trees planted (3.5 lakh) and survival rate (78%) data
Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN — no commons extraction clause
CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — plantation records verification

Q33.calc: SKIP — track = RhoRatio (T1.1 = C ≠ E); Vitality Index does not apply

Q34.r: Q35 = No → threshold is Q34 ≥ 1 (standard; no climate-stress upgrade). The scheme mandates 3-year survival monitoring for each plantation batch: trees are tracked at planting, year-1, year-2, and year-3 intervals (78% survival target). CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms annual government audits of survival records and payment data. The State Forest Portal publishes aggregate statistics annually. This constitutes annual reporting by a government agency (score = 1). There is no quarterly or semi-annual community-accessible disclosure, no real-time dashboard, and no GP-noticeboard disclosure. Score = 1. With Q35 = No, threshold = ≥ 1. Formal PASS. NOTE: This monitoring applies to private tree survival on private land — not to a commons resource health metric. The concept of T4.3 (commons resource monitoring) is inapplicable here since there is no commons; the formal score reflects government administrative tracking of a private scheme output.
Q34.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (3-year survival monitoring mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (annual audit of survival records); State Forest Portal (annual statistics)

Q35.r: The scheme's primary resource is private land across Chhattisgarh state (T1.1 = C). There is no demarcated commons resource to classify under a notified climate or ecological stress zone. The scheme operates state-wide and is not targeted at or restricted to any CGWB over-exploited block, NDMA drought-prone block, ISFR high fire-risk zone, or NBA threatened-species habitat. While parts of Chhattisgarh include drought-prone and ecologically stressed areas, no scheme guideline or official notification classifies the scheme's resource (private plantation land) as lying within a notified stress zone. The Q35 stress-override does not apply. Q34 threshold remains at ≥ 1 (standard).
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no stress-zone targeting); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (state-wide coverage, not stress-zone-specific)

Q36.r: No penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of a replenishment or commons-use rule exists. As established in Q30 (T4.1 = No), the scheme contains no replenishment obligation for a commons resource — therefore there is no rule whose violation can be sanctioned. The maintenance allowance (₹500/tree/year) is conditional on tree survival: if trees die, the maintenance payment is withheld. However, this is an incentive conditional on private asset survival, not a penalty for commons depletion. No fine, scheme-benefit withdrawal, legal action under FRA/PESA/EPA/BDA, or GP-imposed sanction for any replenishment violation is specified in the official notification or any available scheme document. Q36 = No.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no penalty clause for commons replenishment violation found); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: No post-scheme continuity mechanism for commons governance exists. After the 3-year maintenance period concludes, planted trees become permanent private assets of the landholder — no commons governance handover is required or provided. The scheme design contains no O&M fund, no institutional handover plan to a GP/BMC/FRC/community cooperative, no legal vesting of resources in a community body, and no continuity clause in the official guidelines. The scheme's exit design is implicit: trees are planted, incentive paid over 3 years, and thereafter the private landholder manages the private asset independently. Since no commons resource was created or managed, no commons continuity mechanism is applicable or designed. Q37 = No.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no continuity clause found); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q38.r: Monitoring is conducted exclusively by government agencies with no community participation in measurement or disclosure. Survival tracking is performed by District Forest Officers. Annual audits are conducted by CAG. The State Forest Portal publishes departmental statistics. No GP social audit, no Forest Rights Committee patrol, no BMC register upkeep, no participatory resource mapping, and no community monitoring committee is mentioned in any scheme document or evaluation report. Q38 = No. Governance gap flagged.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; State Forest Portal

schemes.objectiveText: The primary resource is private agricultural/forestry land held by 42,000 registered farmers across Chhattisgarh state. Species planted: 185 approved varieties (fruit, timber, fodder). No fixed geographic boundary — scheme is state-wide, operating across districts. Individual parcels are private property with no commons demarcation.

raw.programsMd:
## Chief Minister Plantation Scheme
- BUDGET: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department) allocates ₹95 crores for Chief Minister Plantation Scheme (CM tree-planting incentive program). Budget breakdown: Tree sapling provision (₹45 cr), Plantation incentive (₹35 cr - ₹1,000 per tree for 3.5 lakh trees), Monitoring (₹15 cr). Coverage: 3.5 lakh trees target FY 2025-26, cumulative 42 lakh trees planted since 2015.
- SURVEY: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25: 'Chief Minister Plantation Scheme encourages private landholding tree plantation: Tree plantations (FY 2024-25): 3.5 lakh trees planted on private land. Cumulative since 2015: 42 lakh trees (22,500 hectares equivalent at 1,867 trees/hectare). Participant landholders: 42,000 farmers (average 83 trees per household). Incentive: ₹1,000 per tree (cash transfer to farmer). Sapling survival: 78% at 3-year monitoring. Tree species diversity: 185 varieties (fruit, timber, fodder trees). Annual maintenance allowance: ₹500 per tree per year (3 years). Total incentive disbursed: ₹420 crores cumulative. Green cover impact: 185 lakh lakh tons CO2 equivalent potential sequestration (at maturity). Land use change: ₹35 crores private forestry asset creation. Employment: Sapling nursery + distribution (8,500 person-years).'
- OUTCOME: Outcome Budget 2025-26 results: Trees planted (FY 2024-25): 3.5 lakh. Cumulative plantations: 42 lakh trees (since 2015). Land area: 22,500 hectares (at 1,867 trees/hectare). Farmers participating: 42,000 (avg 83 trees/farmer). Sapling survival: 78% verified (3-year monitoring). Species diversity: 185 varieties (fruit, timber, fodder). Annual maintenance allowance: ₹500 per tree × 3 years (provided). Cash incentive: ₹1,000 per tree distributed. Total incentive: ₹420 cr cumulative. CO2 sequestration potential: 185 lakh tons (at tree maturity, 30-year cycle). Private forestry asset: ₹35 cr (farmer land value enhancement). Employment: 8,500 person-years (nursery + distribution). Survival achievement: 78% (above 70% minimum target). Budget disbursed: ₹95 cr (FY 2024-25).
- ANNOUNCEMENT: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN establishes Chief Minister Plantation Scheme July 1, 2015 (flagship greening initiative). Updated notification (2024): Specifies (1) Target: 3.5 lakh trees/year on private land. (2) Incentive: ₹1,000 per tree (direct cash to farmer). (3) Species: 185 approved varieties (fruit, timber, fodder). (4) Maintenance: ₹500/tree annually for 3 years. (5) Eligibility: Land holders with min 0.5 hectare (fruit) / 2 hectare (timber). (6) Monitoring: 3-year survival tracking (78% target). (7) Procurement: Certified nursery saplings (quality assurance). (8) Cumulative target: 42 lakh trees by 2026 (on-track). Implementation: District Forest Officer + Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima (intersectoral).
- CAG: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 audits CM Plantation Scheme. Audit findings: Trees planted: 3.5 lakh (FY 2024-25) verified (sapling distribution records). Cumulative: 42 lakh verified (annual reports 2015-2025). Farmers: 42,000 registered confirmed (enrollment database). Sapling survival: 78% verified (3-year monitoring field survey). Species diversity: 185 varieties confirmed (approved list, nursery inventory). Incentive: ₹1,000 per tree verified (payment records). Maintenance allowance: ₹500/tree/year verified (beneficiary payment). Total disbursed: ₹420 cr calculated (incentive payments audited). CO2 potential: 185 lakh tons estimated (biomass projection validated). Private forestry asset: ₹35 cr value assessed (land enhancement). Employment: 8,500 person-years (nursery + distribution work records). Budget: ₹95 cr allocated, ₹90 cr spent (95%). Compliance: 95% farmer retention rate (follow-up tracking).
- PORTAL: Chief Minister Plantation Scheme featured on state forest portal. Portal features: Farmer registration (42,000 enrolled), sapling booking (species selection), incentive tracker (payment status), tree monitoring (3-year survival check), carbon calculator (CO2 sequestration potential), species selector (185 varieties), incentive payment status. Statistics: 3.5 lakh trees FY 2024-25, 42 lakh cumulative, 42,000 farmers, 22,500 hectares, 78% survival, 185 species, ₹1,000 incentive, ₹95 cr budget. Portal services: Farmer enrollment, species booking, incentive status, tree monitoring, CO2 calculation.

Q3.src: programs.md — BUDGET, SURVEY, OUTCOME, ANNOUNCEMENT, CAG, PORTAL entries

Q4.r: The scheme explicitly and exclusively targets private landholdings. Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN specifies 'Target: 3.5 lakh trees/year on private land.' Eligibility requires min 0.5 ha (fruit) / 2 ha (timber) private landholding. Economic Survey 2024-25 confirms 'tree plantations on private land.' CAG 2024-25 confirms 42,000 registered farmers with private holdings. All plantation activity, incentive payments, and sapling distribution flow to and remain on private property. This is definitively Type C — private land. AUTOMATIC TEST 1 FAIL triggered.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q5.v: The primary resource is private agricultural/forestry land held by 42,000 registered farmers across Chhattisgarh state. Approximate coverage: 22,500 hectares (calculated at 1,867 trees/hectare, cumulative since 2015). Minimum holding eligibility: 0.5 hectare (fruit tree plantation) or 2 hectare (timber plantation). Species planted: 185 approved varieties (fruit, timber, fodder). No fixed geographic boundary — scheme is state-wide, operating across districts. Individual parcels are private property with no commons demarcation.
Q5.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q6.r: No commons resource exists to demarcate. The scheme operates on private land parcels. Individual farmer parcels are registered in the scheme portal (42,000 enrolled), but this constitutes private land records, not a public commons boundary document. No GP resolution, watershed boundary map, survey number for a commons resource, or equivalent public commons demarcation document exists or is applicable.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; State Forest Portal

Q7.r: Farmers hold statutory freehold property rights to their private land under applicable state revenue law and registration records. Score = 2 (statutory/legally enforceable right). NOTE: This is moot — T1.1 = C (private land) triggers automatic T1 FAIL regardless of T1.2 score. The T1.2 pass/fail assessment is inapplicable to private land under the SOC framework, which requires community access to a commons. Recorded for completeness.
Q7.src: State revenue law (private land title); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (eligibility for landholders)

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition, or equivalent override process affecting the scheme's operational land exists. The land is private property; no evidence of active legal encumbrance found in available records. CAG 2024-25 confirms continued scheme operation without disruption. However, this pass is moot given T1.1 = C automatic fail.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN

Q9.breakdown: Tree sapling provision: ₹45 cr; Plantation incentive (₹1,000/tree × 3.5 lakh trees): ₹35 cr; Monitoring: ₹15 cr
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department)

Q10.r: All scheme expenditure flows to private land and private actors. (1) Sapling provision (₹45 cr): saplings are sourced from certified nurseries and distributed to private farmers for planting on private land — outputs flow exclusively to private assets. (2) Plantation incentive (₹35 cr): direct cash transfer to individual private landholders at ₹1,000/tree. (3) Monitoring (₹15 cr): administrative overhead for tracking survival on private land — no commons benefit created. No expenditure component creates or maintains a commons resource accessible to the broader community. Per the framework, expenditure whose outputs flow exclusively to private actors is excluded from the numerator even if physically located near public land.
Q10.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q11.r: Total scheme expenditure of ₹95 cr (FY 2025-26) constitutes private-benefit spend: ₹45 cr saplings distributed to private farmers for private land planting; ₹35 cr cash incentive to private landholders; ₹15 cr monitoring of private plantations. Q10 = 0, Q11 = 95, sum = 95 = Q9. Rationale for Q11 = 100% of Q9: scheme design is explicitly a private land greening incentive with no commons component.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN

Q12.interp: FAIL — θ₁ = 0.00, well below the 0.50 threshold. Entire scheme expenditure benefits private land assets.

Q14.r: Benefits are fully excludable and accrue only to the individual private landholder. The tree (physical asset), the cash incentive (₹1,000/tree), and the maintenance allowance (₹500/tree/year) all accrue exclusively to the registered farmer on whose private land the plantation occurs. No other community member can access these benefits. While CO2 sequestration is a diffuse environmental benefit, it is not the scheme's primary resource benefit and is not operationalised as a community livelihood or access right. Score = 0 (fully excludable). Automatic T1 FAIL additionally triggered by this sub-parameter.

Q16.src: All 42,000 registered beneficiaries are private landholders residing in Chhattisgarh who plant on their own land. Scheme eligibility requires physical land ownership (min 0.5 ha fruit / 2 ha timber) — landowners are by definition resident in or proximate to their private holdings. No non-resident/distant actors are eligible under scheme guidelines. Source: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (eligibility criteria); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (enrollment database)

Q17.v: Settled_communities — within district/GP of their private landholding across Chhattisgarh state
Q17.r: The scheme has no bounded commons area to define 'local' against. Since T1.1 = C (private land), the applicable definition is settled communities: each beneficiary farmer resides within the GP/district where their private landholding is situated. All 42,000 enrolled farmers are Chhattisgarh residents holding land within the state. This is an inherent consequence of the scheme's private-land model: the landholder is co-located with the plantation site. Definition applied: Settled_communities (GP/district of private land holding).
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (private land eligibility); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.00, well above 0.70 threshold. All registered beneficiaries are Chhattisgarh-resident private landholders. NOTE: This pass is structurally trivial for a private-land scheme — the locality condition is automatically met by scheme eligibility design, not by commons-area proximity.
Q18.src: Derived from Q15 and Q16; Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

The scheme eligibility requires minimum landholding (0.5–2 ha), which structurally excludes landless households — one of the key marginalised groups under the SOC framework definition.>>
Q19.r: Available sources confirm total enrollment (42,000 farmers) and physical outcomes but do not disaggregate beneficiary caste/category or household type. Chhattisgarh state demographics indicate ~31% ST and ~13% SC population share, but this cannot be applied directly to scheme beneficiaries without scheme-specific disaggregated data. The minimum landholding requirement (0.5 ha fruit / 2 ha timber) excludes landless households from eligibility, which structurally suppresses marginalised inclusion for that sub-group.

22a.r: No formally constituted community representative body (Gram Sabha, User Group, JFM Committee, Village Water Committee, SHG federation, BMC, or equivalent) exists in the scheme's governance structure. Implementation is through District Forest Officers and a state-run online portal. The scheme is administered as an individual farmer-level transaction: farmer registers online, books saplings, plants, and receives incentive via direct bank transfer. No collective community body is constituted or even mentioned in the official notification or any available scheme document.

22b.r: No community body's role is mandated in scheme guidelines, since no such body is constituted. Implementation mandate rests with District Forest Officers and the state Forest Department.

22c.r: No community body exists to be functional. No meeting records, resolutions, or community governance evidence are present or applicable.

Q22.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no mention of community governance body); State Forest Portal (farmer self-service model); Outcome Budget 2025-26 (implementation via DFO)

Q23.r: No community governance body exists (see Q22). There is therefore no governance body to assess women's representation in. Women-headed households may be beneficiaries (some of the 42,000 farmers could be women landholders) but no disaggregated data on women's participation is available, and no governance structure exists where representation can be assessed. Governance gap flagged.
Q23.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; <<UNVERIFIED for women beneficiary share>>

Q24.r: No formal grievance or voice mechanism for non-beneficiaries is mandated in the scheme design. The state forest portal provides a farmer registration and incentive tracker service, but this is a beneficiary-facing administrative tool, not a grievance mechanism for those excluded from benefits. No social audit provision, Ombudsman reference, MIS grievance portal, or Gram Sabha complaint process is specified in the scheme guidelines (Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN) or referenced in any available evaluation document. Governance gap flagged.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; State Forest Portal; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q25.r: The scheme's official notification (No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN, updated 2024) explicitly mandates income output indicators: (1) Cash incentive of ₹1,000/tree as a mandatory income transfer to farmers, (2) Maintenance allowance of ₹500/tree/year for 3 years as a mandated livelihood support payment, (3) Employment generation in nursery and distribution (8,500 person-years) explicitly stated as a scheme output in the Outcome Budget 2025-26. The scheme articulates a direct cash-income pathway from the plantation activity to individual farmer households. T3.1 = 1. PASS.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (updated 2024); Outcome Budget 2025-26

observed.calc: Average 83 trees/HH (CAG-confirmed enrollment: 42,000 farmers / 42 lakh trees cumulative) × ₹1,000 incentive/tree = ₹83,000 cash incentive in planting year. Plus ₹41,500/year maintenance allowance (83 trees × ₹500/tree/year) for 3 years. Year-1 ΔY = ₹83,000 + ₹41,500 = ₹124,500; Years 2–3 ΔY = ₹41,500/year each. 3-year average ΔY = (₹124,500 + ₹41,500 + ₹41,500) ÷ 3 = ₹69,167/HH/year. Incentive payment records verified by CAG (₹420 cr total disbursed, 42,000 farmers, payment records audited).
observed.interp: PASS — ₹69,167/HH/year (3-year average) well above ₹3,000 observed threshold; ₹124,500 even in conservative year-1-only calculation exceeds threshold by 40×
observed.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (payment records audited); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (rate schedule); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (avg 83 trees/HH)

projected.calc: Observed data governs; projected modelling not separately published in DPR

Q26.r: Observed ΔY governs. CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms payment records for ₹420 cr cumulative disbursed (incentive portion) to 42,000 farmers. Observed ΔY (3-year avg) ≈ ₹69,167/HH/year, far exceeding the ₹3,000/HH/year observed threshold. Planting-year ΔY = ₹124,500/HH. No separate DPR projection published; CAG-verified actuals are sufficient.
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — ₹420 cr cumulative incentive payment verification
Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (2024 update) — ₹1,000/tree incentive + ₹500/tree/year maintenance rate schedule
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — average 83 trees/HH, ₹420 cr total incentive disbursed

Q27.r: Two distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) CASH INCENTIVE PATHWAY — Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN mandates ₹1,000/tree cash transfer at planting plus ₹500/tree/year maintenance allowance for 3 years, providing direct farm income to private landholders (83 trees avg → ₹83,000 planting + ₹41,500/year maintenance). (2) NURSERY WAGE EMPLOYMENT PATHWAY — Outcome Budget 2025-26 explicitly records 8,500 person-years of employment in sapling nursery establishment and distribution as a scheme output, constituting a distinct wage income stream for nursery workers and distributors. Note: Long-term timber/fruit income from 185 approved species is a natural consequence but is NOT explicitly mandated or tracked as a livelihood output indicator in scheme documents — excluded from count per counting rule.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q28.r: The Government of Chhattisgarh (Forest Department) functions as the institutional buyer/offtake mechanism for tree plantation services. The scheme guarantees ₹1,000/tree upon verified planting + ₹500/tree/year maintenance upon survival confirmation (78% survival verified). This is analogous to the MGNREGS wage payment model — government institutional demand creates a guaranteed offtake for the activity (plantation service + survival maintenance). Budget commitment: ₹35 cr/year plantation incentive (Demand for Grants No. 6, FY 2025-26). CAG-audited: ₹420 cr paid cumulatively, confirming the institutional demand is real and sustained over 10 years. Saplings are sourced from certified government nurseries (quality-assured procurement channel). This constitutes a state government procurement scheme for farmer plantation services.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q29.r: The scheme MIS and evaluation framework track: (1) sapling survival rate (78% at 3-year monitoring) — an operational metric, not a livelihood indicator; (2) species diversity (185 varieties) — a biodiversity metric, not a beneficiary livelihood metric; (3) CO2 sequestration potential (185 lakh tons projected) — an environmental metric, not a household livelihood metric. None of the tracked metrics qualify as non-monetary livelihood indicators per the framework definition (food sufficiency months, livestock feeding days, medicinal plant access, food species count accessed by households). NOTE: ΔY (₹69,167/HH/year avg) is approximately 23× the ₹3,000 threshold — no near-threshold concern. T3.5 absence does not raise potential under-measurement risk at this income level.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; State Forest Portal

pass_verification.C2_deltaY: PASS — Observed ΔY ≈ ₹69,167/HH/year (3-year avg); ₹124,500 in planting year; both far above ₹3,000 observed threshold. CAG-verified.
pass_verification.C3_T3.3: PASS — T3.3 = 2 (two distinct pathways: cash incentive + nursery employment)
pass_verification.C4_T3.4: PASS — Government of Chhattisgarh serves as guaranteed institutional offtake for plantation services at ₹1,000/tree + ₹500/tree/year; CAG-confirmed ₹420 cr paid over 10 years

sources_verified: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department)

Q30.r: The scheme contains no legally-binding or scheme-guideline-mandated obligation to replenish, restore, maintain, or sustain a commons resource. The 3-year survival monitoring requirement (78% target) and the maintenance allowance (₹500/tree/year × 3 years) are incentive mechanisms applied to private tree survival on private land — they are not replenishment obligations for a commons resource. The scheme's cumulative plantation target (42 lakh trees by 2026) is a state green-cover output goal, not a commons regeneration mandate. Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN specifies no afforestation ratio on commons, no recharge structure mandate, no rotational grazing protocol, no seed-bank renewal obligation, no knowledge-transmission component — none of the qualifying replenishment/continuity rules per the SOC framework apply. No consequence mechanism for non-compliance with a replenishment obligation exists because no replenishment obligation exists. AUTOMATIC T4 FAIL triggered.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (2024 update — no commons replenishment clause found); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q31.r: T1.1 (carried from t1_output.json) = 'C' (private land). Track selection rule: T1.1 ≠ E → track = RhoRatio. Q32 (ρ Ratio) is answered; Q33 (Vitality Index) is SKIP.

Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction enabled by scheme ÷ Annual replenishment mandated by scheme. Extraction: The scheme mandates tree planting on private land with no commons harvest component; no extraction from commons is enabled or tracked. Extraction from scheme activity = 0 units/year. Replenishment: Plantation survival = 3.5 lakh trees planted × 78% survival rate = 2.73 lakh trees surviving per year (Outcome Budget 2025-26). ρ = 0 ÷ 2.73 lakh = 0.00. NOTE: Concept is structurally inapplicable — there is no commons resource from which extraction occurs. The ρ ratio is computed using the only available data (plantation survival as a proxy for private-land green cover replenishment), yielding ρ = 0.00 technically within PASS threshold (≤1.2). This calculation is analytically vacuous: a ρ of 0 does not reflect a sustainable commons — it reflects the absence of any commons resource to extract from. Marked Estimated per T1.5 = No.
Q32.interp: PASS — ρ = 0.00 ≤ 1.2 (technical/formal PASS). However, this result is structurally meaningless: the scheme operates on private land with no commons resource extraction. C1_T4.1 = FAIL overrides this result — T4a partial PASS requires both C1_T4.1 = PASS AND ρ ≤ 1.2. Since C1_T4.1 = FAIL (Q30 = No), T4a partial result is FAIL regardless of ρ.
Q32.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (3.5 lakh trees planted, 78% survival); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no commons harvest mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25
docs: Outcome Budget 2025-26 — trees planted (3.5 lakh) and survival rate (78%) data
Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN — no commons extraction clause
CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — plantation records verification

Q33.calc: SKIP — track = RhoRatio (T1.1 = C ≠ E); Vitality Index does not apply

carry_for_t4b.C2_rho_or_VI: PASS (formal, vacuous — ρ = 0.00 ≤ 1.2; but concept inapplicable to private land scheme; moot given C1_T4.1 = FAIL)

carried_from_t4a.C2_rho_or_VI: PASS (formal/vacuous — ρ = 0.00; concept inapplicable to private land scheme; moot given C1_T4.1 = FAIL)

Q35.r: The scheme's primary resource is private land across Chhattisgarh state (T1.1 = C). There is no demarcated commons resource to classify under a notified climate or ecological stress zone. The scheme operates state-wide and is not targeted at or restricted to any CGWB over-exploited block, NDMA drought-prone block, ISFR high fire-risk zone, or NBA threatened-species habitat. While parts of Chhattisgarh include drought-prone and ecologically stressed areas, no scheme guideline or official notification classifies the scheme's resource (private plantation land) as lying within a notified stress zone. The Q35 stress-override does not apply. Q34 threshold remains at ≥ 1 (standard).
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no stress-zone targeting); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (state-wide coverage, not stress-zone-specific)

Q34.r: Q35 = No → threshold is Q34 ≥ 1 (standard; no climate-stress upgrade). The scheme mandates 3-year survival monitoring for each plantation batch: trees are tracked at planting, year-1, year-2, and year-3 intervals (78% survival target). CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms annual government audits of survival records and payment data. The State Forest Portal publishes aggregate statistics annually. This constitutes annual reporting by a government agency (score = 1). There is no quarterly or semi-annual community-accessible disclosure, no real-time dashboard, and no GP-noticeboard disclosure. Score = 1. With Q35 = No, threshold = ≥ 1. Formal PASS. NOTE: This monitoring applies to private tree survival on private land — not to a commons resource health metric. The concept of T4.3 (commons resource monitoring) is inapplicable here since there is no commons; the formal score reflects government administrative tracking of a private scheme output.
Q34.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (3-year survival monitoring mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (annual audit of survival records); State Forest Portal (annual statistics)

Q36.r: No penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of a replenishment or commons-use rule exists. As established in Q30 (T4.1 = No), the scheme contains no replenishment obligation for a commons resource — therefore there is no rule whose violation can be sanctioned. The maintenance allowance (₹500/tree/year) is conditional on tree survival: if trees die, the maintenance payment is withheld. However, this is an incentive conditional on private asset survival, not a penalty for commons depletion. No fine, scheme-benefit withdrawal, legal action under FRA/PESA/EPA/BDA, or GP-imposed sanction for any replenishment violation is specified in the official notification or any available scheme document. Q36 = No.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no penalty clause for commons replenishment violation found); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: No post-scheme continuity mechanism for commons governance exists. After the 3-year maintenance period concludes, planted trees become permanent private assets of the landholder — no commons governance handover is required or provided. The scheme design contains no O&M fund, no institutional handover plan to a GP/BMC/FRC/community cooperative, no legal vesting of resources in a community body, and no continuity clause in the official guidelines. The scheme's exit design is implicit: trees are planted, incentive paid over 3 years, and thereafter the private landholder manages the private asset independently. Since no commons resource was created or managed, no commons continuity mechanism is applicable or designed. Q37 = No.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no continuity clause found); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q38.r: Monitoring is conducted exclusively by government agencies with no community participation in measurement or disclosure. Survival tracking is performed by District Forest Officers. Annual audits are conducted by CAG. The State Forest Portal publishes departmental statistics. No GP social audit, no Forest Rights Committee patrol, no BMC register upkeep, no participatory resource mapping, and no community monitoring committee is mentioned in any scheme document or evaluation report. Q38 = No. Governance gap flagged.

Q4.r: The scheme explicitly and exclusively targets private landholdings. Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN specifies 'Target: 3.5 lakh trees/year on private land.' Eligibility requires min 0.5 ha (fruit) / 2 ha (timber) private landholding. Economic Survey 2024-25 confirms 'tree plantations on private land.' CAG 2024-25 confirms 42,000 registered farmers with private holdings. All plantation activity, incentive payments, and sapling distribution flow to and remain on private property. This is definitively Type C — private land. AUTOMATIC TEST 1 FAIL triggered.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q5.v: The primary resource is private agricultural/forestry land held by 42,000 registered farmers across Chhattisgarh state. Approximate coverage: 22,500 hectares (calculated at 1,867 trees/hectare, cumulative since 2015). Minimum holding eligibility: 0.5 hectare (fruit tree plantation) or 2 hectare (timber plantation). Species planted: 185 approved varieties (fruit, timber, fodder). No fixed geographic boundary — scheme is state-wide, operating across districts. Individual parcels are private property with no commons demarcation.
Q5.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q6.r: No commons resource exists to demarcate. The scheme operates on private land parcels. Individual farmer parcels are registered in the scheme portal (42,000 enrolled), but this constitutes private land records, not a public commons boundary document. No GP resolution, watershed boundary map, survey number for a commons resource, or equivalent public commons demarcation document exists or is applicable.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; State Forest Portal

Q7.r: Farmers hold statutory freehold property rights to their private land under applicable state revenue law and registration records. Score = 2 (statutory/legally enforceable right). NOTE: This is moot — T1.1 = C (private land) triggers automatic T1 FAIL regardless of T1.2 score. The T1.2 pass/fail assessment is inapplicable to private land under the SOC framework, which requires community access to a commons. Recorded for completeness.

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition, or equivalent override process affecting the scheme's operational land exists. The land is private property; no evidence of active legal encumbrance found in available records. CAG 2024-25 confirms continued scheme operation without disruption. However, this pass is moot given T1.1 = C automatic fail.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN

Q9.breakdown: Tree sapling provision: ₹45 cr; Plantation incentive (₹1,000/tree × 3.5 lakh trees): ₹35 cr; Monitoring: ₹15 cr
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department)

Q10.r: All scheme expenditure flows to private land and private actors. (1) Sapling provision (₹45 cr): saplings are sourced from certified nurseries and distributed to private farmers for planting on private land — outputs flow exclusively to private assets. (2) Plantation incentive (₹35 cr): direct cash transfer to individual private landholders at ₹1,000/tree. (3) Monitoring (₹15 cr): administrative overhead for tracking survival on private land — no commons benefit created. No expenditure component creates or maintains a commons resource accessible to the broader community. Per the framework, expenditure whose outputs flow exclusively to private actors is excluded from the numerator even if physically located near public land.
Q10.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q11.r: Total scheme expenditure of ₹95 cr (FY 2025-26) constitutes private-benefit spend: ₹45 cr saplings distributed to private farmers for private land planting; ₹35 cr cash incentive to private landholders; ₹15 cr monitoring of private plantations. Q10 = 0, Q11 = 95, sum = 95 = Q9. Rationale for Q11 = 100% of Q9: scheme design is explicitly a private land greening incentive with no commons component.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN

Q14.r: Benefits are fully excludable and accrue only to the individual private landholder. The tree (physical asset), the cash incentive (₹1,000/tree), and the maintenance allowance (₹500/tree/year) all accrue exclusively to the registered farmer on whose private land the plantation occurs. No other community member can access these benefits. While CO2 sequestration is a diffuse environmental benefit, it is not the scheme's primary resource benefit and is not operationalised as a community livelihood or access right. Score = 0 (fully excludable). Automatic T1 FAIL additionally triggered by this sub-parameter.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (eligibility: individual landholders); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (individual farmer incentive payments)

Scheme eligibility requires physical land ownership (min 0.5 ha fruit / 2 ha timber) — landowners are by definition resident in or proximate to their private holdings. No non-resident/distant actors are eligible under scheme guidelines. Source: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (eligibility criteria); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (enrollment database)

Since T1.1 = C (private land), the applicable definition is settled communities: each beneficiary farmer resides within the GP/district where their private landholding is situated. All 42,000 enrolled farmers are Chhattisgarh residents holding land within the state. This is an inherent consequence of the scheme's private-land model: the landholder is co-located with the plantation site. Definition applied: Settled_communities (GP/district of private land holding).
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (private land eligibility); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

22a.r: No formally constituted community representative body (Gram Sabha, User Group, JFM Committee, Village Water Committee, SHG federation, BMC, or equivalent) exists in the scheme's governance structure. Implementation is through District Forest Officers and a state-run online portal. The scheme is administered as an individual farmer-level transaction: farmer registers online, books saplings, plants, and receives incentive via direct bank transfer. No collective community body is constituted or even mentioned in the official notification or any available scheme document.

22b.r: No community body's role is mandated in scheme guidelines, since no such body is constituted. Implementation mandate rests with District Forest Officers and the state Forest Department.

22c.r: No community body exists to be functional. No meeting records, resolutions, or community governance evidence are present or applicable.

Q23.r: No community governance body exists (see Q22). There is therefore no governance body to assess women's representation in. Women-headed households may be beneficiaries (some of the 42,000 farmers could be women landholders) but no disaggregated data on women's participation is available, and no governance structure exists where representation can be assessed. Governance gap flagged.

Q24.r: No formal grievance or voice mechanism for non-beneficiaries is mandated in the scheme design. The state forest portal provides a farmer registration and incentive tracker service, but this is a beneficiary-facing administrative tool, not a grievance mechanism for those excluded from benefits. No social audit provision, Ombudsman reference, MIS grievance portal, or Gram Sabha complaint process is specified in the scheme guidelines (Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN) or referenced in any available evaluation document. Governance gap flagged.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; State Forest Portal; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q25.r: The scheme's official notification (No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN, updated 2024) explicitly mandates income output indicators: (1) Cash incentive of ₹1,000/tree as a mandatory income transfer to farmers, (2) Maintenance allowance of ₹500/tree/year for 3 years as a mandated livelihood support payment, (3) Employment generation in nursery and distribution (8,500 person-years) explicitly stated as a scheme output in the Outcome Budget 2025-26. The scheme articulates a direct cash-income pathway from the plantation activity to individual farmer households. T3.1 = 1. PASS.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (updated 2024); Outcome Budget 2025-26

observed.calc: Average 83 trees/HH (CAG-confirmed enrollment: 42,000 farmers / 42 lakh trees cumulative) × ₹1,000 incentive/tree = ₹83,000 cash incentive in planting year. Plus ₹41,500/year maintenance allowance (83 trees × ₹500/tree/year) for 3 years. Year-1 ΔY = ₹83,000 + ₹41,500 = ₹124,500; Years 2–3 ΔY = ₹41,500/year each. 3-year average ΔY = (₹124,500 + ₹41,500 + ₹41,500) ÷ 3 = ₹69,167/HH/year. Incentive payment records verified by CAG (₹420 cr total disbursed, 42,000 farmers, payment records audited).
observed.interp: PASS — ₹69,167/HH/year (3-year average) well above ₹3,000 observed threshold; ₹124,500 even in conservative year-1-only calculation exceeds threshold by 40×
observed.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (payment records audited); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (rate schedule); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (avg 83 trees/HH)

projected.calc: Observed data governs; projected modelling not separately published in DPR

Q26.r: Observed ΔY governs. CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms payment records for ₹420 cr cumulative disbursed (incentive portion) to 42,000 farmers. Observed ΔY (3-year avg) ≈ ₹69,167/HH/year, far exceeding the ₹3,000/HH/year observed threshold. Planting-year ΔY = ₹124,500/HH. No separate DPR projection published; CAG-verified actuals are sufficient.
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — ₹420 cr cumulative incentive payment verification
Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (2024 update) — ₹1,000/tree incentive + ₹500/tree/year maintenance rate schedule
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — average 83 trees/HH, ₹420 cr total incentive disbursed

Q27.r: Two distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) CASH INCENTIVE PATHWAY — Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN mandates ₹1,000/tree cash transfer at planting plus ₹500/tree/year maintenance allowance for 3 years, providing direct farm income to private landholders (83 trees avg → ₹83,000 planting + ₹41,500/year maintenance). (2) NURSERY WAGE EMPLOYMENT PATHWAY — Outcome Budget 2025-26 explicitly records 8,500 person-years of employment in sapling nursery establishment and distribution as a scheme output, constituting a distinct wage income stream for nursery workers and distributors. Note: Long-term timber/fruit income from 185 approved species is a natural consequence but is NOT explicitly mandated or tracked as a livelihood output indicator in scheme documents — excluded from count per counting rule.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q28.r: The Government of Chhattisgarh (Forest Department) functions as the institutional buyer/offtake mechanism for tree plantation services. The scheme guarantees ₹1,000/tree upon verified planting + ₹500/tree/year maintenance upon survival confirmation (78% survival verified). This is analogous to the MGNREGS wage payment model — government institutional demand creates a guaranteed offtake for the activity (plantation service + survival maintenance). Budget commitment: ₹35 cr/year plantation incentive (Demand for Grants No. 6, FY 2025-26). CAG-audited: ₹420 cr paid cumulatively, confirming the institutional demand is real and sustained over 10 years. Saplings are sourced from certified government nurseries (quality-assured procurement channel). This constitutes a state government procurement scheme for farmer plantation services.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Demand for Grants No. 6); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q29.r: The scheme MIS and evaluation framework track: (1) sapling survival rate (78% at 3-year monitoring) — an operational metric, not a livelihood indicator; (2) species diversity (185 varieties) — a biodiversity metric, not a beneficiary livelihood metric; (3) CO2 sequestration potential (185 lakh tons projected) — an environmental metric, not a household livelihood metric. None of the tracked metrics qualify as non-monetary livelihood indicators per the framework definition (food sufficiency months, livestock feeding days, medicinal plant access, food species count accessed by households). NOTE: ΔY (₹69,167/HH/year avg) is approximately 23× the ₹3,000 threshold — no near-threshold concern. T3.5 absence does not raise potential under-measurement risk at this income level.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; State Forest Portal

Q30.r: The scheme contains no legally-binding or scheme-guideline-mandated obligation to replenish, restore, maintain, or sustain a commons resource. The 3-year survival monitoring requirement (78% target) and the maintenance allowance (₹500/tree/year × 3 years) are incentive mechanisms applied to private tree survival on private land — they are not replenishment obligations for a commons resource. The scheme's cumulative plantation target (42 lakh trees by 2026) is a state green-cover output goal, not a commons regeneration mandate. Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN specifies no afforestation ratio on commons, no recharge structure mandate, no rotational grazing protocol, no seed-bank renewal obligation, no knowledge-transmission component — none of the qualifying replenishment/continuity rules per the SOC framework apply. No consequence mechanism for non-compliance with a replenishment obligation exists because no replenishment obligation exists. AUTOMATIC T4 FAIL triggered.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (2024 update — no commons replenishment clause found); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q31.r: T1.1 (carried from t1_output.json) = 'C' (private land). Track selection rule: T1.1 ≠ E → track = RhoRatio. Q32 (ρ Ratio) is answered; Q33 (Vitality Index) is SKIP.

Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction enabled by scheme ÷ Annual replenishment mandated by scheme. Extraction: The scheme mandates tree planting on private land with no commons harvest component; no extraction from commons is enabled or tracked. Extraction from scheme activity = 0 units/year. Replenishment: Plantation survival = 3.5 lakh trees planted × 78% survival rate = 2.73 lakh trees surviving per year (Outcome Budget 2025-26). ρ = 0 ÷ 2.73 lakh = 0.00. NOTE: Concept is structurally inapplicable — there is no commons resource from which extraction occurs. The ρ ratio is computed using the only available data (plantation survival as a proxy for private-land green cover replenishment), yielding ρ = 0.00 technically within PASS threshold (≤1.2). This calculation is analytically vacuous: a ρ of 0 does not reflect a sustainable commons — it reflects the absence of any commons resource to extract from. Marked Estimated per T1.5 = No.
Q32.interp: PASS — ρ = 0.00 ≤ 1.2 (technical/formal PASS). However, this result is structurally meaningless: the scheme operates on private land with no commons resource extraction. C1_T4.1 = FAIL overrides this result — T4a partial PASS requires both C1_T4.1 = PASS AND ρ ≤ 1.2. Since C1_T4.1 = FAIL (Q30 = No), T4a partial result is FAIL regardless of ρ.
Q32.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (3.5 lakh trees planted, 78% survival); Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no commons harvest mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25
docs: Outcome Budget 2025-26 — trees planted (3.5 lakh) and survival rate (78%) data
Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN — no commons extraction clause
CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — plantation records verification

Q33.calc: SKIP — track = RhoRatio (T1.1 = C ≠ E); Vitality Index does not apply

Q35.r: The scheme's primary resource is private land across Chhattisgarh state (T1.1 = C). There is no demarcated commons resource to classify under a notified climate or ecological stress zone. The scheme operates state-wide and is not targeted at or restricted to any CGWB over-exploited block, NDMA drought-prone block, ISFR high fire-risk zone, or NBA threatened-species habitat. While parts of Chhattisgarh include drought-prone and ecologically stressed areas, no scheme guideline or official notification classifies the scheme's resource (private plantation land) as lying within a notified stress zone. The Q35 stress-override does not apply. Q34 threshold remains at ≥ 1 (standard).
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no stress-zone targeting); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (state-wide coverage, not stress-zone-specific)

Q34.r: Q35 = No → threshold is Q34 ≥ 1 (standard; no climate-stress upgrade). The scheme mandates 3-year survival monitoring for each plantation batch: trees are tracked at planting, year-1, year-2, and year-3 intervals (78% survival target). CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms annual government audits of survival records and payment data. The State Forest Portal publishes aggregate statistics annually. This constitutes annual reporting by a government agency (score = 1). There is no quarterly or semi-annual community-accessible disclosure, no real-time dashboard, and no GP-noticeboard disclosure. Score = 1. With Q35 = No, threshold = ≥ 1. Formal PASS. NOTE: This monitoring applies to private tree survival on private land — not to a commons resource health metric. The concept of T4.3 (commons resource monitoring) is inapplicable here since there is no commons; the formal score reflects government administrative tracking of a private scheme output.
Q34.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (3-year survival monitoring mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (annual audit of survival records); State Forest Portal (annual statistics)

Q36.r: No penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of a replenishment or commons-use rule exists. As established in Q30 (T4.1 = No), the scheme contains no replenishment obligation for a commons resource — therefore there is no rule whose violation can be sanctioned. The maintenance allowance (₹500/tree/year) is conditional on tree survival: if trees die, the maintenance payment is withheld. However, this is an incentive conditional on private asset survival, not a penalty for commons depletion. No fine, scheme-benefit withdrawal, legal action under FRA/PESA/EPA/BDA, or GP-imposed sanction for any replenishment violation is specified in the official notification or any available scheme document. Q36 = No.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no penalty clause for commons replenishment violation found); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: No post-scheme continuity mechanism for commons governance exists. After the 3-year maintenance period concludes, planted trees become permanent private assets of the landholder — no commons governance handover is required or provided. The scheme design contains no O&M fund, no institutional handover plan to a GP/BMC/FRC/community cooperative, no legal vesting of resources in a community body, and no continuity clause in the official guidelines. The scheme's exit design is implicit: trees are planted, incentive paid over 3 years, and thereafter the private landholder manages the private asset independently. Since no commons resource was created or managed, no commons continuity mechanism is applicable or designed. Q37 = No.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN (no continuity clause found); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q38.r: Monitoring is conducted exclusively by government agencies with no community participation in measurement or disclosure. Survival tracking is performed by District Forest Officers. Annual audits are conducted by CAG. The State Forest Portal publishes departmental statistics. No GP social audit, no Forest Rights Committee patrol, no BMC register upkeep, no participatory resource mapping, and no community monitoring committee is mentioned in any scheme document or evaluation report. Q38 = No. Governance gap flagged.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 6-CMP/2015/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; State Forest Portal

Q4.r: The primary resource the scheme operates on is privately owned dairy livestock (cattle/buffalo) acquired by individual beneficiaries through the dairy animal purchase support subsidy and housed in privately constructed/subsidised sheds. The scheme's budget line items — dairy unit subsidy for shed construction, dairy animal purchase support, feed supplement cost — are all directed at private asset acquisition for individual beneficiaries. Feed supplements and veterinary health programs attach to privately owned animals. Cooperative milk collection/processing infrastructure is listed secondary in the budget description and is supportive of private production, not the primary scheme resource. There is no communal grazing reserve, shared livestock pool, or community-governed animal resource identified as the operative commons.

Private infrastructure includes dairy sheds (subsidy-funded). Secondary cooperative infrastructure includes milk chilling/collection centres and dairy processing units for value-added products (ghee, curd, paneer). No defined geographic commons boundary is identified. No communal grazing reserve, shared pastoral land, or state-notified community resource forms the scheme's operative resource base. State of operation not specified in programs.md.
