Q3.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN

Q4.r: The primary resource is the community cooperative organic matter system governed by cooperative societies and Gram Panchayats. The scheme operates through GP-level collection centers (one per 500 cattle) and 280 community-managed compost/vermicompost processing units run through registered cooperative societies. The resource is governed by a formally constituted community group (cooperative societies under GP oversight), qualifying as a Common Pool Resource (Category B). The cow dung aggregated at collection centers ceases to be individual private property once it enters the cooperative processing system.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Implementation through cooperative societies); Outcome Budget 2025-26 (280 processing units operational)

Q5.v: Cow dung collected from 2.8 lakh cattle-owning households across Chhattisgarh, aggregated at GP-level collection centers (one center per 500 cattle), processed through 280 community compost/vermicompost processing units operated by cooperative societies. Annual procurement: 3.8 lakh MT (FY 2024-25 actual). Output: 3.0 lakh MT organic manure distributed to farmers at subsidized rates (₹500/MT). Scheme covers the whole of Chhattisgarh with GP administrative boundaries defining coverage areas. No specific GPS coordinates or survey numbers are recorded in available public documents for individual collection centers.
Q5.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q6.r: The official notification (No. 26-C-2020/CGN) establishes collection centers at the GP level with defined coverage (one center per 500 cattle per GP). GP administrative boundaries are legally demarcated units under the Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj Act, providing institutional boundary documentation. While GPS coordinates or specific survey numbers for processing units are not available in the public data consulted, the GP-level institutional demarcation constitutes a public boundary document (GP resolution framework under Panchayat Raj Act).
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj Act (GP administrative boundary framework)

Q7.r: The community's access and practice right is statutory and legally enforceable. The scheme is established by an official state government notification (No. 26-C-2020/CGN), granting all cattle owners the right to participate in cow dung procurement. Implementation through cooperative societies (registered legal entities) under the cooperative framework. GP-level collection centers are mandated by state government order. The Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj Act grants GPs jurisdiction over community resources and cooperative activities in the village. These are statutory rights, not merely customary.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj Act; Cooperative Societies Act (Chhattisgarh)

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, change-of-land-use notification, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition proceeding, or equivalent encumbrance is recorded on the scheme's operational resources in any available public document. The scheme is actively operational with 280 processing units functional and procurement continuing as of FY 2024-25 per CAG audit.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal

Q9.breakdown: Cow dung procurement at ₹2/kg for 4 lakh MT: ₹200 cr; Compost/vermicompost processing unit establishment: ₹120 cr; Organic manure subsidy to farmers: ₹50 cr; Marketing and distribution: ₹10 cr
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (FY 2024-25 actual procurement payments: ₹190 cr for 3.8 lakh MT)

Q10.r: All expenditure flows through the community cooperative system with community-level benefits and no expenditure exclusively benefiting a single private actor. (1) Cow dung procurement (₹200 cr): payments are distributed across 2.8 lakh cattle-owner households through the cooperative system; the output — cow dung aggregated into community processing units — is a community resource. This is analogous to MGNREGS wages paid to individuals for labour on commons: the individual wage payment is the mechanism, but the output and purpose are commons development. (2) Processing unit establishment (₹120 cr): community cooperative infrastructure (280 GP-level units) serving all cattle owners and farmers — clearly commons expenditure. (3) Organic manure subsidy (₹50 cr): distributed to farmers across 1.2 lakh hectares to enable adoption of organic farming, producing community-wide soil health improvement (soil health is a commons benefit). (4) Marketing and distribution (₹10 cr): cooperative marketing channel expenditure serving the whole scheme system. No component exclusively benefits a single private household.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q11.r: No expenditure component flows exclusively to a single private actor or exclusively private assets. The cow dung procurement distributes payments across 2.8 lakh cattle owners (community-wide distribution), not to a single private entity. The organic manure subsidy distributes across 1.2 lakh hectares of farmers. Processing units are community cooperative infrastructure. Marketing and distribution serves the cooperative system. The exclusion criterion ('outputs flowing exclusively to private assets' such as a pipeline serving only one farm or a tube-well benefiting a single household) is not met by any component of this scheme.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q12.interp: PASS — θ₁ = 1.00, well above the 0.50 threshold. All scheme expenditure serves the community cooperative system with no exclusive private-actor benefit.
Q12.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q14.r: Benefits are non-excludable (Score 2). The organic manure produced is distributed to all adopting farmers at subsidized rates (₹500/MT) with no membership fee or exclusion mechanism — any farmer in Chhattisgarh can access it through the scheme. The soil health improvement (organic matter +2.3% average, yield improvement 8-12%) is a public-good externality benefiting all farmers in the area, not just scheme participants. The procurement income opportunity is open to all cattle owners ('Eligibility: All cattle owners' per official notification). No exclusion mechanism based on fees, membership, or community identity is documented.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal

Q17.r: The scheme establishes collection centers at the GP level (one per 500 cattle per GP) per Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN. Cattle owners sell cow dung at the collection center of their own GP — they are by design residents of the GP where the commons resource (cooperative processing system) operates. All 2.8 lakh beneficiaries are therefore local to their respective GP-level commons. Settled-community definition applies: within the GP/block unit.
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN

22a.r: Cooperative societies are formally constituted registered legal entities implementing the scheme at GP level per Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN. These cooperatives serve as the community representative body governing cow dung collection and processing. 280 cooperative-managed processing units are formally established as scheme output.

22b.r: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN mandates implementation through cooperative societies with defined roles: collection center operation, cow dung procurement, processing unit management, and organic manure distribution. The cooperative's role is not optional or advisory — it is the mandated implementation vehicle specified in the official scheme notification.

22c.r: Functionality evidenced by: (1) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms fund reconciliation 'complete with village-level records' and payment accuracy of 99.1% — indicating active and documented governance activity; (2) 280 processing units operational as verified by CAG; (3) ₹190 crores disbursed across 2.8 lakh cattle owners in FY 2024-25 (requires continuous cooperative management and record-keeping); (4) Outcome Budget 2025-26 shows continuous procurement (3.8 lakh MT in FY 2024-25). Note: specific cooperative society meeting minutes are not available in public documents consulted — functionality is inferred from verified operational outcomes rather than meeting records directly.

Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN (cooperative mandate)
Q22.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN

Q23.r: Women's representation in cooperative society governing bodies implementing the scheme is not documented in any official source consulted (Official Notification, CAG audit, Economic Survey, Outcome Budget, National Portal). The 73rd Constitutional Amendment mandates ≥33% women in elected local bodies (GPs), but cooperative society governing boards are not subject to the same legal requirement and their composition is not reported in scheme documents. Given the significant share of women-headed households among rural cattle owners in Chhattisgarh, this represents an upgrade pathway gap: scheme benefits are accessed by women cattle owners but women's decision-making role in cooperative governance is undocumented.
Q23.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (no women's representation data found in any source)

Q24.r: No institutionally mandated grievance mechanism for excluded non-beneficiaries (e.g., landless households without cattle who cannot access procurement income, or farmers unable to obtain organic manure) is documented in official scheme guidelines or notification. The National Portal includes an 'application facility' for processing unit applicants, which is a beneficiary onboarding tool, not a grievance mechanism for excluded parties. No Social Audit provision, Ombudsman, or GP complaint process is mandated at scheme level in Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN. Note: the scheme's universal eligibility ('all cattle owners') minimises exclusion from the primary benefit stream, but landless/non-cattle-owning households have no documented voice channel.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; National Portal; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

t3.data_source_summary: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN dated 20.07.2020; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal

Q25.r: The scheme mandates multiple livelihood and income output indicators explicitly in its guidelines and outcome framework. (1) Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN sets a mandatory procurement price of ₹2/kg for cow dung, creating a guaranteed income stream for cattle owners — an explicit income pathway. (2) Outcome Budget 2025-26 includes mandatory output metrics: revenue to cattle owners (₹190 crores annual), processing units operational (target: 280), organic manure produced (target: 3.2 lakh MT), and farmer adoption (1.2 lakh hectares). (3) Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 explicitly states the scheme 'creates income for livestock raisers.' These are mandatory, not aspirational, output indicators in the scheme's operational framework.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q26.r: Observed ΔY governs per framework rule. CAG-verified FY 2024-25 data (₹190 cr total procurement payments to 2.8 lakh cattle owners) provides a verified, conservative income figure of ₹6,785/HH/year. Observed data is available and governs; projected-only flag is not required.

Outcome Budget 2025-26 (beneficiary count: 2.8 lakh cattle owners)

Q27.r: Three distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) Cow dung sales income: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN mandates ₹2/kg procurement price at GP collection centers, creating a direct and guaranteed livestock income pathway for 2.8 lakh cattle-owner households; this is the scheme's primary economic mechanism. (2) Wage employment in processing units: Outcome Budget 2025-26 mandates 280 compost/vermicompost processing units as scheme output, each requiring operational staff — this is an explicit employment pathway articulated in scheme design (not coincidental). (3) Organic farming yield improvement: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 documents that the scheme 'improves soil health and creates income for livestock raisers' with a measured yield impact of 8-12% increase on organic farms and 45% increase in organic manure adoption — the scheme design explicitly channels organic manure to farmers as a livelihood-enhancing agricultural input at subsidized ₹500/MT.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q28.r: The Chhattisgarh state government acts as guaranteed institutional buyer for cow dung at a fixed price of ₹2/kg, mandated by Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN. This is a government procurement mechanism with a fixed statutory price — the strongest form of institutional offtake backstop. The District Agriculture and Cooperation Departments operate the procurement. Additionally, government agencies procure and distribute organic manure at ₹500/MT to farmers (organic manure subsidy: ₹50 cr per State Budget 2025-26 + ₹84 cr subsidy disbursement per CAG audit). The scheme is not market-dependent — the state provides the price guarantee and the institutional buyer role.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN (₹2/kg fixed procurement price mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (₹84 crores organic manure subsidy disbursement); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q29.r: The scheme tracks multiple non-monetary livelihood indicators. (1) Soil health improvement: Outcome Budget 2025-26 and Economic Survey 2024-25 track organic matter content (measured at +2.3% average increase) — a direct non-monetary soil health metric. (2) Organic manure adoption area: 1.2 lakh hectares of farmer adoption tracked, reflecting food production security improvement. (3) Yield impact: 8-12% yield increase on organic farms tracked as a non-monetary productivity metric (documented in Economic Survey). (4) National Portal explicitly tracks soil health improvement metrics alongside monetary income data. ΔY is ₹6,785 — well above the ₹3,000 threshold, so the near-threshold under-measurement flag does not apply.
Q29.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal (soil health metrics and farmer adoption tracking)

t4a.data_source_summary: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN dated 20.07.2020; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q30.r: The scheme mandates a continuity and renewal obligation for the cooperative organic matter commons through multiple mechanisms: (1) Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN mandates establishment and operation of one collection center per 500 cattle at GP level — this is a structural maintenance obligation for the cooperative system. (2) Outcome Budget 2025-26 mandates specific annual procurement targets (4 lakh MT) and processing unit operational targets (280 units) — underperformance against these mandated targets triggers budget revision/non-renewal (consequence mechanism). (3) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms 'scheme sustainability: self-financing through organic manure sales after subsidy phase' — the scheme design includes a financial continuity obligation ensuring the cooperative processing system can self-sustain without indefinite state subsidy. (4) The organic manure distribution mandate (3.2 lakh MT to farmers) itself constitutes a soil fertility replenishment obligation — the scheme's core mechanism IS soil commons replenishment through the cooperative organic cycle. Non-compliance with collection/processing mandates would constitute scheme failure with budget consequences.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26 (mandated targets); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (self-financing sustainability note)

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = 'B' (Community land / Common Pool Resource governed by cooperative societies). T1.1 ≠ E (not an intangible/knowledge/cultural commons). Per track rule: T1.1 ∈ {A1, A2, B, D} → Track = RhoRatio. Q32 will be answered; Q33 is SKIP.

Q32.calc: Proxy method (Estimated): Soil organic matter commons. Annual replenishment enabled by scheme: 3.2 lakh MT organic manure distributed to 1.2 lakh ha = 2.67 MT/ha/year of organic matter input. Annual extraction proxy (soil organic matter depletion from cropping): typical range 1.0–2.0 MT organic matter equivalent/ha/year for rainfed agriculture in Central India (per standard agronomic literature). Estimated ρ = midpoint extraction (1.5 MT/ha) ÷ scheme replenishment (2.67 MT/ha) ≈ 0.56. Conservative upper bound: 2.0 MT/ha ÷ 2.67 MT/ha ≈ 0.75. Supported by Outcome Budget 2025-26 observed outcome: soil organic matter +2.3% average on organic farms — a net positive change confirming ρ < 1.0. Adopted estimate: ρ ≈ 0.60 (conservative midpoint).
Q32.interp: PASS — estimated ρ ≈ 0.60 ≤ 1.2 threshold. Soil organic matter is increasing (+2.3% measured), confirming replenishment-dominant system. Note: T1.5 = Yes (GP boundary demarcated), so Measured classification is permissible where MIS data exists; however, direct extraction/replenishment flow data at field level is not in public documents. Estimated proxy based on organic matter balance and observed net improvement. Re-verify with scheme-level soil carbon monitoring data when available.
Q32.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (organic manure: 3.2 lakh MT, coverage: 1.2 lakh ha, soil organic matter +2.3%); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (yield impact 8-12% on organic farms)

Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (soil health improvement, farmer adoption metrics)

Q33.interp: SKIP — T1.1 = 'B' (not 'E'), VitalityIndex track does not apply

t4b.data_source_summary: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN dated 20.07.2020; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; National Portal; NDMA drought classification (for Q35)

Q35.r: Godhan Nyay Yojana operates state-wide across Chhattisgarh. While several blocks in northern Chhattisgarh (Surguja division) and parts of central Chhattisgarh appear on NDMA drought-prone lists, the scheme's primary resource — the state-wide cooperative organic matter system and agricultural soil commons — spans the entire state and is not confined to NDMA-notified drought-prone blocks. No official notification designates the scheme's operational area as a whole as a climate-stressed zone (CGWB over-exploited block, NDMA drought-prone, or equivalent) in the documents consulted. Standard T4.3 threshold (≥1) applies; no upgrade triggered.
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN (state-wide scheme scope); NDMA district drought classification (Chhattisgarh — partial drought-prone blocks, not state-wide designation)

Q34.r: Annual monitoring is documented through three channels: (1) Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks key resource health metrics annually — cow dung collected (3.8 lakh MT), processing units operational (280), organic manure produced (3.0 lakh MT), soil organic matter improvement (+2.3%), and farmer adoption (1.2 lakh ha); (2) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 conducts annual independent audit of fund management, procurement payments, and processing unit verification; (3) National Portal tracks procurement statistics, price data, and beneficiary metrics — update frequency not specified but includes live price tracking. Monitoring is by government agencies only (Agriculture Department, CAG, National Portal). No quarterly or real-time community-accessible disclosure documented. Since Q35 = No (no climate stress upgrade), T4.3 threshold is ≥1 — annual monitoring is sufficient to PASS.
Q34.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal (Agriculture > Organic Farming > Soil Health)

Q36.r: No explicit, documented financial penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of replenishment or use obligations is stated in Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN or the Outcome Budget. The consequence for non-performance is implicit through the Outcome Budget framework (underperformance against mandated targets leads to budget revision in subsequent years) but this is a budget management mechanism, not a mandated scheme-level penalty for commons violation. No fine, benefit withdrawal, suspension of access, or legal sanction provision is articulated in scheme guidelines for failure to maintain processing units or meet procurement obligations. Note: Q37 is evaluated; C4 pass condition requires Q36 = Yes OR Q37 = Yes.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: The scheme explicitly designs for post-subsidy continuity through two documented mechanisms: (1) Self-financing model: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 records 'Scheme sustainability: Self-financing through organic manure sales after subsidy phase.' The cooperative processing units are designed to generate revenue from organic manure sales (₹500/MT) sufficient to sustain operations after the state subsidy period ends — this is a financially designed continuity mechanism, not an assumption. (2) Cooperative institutional continuity: Implementation through cooperative societies (registered legal entities under Cooperative Societies Act) provides an institutional vehicle that persists after scheme intervention. The 280 processing units are physical assets belonging to cooperative societies that remain operational under the cooperative governance structure. The cooperative structure provides post-scheme institutional continuity without requiring ongoing state subsidy.
Q37.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (self-financing sustainability note); Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN (cooperative implementation mandate); Chhattisgarh Cooperative Societies Act

Q38.r: No community-led monitoring mechanism is mandated in scheme design or documented in official sources. All monitoring is conducted by government agencies: Agriculture Department (Outcome Budget tracking), CAG (annual audit), and National Portal (government-managed statistics). No Social Audit provision, community monitoring committee, participatory resource assessment, or community-managed data platform is established under the scheme. The National Portal includes a 'procurement center locator and price tracking' but this is a government-managed tool, not a community monitoring instrument. The absence of community-led monitoring is the primary structural governance gap in the sustainability lock. Corrective action: mandate annual GP-level social audit of cooperative performance and procurement data, with results posted at GP noticeboard.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; National Portal; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q3.v: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN dated 20.07.2020; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25
Q3.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN

Q4.r: The primary resource is the community cooperative organic matter system governed by cooperative societies and Gram Panchayats. The scheme operates through GP-level collection centers (one per 500 cattle) and 280 community-managed compost/vermicompost processing units run through registered cooperative societies. The resource is governed by a formally constituted community group (cooperative societies under GP oversight), qualifying as a Common Pool Resource (Category B). The cow dung aggregated at collection centers ceases to be individual private property once it enters the cooperative processing system.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (Implementation through cooperative societies); Outcome Budget 2025-26 (280 processing units operational)

Q5.v: Cow dung collected from 2.8 lakh cattle-owning households across Chhattisgarh, aggregated at GP-level collection centers (one center per 500 cattle), processed through 280 community compost/vermicompost processing units operated by cooperative societies. Annual procurement: 3.8 lakh MT (FY 2024-25 actual). Output: 3.0 lakh MT organic manure distributed to farmers at subsidized rates (₹500/MT). Scheme covers the whole of Chhattisgarh with GP administrative boundaries defining coverage areas. No specific GPS coordinates or survey numbers are recorded in available public documents for individual collection centers.
Q5.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q6.r: The official notification (No. 26-C-2020/CGN) establishes collection centers at the GP level with defined coverage (one center per 500 cattle per GP). GP administrative boundaries are legally demarcated units under the Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj Act, providing institutional boundary documentation. While GPS coordinates or specific survey numbers for processing units are not available in the public data consulted, the GP-level institutional demarcation constitutes a public boundary document (GP resolution framework under Panchayat Raj Act).
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj Act (GP administrative boundary framework)

Q7.r: The community's access and practice right is statutory and legally enforceable. The scheme is established by an official state government notification (No. 26-C-2020/CGN), granting all cattle owners the right to participate in cow dung procurement. Implementation through cooperative societies (registered legal entities) under the cooperative framework. GP-level collection centers are mandated by state government order. The Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj Act grants GPs jurisdiction over community resources and cooperative activities in the village. These are statutory rights, not merely customary.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj Act; Cooperative Societies Act (Chhattisgarh)

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, change-of-land-use notification, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition proceeding, or equivalent encumbrance is recorded on the scheme's operational resources in any available public document. The scheme is actively operational with 280 processing units functional and procurement continuing as of FY 2024-25 per CAG audit.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal

Q9.breakdown: Cow dung procurement at ₹2/kg for 4 lakh MT: ₹200 cr; Compost/vermicompost processing unit establishment: ₹120 cr; Organic manure subsidy to farmers: ₹50 cr; Marketing and distribution: ₹10 cr
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (FY 2024-25 actual procurement payments: ₹190 cr for 3.8 lakh MT)

Q10.r: All expenditure flows through the community cooperative system with community-level benefits and no expenditure exclusively benefiting a single private actor. (1) Cow dung procurement (₹200 cr): payments are distributed across 2.8 lakh cattle-owner households through the cooperative system; the output — cow dung aggregated into community processing units — is a community resource. This is analogous to MGNREGS wages paid to individuals for labour on commons: the individual wage payment is the mechanism, but the output and purpose are commons development. (2) Processing unit establishment (₹120 cr): community cooperative infrastructure (280 GP-level units) serving all cattle owners and farmers — clearly commons expenditure. (3) Organic manure subsidy (₹50 cr): distributed to farmers across 1.2 lakh hectares to enable adoption of organic farming, producing community-wide soil health improvement (soil health is a commons benefit). (4) Marketing and distribution (₹10 cr): cooperative marketing channel expenditure serving the whole scheme system. No component exclusively benefits a single private household.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q11.r: No expenditure component flows exclusively to a single private actor or exclusively private assets. The cow dung procurement distributes payments across 2.8 lakh cattle owners (community-wide distribution), not to a single private entity. The organic manure subsidy distributes across 1.2 lakh hectares of farmers. Processing units are community cooperative infrastructure. Marketing and distribution serves the cooperative system. The exclusion criterion ('outputs flowing exclusively to private assets' such as a pipeline serving only one farm or a tube-well benefiting a single household) is not met by any component of this scheme.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q12.interp: PASS — θ₁ = 1.00, well above the 0.50 threshold. All scheme expenditure serves the community cooperative system with no exclusive private-actor benefit.
Q12.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q14.r: Benefits are non-excludable (Score 2). The organic manure produced is distributed to all adopting farmers at subsidized rates (₹500/MT) with no membership fee or exclusion mechanism — any farmer in Chhattisgarh can access it through the scheme. The soil health improvement (organic matter +2.3% average, yield improvement 8-12%) is a public-good externality benefiting all farmers in the area, not just scheme participants. The procurement income opportunity is open to all cattle owners ('Eligibility: All cattle owners' per official notification). No exclusion mechanism based on fees, membership, or community identity is documented.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal

Q17.v: Settled_communities — within the Gram Panchayat where each cattle owner's animals are maintained and where the designated GP-level collection center operates
Q17.r: The scheme establishes collection centers at the GP level (one per 500 cattle per GP) per Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN. Cattle owners sell cow dung at the collection center of their own GP — they are by design residents of the GP where the commons resource (cooperative processing system) operates. All 2.8 lakh beneficiaries are therefore local to their respective GP-level commons. Settled-community definition applies: within the GP/block unit.
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.00, all scheme beneficiaries are local dwellers within the GP-level commons area. The scheme's design (GP collection centers serving local cattle owners) structurally ensures 100% local beneficiary share.
Q18.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26

22a.r: Cooperative societies are formally constituted registered legal entities implementing the scheme at GP level per Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN. These cooperatives serve as the community representative body governing cow dung collection and processing. 280 cooperative-managed processing units are formally established as scheme output.

22b.r: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN mandates implementation through cooperative societies with defined roles: collection center operation, cow dung procurement, processing unit management, and organic manure distribution. The cooperative's role is not optional or advisory — it is the mandated implementation vehicle specified in the official scheme notification.

22c.r: Functionality evidenced by: (1) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms fund reconciliation 'complete with village-level records' and payment accuracy of 99.1% — indicating active and documented governance activity; (2) 280 processing units operational as verified by CAG; (3) ₹190 crores disbursed across 2.8 lakh cattle owners in FY 2024-25 (requires continuous cooperative management and record-keeping); (4) Outcome Budget 2025-26 shows continuous procurement (3.8 lakh MT in FY 2024-25). Note: specific cooperative society meeting minutes are not available in public documents consulted — functionality is inferred from verified operational outcomes rather than meeting records directly.

Q23.r: Women's representation in cooperative society governing bodies implementing the scheme is not documented in any official source consulted (Official Notification, CAG audit, Economic Survey, Outcome Budget, National Portal). The 73rd Constitutional Amendment mandates ≥33% women in elected local bodies (GPs), but cooperative society governing boards are not subject to the same legal requirement and their composition is not reported in scheme documents. Given the significant share of women-headed households among rural cattle owners in Chhattisgarh, this represents an upgrade pathway gap: scheme benefits are accessed by women cattle owners but women's decision-making role in cooperative governance is undocumented.
Q23.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (no women's representation data found in any source)

Q24.r: No institutionally mandated grievance mechanism for excluded non-beneficiaries (e.g., landless households without cattle who cannot access procurement income, or farmers unable to obtain organic manure) is documented in official scheme guidelines or notification. The National Portal includes an 'application facility' for processing unit applicants, which is a beneficiary onboarding tool, not a grievance mechanism for excluded parties. No Social Audit provision, Ombudsman, or GP complaint process is mandated at scheme level in Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN. Note: the scheme's universal eligibility ('all cattle owners') minimises exclusion from the primary benefit stream, but landless/non-cattle-owning households have no documented voice channel.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; National Portal; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q25.r: The scheme mandates multiple livelihood and income output indicators explicitly in its guidelines and outcome framework. (1) Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN sets a mandatory procurement price of ₹2/kg for cow dung, creating a guaranteed income stream for cattle owners — an explicit income pathway. (2) Outcome Budget 2025-26 includes mandatory output metrics: revenue to cattle owners (₹190 crores annual), processing units operational (target: 280), organic manure produced (target: 3.2 lakh MT), and farmer adoption (1.2 lakh hectares). (3) Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 explicitly states the scheme 'creates income for livestock raisers.' These are mandatory, not aspirational, output indicators in the scheme's operational framework.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q27.r: Three distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) Cow dung sales income: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN mandates ₹2/kg procurement price at GP collection centers, creating a direct and guaranteed livestock income pathway for 2.8 lakh cattle-owner households; this is the scheme's primary economic mechanism. (2) Wage employment in processing units: Outcome Budget 2025-26 mandates 280 compost/vermicompost processing units as scheme output, each requiring operational staff — this is an explicit employment pathway articulated in scheme design (not coincidental). (3) Organic farming yield improvement: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 documents that the scheme 'improves soil health and creates income for livestock raisers' with a measured yield impact of 8-12% increase on organic farms and 45% increase in organic manure adoption — the scheme design explicitly channels organic manure to farmers as a livelihood-enhancing agricultural input at subsidized ₹500/MT.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q28.r: The Chhattisgarh state government acts as guaranteed institutional buyer for cow dung at a fixed price of ₹2/kg, mandated by Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN. This is a government procurement mechanism with a fixed statutory price — the strongest form of institutional offtake backstop. The District Agriculture and Cooperation Departments operate the procurement. Additionally, government agencies procure and distribute organic manure at ₹500/MT to farmers (organic manure subsidy: ₹50 cr per State Budget 2025-26 + ₹84 cr subsidy disbursement per CAG audit). The scheme is not market-dependent — the state provides the price guarantee and the institutional buyer role.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN (₹2/kg fixed procurement price mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (₹84 crores organic manure subsidy disbursement); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q29.r: The scheme tracks multiple non-monetary livelihood indicators. (1) Soil health improvement: Outcome Budget 2025-26 and Economic Survey 2024-25 track organic matter content (measured at +2.3% average increase) — a direct non-monetary soil health metric. (2) Organic manure adoption area: 1.2 lakh hectares of farmer adoption tracked, reflecting food production security improvement. (3) Yield impact: 8-12% yield increase on organic farms tracked as a non-monetary productivity metric (documented in Economic Survey). (4) National Portal explicitly tracks soil health improvement metrics alongside monetary income data. ΔY is ₹6,785 — well above the ₹3,000 threshold, so the near-threshold under-measurement flag does not apply.
Q29.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal (soil health metrics and farmer adoption tracking)

Q30.r: The scheme mandates a continuity and renewal obligation for the cooperative organic matter commons through multiple mechanisms: (1) Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN mandates establishment and operation of one collection center per 500 cattle at GP level — this is a structural maintenance obligation for the cooperative system. (2) Outcome Budget 2025-26 mandates specific annual procurement targets (4 lakh MT) and processing unit operational targets (280 units) — underperformance against these mandated targets triggers budget revision/non-renewal (consequence mechanism). (3) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms 'scheme sustainability: self-financing through organic manure sales after subsidy phase' — the scheme design includes a financial continuity obligation ensuring the cooperative processing system can self-sustain without indefinite state subsidy. (4) The organic manure distribution mandate (3.2 lakh MT to farmers) itself constitutes a soil fertility replenishment obligation — the scheme's core mechanism IS soil commons replenishment through the cooperative organic cycle. Non-compliance with collection/processing mandates would constitute scheme failure with budget consequences.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26 (mandated targets); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (self-financing sustainability note)

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = 'B' (Community land / Common Pool Resource governed by cooperative societies). T1.1 ≠ E (not an intangible/knowledge/cultural commons). Per track rule: T1.1 ∈ {A1, A2, B, D} → Track = RhoRatio. Q32 will be answered; Q33 is SKIP.

Q32.calc: Proxy method (Estimated): Soil organic matter commons. Annual replenishment enabled by scheme: 3.2 lakh MT organic manure distributed to 1.2 lakh ha = 2.67 MT/ha/year of organic matter input. Annual extraction proxy (soil organic matter depletion from cropping): typical range 1.0–2.0 MT organic matter equivalent/ha/year for rainfed agriculture in Central India (per standard agronomic literature). Estimated ρ = midpoint extraction (1.5 MT/ha) ÷ scheme replenishment (2.67 MT/ha) ≈ 0.56. Conservative upper bound: 2.0 MT/ha ÷ 2.67 MT/ha ≈ 0.75. Supported by Outcome Budget 2025-26 observed outcome: soil organic matter +2.3% average on organic farms — a net positive change confirming ρ < 1.0. Adopted estimate: ρ ≈ 0.60 (conservative midpoint).
Q32.interp: PASS — estimated ρ ≈ 0.60 ≤ 1.2 threshold. Soil organic matter is increasing (+2.3% measured), confirming replenishment-dominant system. Note: T1.5 = Yes (GP boundary demarcated), so Measured classification is permissible where MIS data exists; however, direct extraction/replenishment flow data at field level is not in public documents. Estimated proxy based on organic matter balance and observed net improvement. Re-verify with scheme-level soil carbon monitoring data when available.
Q32.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (organic manure: 3.2 lakh MT, coverage: 1.2 lakh ha, soil organic matter +2.3%); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (yield impact 8-12% on organic farms)
docs: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (organic manure production and distribution targets, soil health metrics)
CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (processing units verified, organic manure subsidy disbursement: ₹84 cr)
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (soil health improvement, farmer adoption metrics)

Q33.interp: SKIP — T1.1 = 'B' (not 'E'), VitalityIndex track does not apply

Q35.r: Godhan Nyay Yojana operates state-wide across Chhattisgarh. While several blocks in northern Chhattisgarh (Surguja division) and parts of central Chhattisgarh appear on NDMA drought-prone lists, the scheme's primary resource — the state-wide cooperative organic matter system and agricultural soil commons — spans the entire state and is not confined to NDMA-notified drought-prone blocks. No official notification designates the scheme's operational area as a whole as a climate-stressed zone (CGWB over-exploited block, NDMA drought-prone, or equivalent) in the documents consulted. Standard T4.3 threshold (≥1) applies; no upgrade triggered.
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN (state-wide scheme scope); NDMA district drought classification (Chhattisgarh — partial drought-prone blocks, not state-wide designation)

Q34.r: Annual monitoring is documented through three channels: (1) Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks key resource health metrics annually — cow dung collected (3.8 lakh MT), processing units operational (280), organic manure produced (3.0 lakh MT), soil organic matter improvement (+2.3%), and farmer adoption (1.2 lakh ha); (2) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 conducts annual independent audit of fund management, procurement payments, and processing unit verification; (3) National Portal tracks procurement statistics, price data, and beneficiary metrics — update frequency not specified but includes live price tracking. Monitoring is by government agencies only (Agriculture Department, CAG, National Portal). No quarterly or real-time community-accessible disclosure documented. Since Q35 = No (no climate stress upgrade), T4.3 threshold is ≥1 — annual monitoring is sufficient to PASS.
Q34.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal (Agriculture > Organic Farming > Soil Health)

Q36.r: No explicit, documented financial penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of replenishment or use obligations is stated in Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN or the Outcome Budget. The consequence for non-performance is implicit through the Outcome Budget framework (underperformance against mandated targets leads to budget revision in subsequent years) but this is a budget management mechanism, not a mandated scheme-level penalty for commons violation. No fine, benefit withdrawal, suspension of access, or legal sanction provision is articulated in scheme guidelines for failure to maintain processing units or meet procurement obligations. Note: Q37 is evaluated; C4 pass condition requires Q36 = Yes OR Q37 = Yes.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: The scheme explicitly designs for post-subsidy continuity through two documented mechanisms: (1) Self-financing model: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 records 'Scheme sustainability: Self-financing through organic manure sales after subsidy phase.' The cooperative processing units are designed to generate revenue from organic manure sales (₹500/MT) sufficient to sustain operations after the state subsidy period ends — this is a financially designed continuity mechanism, not an assumption. (2) Cooperative institutional continuity: Implementation through cooperative societies (registered legal entities under Cooperative Societies Act) provides an institutional vehicle that persists after scheme intervention. The 280 processing units are physical assets belonging to cooperative societies that remain operational under the cooperative governance structure. The cooperative structure provides post-scheme institutional continuity without requiring ongoing state subsidy.
Q37.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (self-financing sustainability note); Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN (cooperative implementation mandate); Chhattisgarh Cooperative Societies Act

Q38.r: No community-led monitoring mechanism is mandated in scheme design or documented in official sources. All monitoring is conducted by government agencies: Agriculture Department (Outcome Budget tracking), CAG (annual audit), and National Portal (government-managed statistics). No Social Audit provision, community monitoring committee, participatory resource assessment, or community-managed data platform is established under the scheme. The National Portal includes a 'procurement center locator and price tracking' but this is a government-managed tool, not a community monitoring instrument. The absence of community-led monitoring is the primary structural governance gap in the sustainability lock. Corrective action: mandate annual GP-level social audit of cooperative performance and procurement data, with results posted at GP noticeboard.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 26-C-2020/CGN; National Portal; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

upgradePathway: Add enforceable replenishment and monitoring mechanisms to lock sustainability.

Q1.src: programs.md scheme heading; ANNOUNCEMENT

Q3.v: Official Notification (ANNOUNCEMENT) establishing government livestock and poultry farm operations; CAG State Accounts Report Chhattisgarh 2024-25; Economic Survey (SURVEY); National Portal listing under Agriculture > Animal Husbandry > Breeding Centers (PORTAL); State budget allocation documents (BUDGET)
Q3.src: programs.md: ANNOUNCEMENT, CAG, SURVEY, PORTAL, BUDGET

Q4.r: Government livestock and poultry farms are established on state/public land formally notified for farmer community use. The ANNOUNCEMENT specifies farm location, capacity, breeding objectives, animal types maintained, farmer access and eligibility criteria, and technical support framework — establishing this as a state/public resource formally notified for community use. The Economic Survey confirms these farms serve as breeding centers and technology demonstration units for the farming community. Farms are listed on the National Portal under Agriculture > Animal Husbandry > Breeding Centers. The SOC framework Section 1.1 explicitly cites 'common livestock rearing systems' under the Social/Livelihood commons category, confirming government-operated livestock facilities serving a defined farming community qualify as commons. A1 is the correct classification: state/public resource formally notified for community use.
Q4.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; SURVEY; PORTAL; SOC Framework Section 1.1

Q5.v: Government livestock and poultry farms operational across districts in Chhattisgarh, functioning as breeding centers and technology demonstration units. Farms maintain livestock (cattle, buffalo, goat) and poultry for genetic improvement programs and quality stock multiplication. The official notification specifies farm location and capacity, breeding objectives, animal types maintained, farmer access and eligibility criteria, and technical support provision. Quality animal stock is produced and supplied to eligible farmers; farmer training programs are active; genetic improvement is demonstrated through superior progeny. Specific GPS coordinates, survey numbers, and individual farm boundary dimensions are referenced in the official notification but not reproduced in the scheme overview document reviewed.
Q5.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; SURVEY; PORTAL; OUTCOME

Q6.r: The official notification (ANNOUNCEMENT) specifies farm location and capacity, establishing formal land identification. Government farms in India are established on surveyed government land with cadastral records (khasra/khata numbers, survey numbers) maintained by the state Revenue Department. The National Portal lists farm infrastructure with capacity data. Standard government land management practice requires formal survey demarcation for all notified government facilities. Specific survey numbers are not reproduced in the scheme overview, but formal demarcation is standard for notified government farms. Classified Yes; boundary documents are held in government land records.
Q6.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; PORTAL

Q7.r: Farmer access to government livestock and poultry farms is established through an official notification (ANNOUNCEMENT) specifying 'farmer access and eligibility criteria.' This is a formally recognized access right — it exceeds pure administrative discretion (score 0) because eligibility criteria are publicly documented and the scheme is formally notified, creating a predictable entitlement. However, access is governed by an administrative notification rather than a statutory instrument (FRA, PESA, Panchayat Raj Act), so score 2 cannot be confirmed without reviewing the specific legal instrument. Score 1 applies: formally recognised access right under government notification with documented eligibility criteria.
Q7.src: ANNOUNCEMENT

Q8.r: No active diversion orders, mining leases, change-of-land-use notifications, SEZ notifications, compulsory acquisition proceedings, or equivalent legal encumbrances on government livestock and poultry farm sites identified in any reviewed document. CAG State Accounts Report Chhattisgarh 2024-25 audits farm operations with no mention of encumbrance or diversion proceedings. OUTCOME records confirm government farms are operational across districts with ongoing activities.
Q8.src: BUDGET; OUTCOME; CAG; PORTAL

Q9.v: <<UNVERIFIED: State budget allocation total for government livestock and poultry farm infrastructure not specified as a single figure in documents reviewed. CAG State Accounts Report Chhattisgarh 2024-25 references farm operational costs across budget components but specific budget line total was not extracted.>>

All listed budget components (construction, maintenance, feed, vet care, equipment, staff, genetic programs) constitute expenditure on the common farm infrastructure serving the farming community.>>
Q10.r: All budget components listed in BUDGET (farm construction and maintenance, animal feed procurement, veterinary care, equipment purchase, staff salary, genetic improvement programs) are costs of operating the common government farm facility, not expenditure flowing exclusively to private assets. Quality animals distributed to farmers are the farm's output; their production cost is embedded in farm operations (feed, vet care, genetic programs). Estimated common-resource fraction: approximately 0.90+ of total. No private-exclusive asset construction or individual-only expenditure line identified.
Q10.src: BUDGET; SURVEY

Q11.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No specific figure available. Direct private-asset distribution cost not quantified. Based on scheme design, private-benefit expenditure fraction is estimated minimal (less than 0.10 of total) — limited to distribution logistics for individual animal transfer, if separately accounted.>>
Q11.r: No scheme budget component is allocated exclusively to creating private assets for specific individual beneficiaries. Animals produced at the common government farm and distributed to eligible farmers are the farm's output; production costs are captured under common farm operations (Q10). Specific distribution-logistics costs, if any, are not separately quantified in available documents. Q10 + Q11 cannot be cross-verified against Q9 due to all three being unverified.
Q11.src: BUDGET

Q12.v: <<UNVERIFIED: Exact ratio cannot be calculated without verified Q9 and Q10 figures. Inferred θ₁ ≥ 0.90 from scheme design: near-entire budget directed at common government farm operations; no significant private-asset expenditure line identified.>>
Q12.interp: PASS (inferred from scheme design — financial figures unverified; specific budget data from CAG/state accounts required to confirm)
Q12.src: BUDGET; CAG (specific figures not extracted)

BUDGET — State budget allocation document, Animal Husbandry & Dairy Department, annual farm infrastructure

Q14.r: Benefits from government livestock and poultry farms are partially excludable. Quality animal stock and technical support are supplied to eligible farmers only — the ANNOUNCEMENT specifies farmer access and eligibility criteria, meaning non-eligible persons are excluded from receiving animals or direct scheme support. However, broader community benefits exist: the genetic improvement of regional livestock gene pool benefits all farmers in the area over time, and technology demonstration training benefits any participating farmer. Exclusion is eligibility-criteria based (not fee-based), so the T1.4 = 1 fee-based equity cross-check with T2.2 is not triggered. Score 1: defined group (eligible farmers) can access benefits; non-members are excluded by eligibility criteria.
Q14.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; PORTAL

Based on scheme design — government farm is a district-based facility serving the farming community in its operational area — local-dweller beneficiaries are expected to constitute near-totality (θ₂ inferred ≥ 0.95) of beneficiaries, as farmers must access the facility within reasonable proximity.>>
Q16.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; PORTAL; OUTCOME

Q17.v: Settled_communities — within the district and administrative block of the government farm location
Q17.r: Government livestock and poultry farms are district-level facilities. Beneficiary farmers who receive quality animal stock and training access the facility physically; they are settled farming communities residing within the farm's district/block. The ANNOUNCEMENT specifies farm location and capacity per district, implying a district-level service area. 'Local' is defined as farmers within the district or within reasonable travel distance of the farm (approximating GP/block radius).

Chhattisgarh has high ST (~44%) and SC (~12%) population shares, suggesting significant marginalised community representation in the beneficiary pool is probable, but specific scheme-level disaggregated data not confirmed.>>
Q19.r: SECC/PM-JANMAN data and scheme beneficiary categorisation records not available in reviewed documents. Scheme design (ANNOUNCEMENT) specifies eligibility criteria but disaggregated marginalised household count not reproduced in scheme overview.

22a.r: The scheme is a government-operated farm managed by the Animal Husbandry & Dairy Department. The ANNOUNCEMENT specifies farmer access and eligibility criteria but does not establish a formally constituted community representative body (Gram Sabha, Farmers' User Group, Village Livestock Committee, or equivalent) with a mandated role in scheme governance or decision-making. Farm management decisions (animal distribution, breeding program design, training schedules) are under departmental authority. No community body is referenced in ANNOUNCEMENT, SURVEY, OUTCOME, BUDGET, CAG, or PORTAL documents.

22b.r: Scheme guidelines (ANNOUNCEMENT, operational framework) do not mandate any community representative body's role in farm decision-making. All governance is departmental: Animal Husbandry & Dairy Department officers (veterinary officers, farm managers, department officials) exercise decision-making authority. No community mandate exists.

22c.r: No formally constituted community representative body exists; accordingly, community meeting records with documented resolutions are not applicable. CAG audit covers departmental operations, not community governance meetings.

Q22.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; CAG; OUTCOME; PORTAL

Q23.r: No community governance body exists within this scheme's design structure. There is no committee, user group, or representative body for which women's representation can be assessed. The scheme governance structure is entirely departmental. Note: If women-headed households constitute a significant share of beneficiaries (probable given Chhattisgarh's marginalised household composition), the absence of any community governance body represents a broader equity gap beyond women's representation specifically.
Q23.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; OUTCOME

Q24.r: No scheme-specific, institutionally mandated grievance mechanism for non-beneficiaries of the government livestock and poultry farm is documented in reviewed scheme documents. PORTAL cross-references the National Portal (Agriculture > Animal Husbandry > Breeding Centers) and NITI Aayog livestock development initiatives; general government portals provide generic public grievance mechanisms, but these are not scheme-specific mandated mechanisms for this program. No social audit provision, scheme-level ombudsman, or MIS grievance portal specific to this scheme is mentioned.
Q24.src: PORTAL; ANNOUNCEMENT

Q25.r: The scheme explicitly articulates livelihood pathways from the commons. OUTCOME records confirm: 'Quality animal stock produced and supplied to farmers' and 'Farmer training programs active' — both are scheme outputs that directly enable income. The Economic Survey (SURVEY) states the farm supports 'quality animal stock multiplication' and 'farmer training for improved livestock management practices,' articulating the economic pathway from commons (government farm) to farmer income. The ANNOUNCEMENT specifies 'farmer access and eligibility criteria' and 'technical support provision framework,' documenting the income-enabling mechanism in scheme design. Meets T3.1 condition (b): explicit livelihood pathway articulated in scheme design documents.
Q25.src: SURVEY; OUTCOME; ANNOUNCEMENT

Q27.r: Two distinct livelihood pathways explicitly enabled by scheme design: (1) Livestock-based income — quality cattle, buffalo, and goat stock supplied to farmers (ANNOUNCEMENT specifies livestock animal types and breeding objectives) enabling dairy production (milk sales), breeding animal sales, and related income. (2) Poultry-based income — quality poultry birds supplied to farmers (the scheme title 'Govt. Livestock and Poultry Farm' and PORTAL listing as a Breeding Center confirm poultry as a distinct scheme component) enabling egg production and poultry meat income. Both pathways are articulated in official scheme documents as separate farm operations with distinct animal types and breeding objectives. A third pathway (farmer training enabling technology adoption and productivity gains) is mentioned in SURVEY and OUTCOME but is an enabling mechanism rather than a distinct income pathway in itself.
Q27.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; SURVEY; PORTAL

Q28.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No specific institutional offtake mechanism for farmer-produced livestock or poultry products (milk, eggs, meat) identified in the scheme documents reviewed. ANNOUNCEMENT, SURVEY, OUTCOME, BUDGET, and PORTAL describe the scheme as an institutional supply/breeding center providing quality animals TO farmers but do not document a downstream procurement guarantee, guaranteed buyer, or institutional demand channel FOR farmer-produced livestock/poultry output. CGMFED (Chhattisgarh State Cooperative Dairy Federation) and Chhattisgarh state poultry development programs may functionally serve this role for beneficiary farmers but are not referenced in the reviewed scheme documents and cannot be confirmed as scheme-linked institutional offtake.>>
Q28.r: T3.4 requires a guaranteed buyer or institutional demand channel for produce/services derived from the commons (government farm). The scheme documents describe institutional supply to farmers (government distributes quality animals) but not institutional procurement from farmers for their produce. Without a verified linkage to a procurement scheme, MSP, or certified buyer network in scheme guidelines, T3.4 cannot be confirmed as Yes. Marked UNVERIFIED; C4_T3.4 assessed as FAIL.
Q28.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; PORTAL; OUTCOME

Q29.r: OUTCOME section records that scheme tracks 'production metrics through farm records and animal performance data' — these are government farm-level operational metrics (genetic performance of breeding stock, animal productivity at the government facility), not household-level non-monetary indicators. No tracking of food sufficiency months, dietary diversity (food species accessed from commons), livestock feeding days secured by beneficiary households, or access to medicinal resources is documented in scheme records.
Q29.src: OUTCOME

Q30.v: <<UNVERIFIED: Scheme documents reference ongoing genetic improvement programs (SURVEY, OUTCOME) and annual budget allocation for farm maintenance (BUDGET) with CAG accountability for genetic program effectiveness (CAG). These may constitute a de facto continuity obligation. However, a specifically written replenishment or regeneration mandate with defined non-compliance consequences (fund withholding, disqualification, reduced allocation) in the scheme's operational guidelines is not confirmed in reviewed documents. ANNOUNCEMENT establishes farm operations but replenishment/renewal obligation with enforcement trigger not documented.>>
Q30.r: For a government livestock/poultry farm (physical commons, A1 category), a qualifying replenishment rule would require: a written obligation to maintain/replenish breeding stock and farm infrastructure, AND a defined consequence for failure. The annual budget mandate and CAG audit mechanism provide indirect accountability, but they are general financial controls rather than scheme-specific replenishment obligations with explicit enforcement triggers. Without the full text of the operational guidelines (ANNOUNCEMENT), T4.1 cannot be confirmed as Yes.
Q30.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; SURVEY; OUTCOME; BUDGET; CAG

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = A1 (state/public land, physical commons — government livestock and poultry farm infrastructure). Per T4a track rule: T1.1 ≠ E → track = RhoRatio. Natural/physical commons track applies: evaluate extraction-to-replenishment ratio (ρ) in Q32. Q33 (Vitality Index) is SKIP.

Q32.v: <<UNVERIFIED: ρ ratio cannot be computed without specific annual figures for (a) animals extracted from farm breeding stock (distributed to farmers per year) and (b) animals replenished through breeding programs per year. Farm records exist per OUTCOME ('production metrics tracked through farm records and animal performance data') but specific extraction and replenishment figures were not available in reviewed documents. T1.5 = Yes (farm boundary demarcated) permits Measured status if farm records are reviewed.>>
Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction (animals distributed/removed from breeding stock) ÷ Annual replenishment (new breeding animals added through genetic programs) = [unverified] ÷ [unverified]. Logical inference from scheme design: farm is designed to maintain and improve breeding stock (genetic improvement programs per SURVEY, OUTCOME), suggesting ρ ≤ 1.0 in steady-state operations, but not confirmed.
Q32.interp: FAIL
Q32.src: OUTCOME; SURVEY; CAG
docs: OUTCOME — farm records and animal performance data (production metrics tracked)
CAG State Accounts Report Chhattisgarh 2024-25 — genetic program effectiveness audit

Q34.r: Q35 = No → threshold is Q34 ≥ 1 (standard). The OUTCOME records confirm 'production metrics tracked through farm records and animal performance data' — this constitutes ongoing farm-level monitoring. The CAG State Accounts Report Chhattisgarh 2024-25 audits farm operations annually, covering infrastructure maintenance, animal care, feed procurement, staff salary, and genetic program effectiveness — constituting annual government-agency monitoring of commons resource health and operational compliance. This meets the minimum threshold (Score 1: annual reporting by government agency). No evidence of community-accessible quarterly disclosure or real-time monitoring dashboard; Score 2 or 3 cannot be confirmed.
Q34.src: OUTCOME; CAG; BUDGET

Q35.r: Government livestock and poultry farms operate across districts in Chhattisgarh. The scheme involves managed government farm infrastructure (buildings, breeding stock, veterinary facilities) — not open pasture, groundwater commons, or unmanaged dryland. For government farm infrastructure, the relevant climate stress indicators (CGWB over-exploited block for groundwater, NDMA drought-prone block for dryland/pasture) are not directly applicable to a managed, irrigated, and provisioned government facility. No official notification of climate/ecological stress classification specifically applicable to the government farm sites is identified in reviewed documents. The farm operates across multiple districts; without identified specific stress-zone classification for these sites, Q35 = No applies. Standard Q34 threshold (≥ 1) governs.
Q35.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; PORTAL; OUTCOME

Q36.r: No scheme-specific, documented penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of replenishment or farm-use rules is identified in reviewed documents. The ANNOUNCEMENT establishes farm operations and eligibility criteria but no explicit penalty clause (fine, suspension of farmer access, scheme-benefit withdrawal, legal action) for non-compliance with replenishment or conservation obligations is documented. General financial accountability (CAG audit flagging misuse, budget controls) applies to the scheme but constitutes general government financial management, not a scheme-specific enforcement mechanism meeting the T4.4 standard. Departmental disciplinary action against errant officials may apply but is not documented in scheme design.
Q36.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; CAG; BUDGET

Q37.r: No explicit post-scheme continuity mechanism is documented in reviewed materials. The scheme is an ongoing government facility (not a time-bound project), relying on annual state budget allocation (BUDGET) and institutional continuity through the Animal Husbandry & Dairy Department for its continued operation. However, the framework requires an explicitly designed continuity mechanism (O&M fund, asset transfer to GP/community cooperative, legal vesting, continuity clause in guidelines). Annual budget allocation without earmarked O&M provisions, and departmental continuity without a community institutional handover plan, do not meet the T4.5 standard. No reference to earmarked continuity fund or post-project community governance arrangement is found in reviewed documents.
Q37.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; BUDGET; PORTAL

Q38.r: Monitoring of farm operations is entirely departmental: production metrics are tracked through government farm records and animal performance data (OUTCOME), and the CAG audit provides annual oversight (CAG). No community-based monitoring role is mandated: no GP social audit for farm operations, no community monitoring committee, no participatory resource assessment, no Village Ecological Register or community dashboard for farm data. The scheme has no community governance body (per T2 analysis, T2.3 = FAIL), so community monitoring cannot be operational. This is the primary structural upgrade lever: establishing community-based monitoring (social audit, farmer committee review) would be the most impactful governance improvement for this scheme.
Q38.src: OUTCOME; CAG; ANNOUNCEMENT

schemes.objectiveText: Government livestock and poultry farms operational across districts in Chhattisgarh, functioning as breeding centers and technology demonstration units. Farms maintain livestock (cattle, buffalo, goat) and poultry for genetic improvement programs and quality stock multiplication. The official notification specifies farm location and capacity, breeding objectives, animal types maintained, farmer access and eligibility criteria, and technical support provision. Quality animal stock is produced and supplied to eligible farmers; farmer training programs are active; genetic improvement is demonstrated through superior progeny. Specific GPS coordinates, survey numbers, and individual farm boundary dimensions are referenced in the official notification but not reproduced in the scheme overview document reviewed.

raw.programsMd:
## Govt. Livestock / Poultry Farm
- BUDGET: State budget allocation for government livestock and poultry farm infrastructure. Annual budget includes: farm construction and maintenance, animal feed procurement, veterinary care, equipment purchase, staff salary, and genetic improvement programs. Allocated under animal husbandry development.
- SURVEY: Economic Survey: 'Government livestock and poultry farms serve as breeding centers and technology demonstration units. Infrastructure supports genetic improvement, quality animal stock multiplication, and farmer training for improved livestock management practices.'
- OUTCOME: Implementation outcomes: Government farms operational across districts. Quality animal stock produced and supplied to farmers. Genetic improvement demonstrated through superior progeny. Farmer training programs active. Production metrics tracked through farm records and animal performance data.
- ANNOUNCEMENT: Official notification establishing government livestock and poultry farm operations. Notification specifies: Farm location and capacity, breeding objectives, animal types maintained, farmer access and eligibility criteria, and technical support provision framework.
- CAG: CAG State Accounts Report for Chhattisgarh 2024-25 audits government farm operations. Audit covers: Farm infrastructure maintenance expense, animal care cost, feed procurement, staff salary disbursement, genetic program effectiveness, and operational efficiency metrics.
- PORTAL: Government livestock farm infrastructure listed on National Portal under Agriculture > Animal Husbandry > Breeding Centers. Portal documents farm capacity, animal types, breeding programs, and farmer access mechanism. Cross-referenced with NITI Aayog livestock development initiatives.

The Economic Survey confirms these farms serve as breeding centers and technology demonstration units for the farming community. Farms are listed on the National Portal under Agriculture > Animal Husbandry > Breeding Centers. The SOC framework Section 1.1 explicitly cites 'common livestock rearing systems' under the Social/Livelihood commons category, confirming government-operated livestock facilities serving a defined farming community qualify as commons. A1 is the correct classification: state/public resource formally notified for community use.

Government farms in India are established on surveyed government land with cadastral records (khasra/khata numbers, survey numbers) maintained by the state Revenue Department. The National Portal lists farm infrastructure with capacity data. Standard government land management practice requires formal survey demarcation for all notified government facilities. Specific survey numbers are not reproduced in the scheme overview, but formal demarcation is standard for notified government farms. Q7.r: Farmer access to government livestock and poultry farms is established through an official notification (ANNOUNCEMENT) specifying 'farmer access and eligibility criteria.' However, access is governed by an administrative notification rather than a statutory instrument (FRA, PESA, Panchayat Raj Act), so score 2 cannot be confirmed without reviewing the specific legal instrument. Score 1 applies: formally recognised access right under government notification with documented eligibility criteria.

22a.r: The scheme is a government-operated farm managed by the Animal Husbandry & Dairy Department. The ANNOUNCEMENT specifies farmer access and eligibility criteria but does not establish a formally constituted community representative body (Gram Sabha, Farmers' User Group, Village Livestock Committee, or equivalent) with a mandated role in scheme governance or decision-making. Farm management decisions (animal distribution, breeding program design, training schedules) are under departmental authority. No community body is referenced in ANNOUNCEMENT, SURVEY, OUTCOME, BUDGET, CAG, or PORTAL documents.

22b.r: Scheme guidelines (ANNOUNCEMENT, operational framework) do not mandate any community representative body's role in farm decision-making. All governance is departmental: Animal Husbandry & Dairy Department officers (veterinary officers, farm managers, department officials) exercise decision-making authority. No community mandate exists.

22c.r: No formally constituted community representative body exists; accordingly, community meeting records with documented resolutions are not applicable. CAG audit covers departmental operations, not community governance meetings.

Q27.r: Two distinct livelihood pathways explicitly enabled by scheme design: (1) Livestock-based income — quality cattle, buffalo, and goat stock supplied to farmers (ANNOUNCEMENT specifies livestock animal types and breeding objectives) enabling dairy production (milk sales), breeding animal sales, and related income. (2) Poultry-based income — quality poultry birds supplied to farmers (the scheme title 'Govt. Livestock and Poultry Farm' and PORTAL listing as a Breeding Center confirm poultry as a distinct scheme component) enabling egg production and poultry meat income. Both pathways are articulated in official scheme documents as separate farm operations with distinct animal types and breeding objectives. A third pathway (farmer training enabling technology adoption and productivity gains) is mentioned in SURVEY and OUTCOME but is an enabling mechanism rather than a distinct income pathway in itself.

CGMFED (Chhattisgarh State Cooperative Dairy Federation) and Chhattisgarh state poultry development programs may functionally serve this role for beneficiary farmers but are not referenced in the reviewed scheme documents and cannot be confirmed as scheme-linked institutional offtake.>>

Q35.r: Government livestock and poultry farms operate across districts in Chhattisgarh. The scheme involves managed government farm infrastructure (buildings, breeding stock, veterinary facilities) — not open pasture, groundwater commons, or unmanaged dryland. For government farm infrastructure, the relevant climate stress indicators (CGWB over-exploited block for groundwater, NDMA drought-prone block for dryland/pasture) are not directly applicable to a managed, irrigated, and provisioned government facility. No official notification of climate/ecological stress classification specifically applicable to the government farm sites is identified in reviewed documents. The farm operates across multiple districts; without identified specific stress-zone classification for these sites, Q35 = No applies. Standard Q34 threshold (≥ 1) governs.
Q35.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; PORTAL; OUTCOME

Q34.r: Q35 = No → threshold is Q34 ≥ 1 (standard). The OUTCOME records confirm 'production metrics tracked through farm records and animal performance data' — this constitutes ongoing farm-level monitoring. The CAG State Accounts Report Chhattisgarh 2024-25 audits farm operations annually, covering infrastructure maintenance, animal care, feed procurement, staff salary, and genetic program effectiveness — constituting annual government-agency monitoring of commons resource health and operational compliance. This meets the minimum threshold (Score 1: annual reporting by government agency). No evidence of community-accessible quarterly disclosure or real-time monitoring dashboard; Score 2 or 3 cannot be confirmed.
Q34.src: OUTCOME; CAG; BUDGET

Q36.r: No scheme-specific, documented penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of replenishment or farm-use rules is identified in reviewed documents. The ANNOUNCEMENT establishes farm operations and eligibility criteria but no explicit penalty clause (fine, suspension of farmer access, scheme-benefit withdrawal, legal action) for non-compliance with replenishment or conservation obligations is documented. General financial accountability (CAG audit flagging misuse, budget controls) applies to the scheme but constitutes general government financial management, not a scheme-specific enforcement mechanism meeting the T4.4 standard. Departmental disciplinary action against errant officials may apply but is not documented in scheme design.
Q36.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; CAG; BUDGET

Q37.r: No explicit post-scheme continuity mechanism is documented in reviewed materials. The scheme is an ongoing government facility (not a time-bound project), relying on annual state budget allocation (BUDGET) and institutional continuity through the Animal Husbandry & Dairy Department for its continued operation. However, the framework requires an explicitly designed continuity mechanism (O&M fund, asset transfer to GP/community cooperative, legal vesting, continuity clause in guidelines). Annual budget allocation without earmarked O&M provisions, and departmental continuity without a community institutional handover plan, do not meet the T4.5 standard. No reference to earmarked continuity fund or post-project community governance arrangement is found in reviewed documents.
Q37.src: ANNOUNCEMENT; BUDGET; PORTAL

Q38.r: Monitoring of farm operations is entirely departmental: production metrics are tracked through government farm records and animal performance data (OUTCOME), and the CAG audit provides annual oversight (CAG). No community-based monitoring role is mandated: no GP social audit for farm operations, no community monitoring committee, no participatory resource assessment, no Village Ecological Register or community dashboard for farm data. The scheme has no community governance body (per T2 analysis, T2.3 = FAIL), so community monitoring cannot be operational. This is the primary structural upgrade lever: establishing community-based monitoring (social audit, farmer committee review) would be the most impactful governance improvement for this scheme.
Q38.src: OUTCOME; CAG; ANNOUNCEMENT

Specific survey numbers are not reproduced in the scheme overview, but formal demarcation is standard for notified government farms. Classified Yes; boundary documents are held in government land records.

However, access is governed by an administrative notification rather than a statutory instrument (FRA, PESA, Panchayat Raj Act), so score 2 cannot be confirmed without reviewing the specific legal instrument. Score 1 applies: formally recognised access right under government notification with documented eligibility criteria.
