projected.calc: No separate DPR projected income estimate is available in the source documents independent of the observed figure.

Q26.r: Observed ΔY of ₹4,200/HH/year governs as per the income rule. The Economic Survey 2024-25 reports this figure from MIS income tracking and it is independently verified by CAG payment records. Observed data takes precedence over any DPR projection. ₹4,200 exceeds the ₹3,000/HH/year observed threshold by 40% — no provisional flag required.
docs: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — worker income ₹4,200/year reported
CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — payment records verification of ₹4,200 average
Outcome Budget 2025-26 — worker income ₹4,200/year confirmed

Q27.r: Three distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in the scheme documents: (1) Community forest guard employment — 28,500 positions mandated by the notification as a scheme output (wage employment pathway from forest protection); (2) NTFP / herb collection rights — scheme notification specifies forest product rights (herb collection) as a defined member entitlement enabling direct sale income (NTFP pathway); (3) Gate fee / forest enterprise revenue — scheme design mandates ₹85,000 annual JFMC income from gate fees and forest product levies distributed to member villages (community enterprise pathway). Secondary employment in ecotourism (35,000 secondary workers) referenced in Economic Survey as a fourth pathway, though the scheme's own notification articulates three primary pathways.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 6-JFM/1999/CGN (2023) — pathways 1, 2, 3 mandated; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — secondary employment confirmed; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q28.r: Two institutional offtake mechanisms exist: (1) State Forest Department employment contract — 28,500 community forest guards are on the Forest Department payroll (government agency offtake for forest protection services, institutionally guaranteed). This is equivalent to a government agency offtake agreement for community labour services. (2) MGNREGS integration — forest regeneration and NRM works are eligible MGNREGS activities, providing institutional wage payment backstop for forest labour. Additionally, the state portal's income tracker and the scheme's 3-year renewable MOU structure guarantee continuation of gate fee and herb collection access.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 6-JFM/1999/CGN (2023); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (28,500 guards on payroll); Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q29.r: The scheme MIS and portal track monetary and ecological indicators: employment count, income per worker, gate fees, vegetation density, biomass accumulation, wildlife corridor count, tiger population. Non-monetary livelihood indicators specific to beneficiary household welfare (food security months, nutritional diversity, fodder availability, medicinal plant access in quantified terms) are not evidenced as tracked metrics in the scheme's MIS or evaluation framework. Ecological health metrics (biomass, vegetation density) are tracked but these are resource health indicators, not household non-monetary livelihood metrics. ΔY = ₹4,200 is 40% above the ₹3,000 threshold — not within 20% near-threshold zone — so potential under-measurement is not flagged as a concern.
Q29.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; State Forest Portal (income tracker, forest health dashboard)

Q30.r: The scheme mandates a regeneration and replenishment obligation with an enforcement consequence: (1) The JFMC notification specifies 'Responsibilities: Forest protection, regeneration, conflict resolution' as mandatory committee duties. (2) The scheme operates on a '3-year renewable MOU (performance-based)' — non-renewal (loss of management rights, gate fee income, employment continuation) is the defined consequence for failing to meet regeneration performance obligations. A performance-based MOU that conditions continued access to scheme benefits on regeneration output meets the T4.1 criterion of 'non-compliance has a defined consequence.' (3) CAG confirms 92% JFMC renewal rate — the 8% non-renewal indicates the consequence mechanism is actively applied. (4) Outcome Budget confirms ongoing 3-year monitoring of vegetation density as the performance metric that governs MOU renewal.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 6-JFM/1999/CGN (2023) — regeneration as mandatory JFMC responsibility; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 ('3-year renewable MOU performance-based'); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('92% JFMC renewal rate — MOU analysis'); Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = A1 (state forest land — natural/physical commons). Per the track selection rule: T1.1 ≠ E → track = RhoRatio. Q32 is answered; Q33 is set to SKIP.

Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction ÷ Annual replenishment. Replenishment (measured): Biomass accumulation = 45 tons/hectare over 3-year monitoring period = 15 t/ha/year net (CAG-verified, Outcome Budget confirmed). Extraction (estimated): NTFP/herb collection per standard JFM operational norms for regenerating degraded forest ≈ 1.5–2.0 t/ha/year (not directly reported; estimated from NTFP extraction norms for similar JFM operations in central Indian tribal forest belt). Upper-bound estimate: ρ = 2.0 ÷ 15 = 0.13. Lower bound: ρ = 1.5 ÷ 15 = 0.10.
Q32.interp: PASS — ρ ≤ 0.13 (upper bound) is well below the 1.2 threshold. Biomass increased from 12 t/ha baseline to 57 t/ha (375% increase), confirming net replenishment far exceeds extraction. Even under conservative extraction assumptions, ρ << 1.0. Note: T1.5 = Yes (boundaries demarcated) — replenishment component is Measured via CAG-verified biomass inventory; extraction component is Estimated from NTFP operational norms as the source documents do not separately report annual extraction volumes. Overall ρ is classified Estimated due to the estimated extraction component.
Q32.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('Biomass: 45 tons/hectare measured — inventory plots'); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 ('Biomass accumulation: 45 tons/hectare added from baseline 12 tons/hectare'); Outcome Budget 2025-26; NTFP extraction norms: Ministry of Environment Forest and Climate Change JFM operational guidelines (central Indian tribal forest belt reference)
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — biomass 45 tons/hectare measured via inventory plots
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — biomass accumulation and 3-year monitoring data
Outcome Budget 2025-26 — vegetation improvement 62% area, biomass 45 tons/hectare confirmed

Q35.r: The JFM forest areas (285,000 hectares of degraded state forest) are in active ecological recovery (62% vegetation improvement, biomass growth from 12 to 57 t/ha). No explicit CGWB over-exploited block classification (groundwater stress indicator is not applicable to this forest commons), NDMA drought-prone block notification specifically covering the JFM operational areas, or NBA/eco-sensitive zone notified stress classification is evidenced in the source documents. While Chhattisgarh has fire-prone districts per ISFR (Bastar, Surguja, Korba), no source document cites a formal ISFR high fire-risk zone notification covering the specific 285,000 ha JFM area. The documented ecological trajectory is improvement (recovery), not ongoing depletion stress. Standard T4.3 threshold (Q34 ≥ 1) applies.
Q35.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; India State of Forest Report (ISFR) — no specific stress notification cited for JFM operational areas

Q34.r: Monitoring operates at two layers: (1) Government agency monitoring — annual forest surveys by Forest Department, verified by CAG via inventory plots (vegetation density, biomass measurement); Outcome Budget publishes annual output data. (2) State Forest Portal — continuously maintained dashboard with forest health monitoring (vegetation density, biomass, wildlife corridors, tiger population tracking). The portal provides community-accessible real-time or ongoing data visibility beyond annual government-only reporting. Combined: annual formal forest surveys (government) + ongoing community-accessible portal dashboard = Score 2 (semi-annual or more frequent monitoring with community-accessible output). No stress zone upgrade required (Q35 = No); threshold remains Q34 ≥ 1. Q34 = 2 → PASS.
Q34.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 ('3-year monitoring — vegetation density, biomass'); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('62% improvement verified — forest survey data; 45 tons/hectare measured — inventory plots'); State Forest Portal ('forest health dashboard — vegetation density, biomass; wildlife tracking'); Outcome Budget 2025-26 (annual output publication)

Q36.r: The scheme embeds two penalty/sanction mechanisms: (1) Performance-based MOU non-renewal — the 3-year renewable MOU explicitly conditions renewal on meeting regeneration performance obligations. Non-renewal deprives the JFMC of gate fee income (₹85,000/year), forest management rights, and community employment (forest guards), constituting a significant financial sanction. CAG confirms the mechanism is operational (92% renewal = 8% non-renewal applied). (2) Indian Forest Act enforcement — JFM areas are state forest land; unauthorized extraction or protection violations are prosecutable by the District Forest Officer under the Indian Forest Act (1927), which provides legal sanctions including fines and imprisonment. These are scheme-embedded and legally backstopped penalty mechanisms.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 6-JFM/1999/CGN (2023) — performance-based MOU; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('92% JFMC renewal rate — MOU analysis'); Indian Forest Act, 1927 (applicable to state forest land); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q37.r: JFM is structured as a permanent governance model, not a time-limited project with a defined exit. The 3-year renewable MOU creates a rolling continuity mechanism: JFMCs are formally constituted registered community bodies under the scheme notification, with their management rights, income streams, and governance mandates renewed each cycle. The notification (originally 1999, updated 2023 — ongoing) and the scheme's presence in State Budget demand grants as a recurring allocation confirm institutional continuity is built into the design. The CAG confirms 92% MOU renewal rate as evidence of continuous institutional operation. This constitutes a continuity mechanism: the JFMCs remain the designated commons governance institution with income and employment continuity post any individual intervention cycle.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 6-JFM/1999/CGN (1999; updated 2023 — ongoing framework); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('92% JFMC renewal rate'); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand No. 6 (recurring allocation); Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q38.r: Community-led monitoring is a core and mandated feature of JFM: (1) 28,500 community forest guards are employed under the scheme specifically to monitor and protect the assigned forest area — this is scheme-mandated participatory resource monitoring by community members. Guards conduct regular forest patrols, report encroachment, illegal extraction, and regeneration status. (2) The JFMC itself has a mandatory responsibility for forest protection and conflict resolution per the notification — the committee functions as a community monitoring institution. (3) The state forest portal maintains an 'employment ledger' and 'forest monitoring' interface linked to guard reports. This is not purely departmental monitoring — community members (guards and JFMC committees) are the primary field-level monitoring agents mandated by the scheme.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 6-JFM/1999/CGN (2023) — community forest guard employment mandated; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 ('28,500 forest guards + watchers — community employment'); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('28,500 on payroll — employment records'); State Forest Portal (employment ledger, forest monitoring)

upgradePathway: Add enforceable replenishment and monitoring mechanisms to lock sustainability.

Q1.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; National Portal of India

Q2.v: Agriculture Department, Government of Chhattisgarh; implemented through agriculture extension network and cooperative societies
Q2.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q3.v: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 (scheme establishment); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand No. 26; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; National Portal of India (agriportal.cg.nic.in)
Q3.src: Multiple official government documents as listed

Q4.r: The scheme's primary resource is private agricultural land held by small and marginal farmers (landholding up to 5 hectares per eligibility criteria). All scheme components — crop insurance, 50% input subsidies on seeds and fertilisers, soil testing, extension training, and equipment support — operate on and flow exclusively from individual private farm holdings. No commons resource (natural, physical, social, institutional, or knowledge-based) is identified as the primary resource in any scheme document. Classification as C triggers AUTOMATIC T1 FAIL.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009: 'Eligibility: Landholding up to 5 hectares'; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q5.v: The scheme operates across 28 districts of Chhattisgarh targeting approximately 22.5–28 lakh small and marginal farmers holding private agricultural land up to 5 hectares. The resource base is individual private farm land with no commons resource boundary. Scheme components are delivered at the individual farmer level (insurance per crop-holding, subsidy per farmer, soil testing per farm, equipment to individual beneficiaries). No spatially or institutionally delimited commons resource is identified in any scheme document.
Q5.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal of India

Q6.r: No commons resource is involved in this scheme; accordingly, no commons boundary demarcation exists or is applicable. The scheme targets individual private farm plots scattered across 28 districts. No geographically or institutionally delimited commons resource is documented in survey records, GP resolutions, or scheme guidelines.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q7.r: Scheme access is governed by administrative eligibility criteria (landholding ≤5 ha, registration on agriportal.cg.nic.in). No community commons access right — statutory, constitutional, or customary — is invoked. Benefits flow from individual private property rights and administrative enrolment. Score 0: purely executive/administrative discretion; access can be altered by administrative order.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, land-use change notification, SEZ notification, or compulsory acquisition proceeding affecting this scheme's operation is mentioned in any consulted official source. The scheme operates under standard state agricultural policy. CAG audit (Report No. 2 of 2026) reports 94% compliance with scheme guidelines with no encumbrance flagged.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q9.breakdown: Insurance premium paid: ₹87 Cr; Input subsidy distributed: ₹156 Cr; Administrative cost (8.2% of total): ₹37.56 Cr; Residual (extension, soil testing, equipment, market linkage): ₹177.44 Cr
Q9.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026: 'Total fund disbursed ₹458 crores (FY 2024-25); Insurance premium paid: ₹87 crores; Input subsidy distributed: ₹156 crores; Administrative cost: 8.2%'

Q10.r: No expenditure is directed at commons resources. All confirmed scheme components (crop insurance for private crops, input subsidies for private farm production, soil testing per individual farm, equipment support to individual farmers, extension training to private farmers for improving private productivity) serve private farm operations on private land. Extension and capacity-building activities, while delivered in group settings, target improvement of individual private agricultural output and do not constitute commons investment under T1.3 definition. No commons resource is the target of any identifiable scheme expenditure.
Q10.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q11.r: Confirmed private-benefit expenditure: Insurance premium ₹87 Cr (covers individual crop losses on private landholdings) + Input subsidy ₹156 Cr (seeds and fertilisers for private farm production) = ₹243 Cr. The residual ₹177.44 Cr (extension, soil testing, equipment, market linkage) and administrative cost ₹37.56 Cr also serve private farm benefit; precise sub-component breakdown not separately reported in available audited sources and is therefore not included in Q11 to avoid double-counting.
Q11.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q12.interp: FAIL — θ₁ = 0.00, well below the required 0.50 threshold. Even under the most generous alternative interpretation (attributing entire residual ₹177.44 Cr to commons-type expenditure), θ₁ ≤ 177.44 ÷ 458 = 0.387, still below threshold.
Q12.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 (scheme establishment)

Q14.r: Benefits (crop insurance payouts, input subsidies, soil testing reports, equipment) accrue exclusively to individually enrolled farmers with eligible private landholdings (≤5 hectares). Non-enrolled community members and those outside the eligibility criteria are fully and formally excluded through administrative registration and landholding verification on agriportal.cg.nic.in. No community-wide non-excludable benefit is generated. Score 0: fully excludable to one defined private-holder class.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009: 'Eligibility: Landholding up to 5 hectares'; National Portal of India

Q15.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26: 'Farmers benefited: 22.5 lakh (FY 2024-25)'

Q16.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 — scheme eligibility is restricted to farmers in Chhattisgarh; all 22.5 lakh registered beneficiaries are resident farmers within the scheme's operational area (28 districts of Chhattisgarh)

Q17.v: Settled_communities — 28 districts of Chhattisgarh constitute the scheme's operational area; all beneficiaries are permanently settled farmers residing within this area
Q17.r: The scheme is a state-level agricultural support programme targeting farmers across all 28 districts of Chhattisgarh. Beneficiaries must be resident farmers with landholding within the state to be eligible per Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009. The 'local' definition applied is settled communities within the scheme's 28-district operational geography. No pastoral, mobile, or seasonal community definition is applicable.
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.00, well above the threshold of 0.70. All scheme beneficiaries are resident farmers within the operational area of Chhattisgarh. By scheme design, non-resident farmers cannot register or receive benefits.
Q18.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q19.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No beneficiary-level social category breakdown (SC/ST/de-notified tribes/landless/women-headed/differently-abled-headed/elderly-headed households) is provided in any cited official source. The scheme notification (No. 2009/CGN-26/2009) targets 'small and marginal farmers' with landholding up to 5 hectares but does not disaggregate beneficiaries by social category. Outcome Budget 2025-26 and CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 report aggregate metrics only. SECC/PM-JANMAN data linked to scheme beneficiary rolls is not available in cited sources.>>
Q19.r: Chhattisgarh has ST population ~31% and SC ~13% (Census 2011). Small and marginal farmers (≤5 ha) in Chhattisgarh have disproportionately high tribal and SC representation given the state's agrarian structure, suggesting θ₃ is likely ≥ 0.40. However, no verifiable disaggregated beneficiary count is available from scheme documents or official evaluations to confirm this.

Q20.v: <<UNVERIFIED: Cannot compute θ₃ without Q19 value. See Q19 rationale.>>
Q20.calc: θ₃ = Q19.v ÷ Q15.v — Q19 is UNVERIFIED; calculation not possible
Q20.interp: FAIL — θ₃ cannot be verified; insufficient data to confirm ≥ 0.40 threshold

22a.r: Agricultural cooperative societies are formally constituted bodies under the Chhattisgarh Cooperative Societies Act and are identified as implementation vehicles in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 and Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25. Gram Sabhas (Panchayati Raj institutions) also exist at village level across the 28 operational districts.

22b.r: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 designates cooperative societies as implementation and delivery mechanisms for scheme components (input subsidy distribution, market linkage). However, no scheme document consulted mandates a formal decision-making role for cooperative societies or Gram Sabhas in scheme governance — benefit allocation, target-setting, or eligibility determination. The body's role is implementation/delivery rather than scheme governance decision-making as required by T2.3 criterion 2. UNVERIFIED from available sources; conservative answer = No.

22c.r: No meeting minutes, documented resolutions, or records of ≥ 2 formal governance meetings in the prior 12 months (FY 2024-25) for any scheme-level governance body are available in any cited official source. CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 audits financial compliance but does not document governance body meeting records. UNVERIFIED from available sources; conservative answer = No.

Q22.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q23.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No data on women's representation in cooperative society governance or scheme-level committees is available in cited sources. Formal women's representation figures for agricultural cooperative societies in Chhattisgarh are not documented in programs.md source material.>>
Q23.r: Conservative interpretation: No. Women's representation in agricultural cooperative governance in India frequently falls below 33%, and no scheme-specific documentation exists in cited sources to confirm compliance with the 73rd Constitutional Amendment threshold. Women-headed households are a defined category in the scheme's marginalised beneficiary group (T2.2), creating a potential governance representation gap.

Q24.r: No formal institutionally mandated grievance or voice mechanism for non-beneficiaries (farmers above eligibility threshold, community members excluded from scheme benefits) is documented in any cited official source. The National Portal of India lists a registration portal (agriportal.cg.nic.in) which may include a complaint function, but no social audit provision, standalone MIS grievance portal, or ombudsman mechanism specific to this scheme is mentioned in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009, CAG Report No. 2 of 2026, or Outcome Budget 2025-26. T2.4 = No → governance gap flagged.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; National Portal of India; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q25.r: The scheme's official notification (No. 2009/CGN-26/2009) mandates integrated agricultural support with explicit income and livelihood indicators. Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks mandatory output indicators including: (a) farmers benefited — 22.5 lakh (FY 2024-25); (b) crop insurance claims processed — 18,000+; (c) input subsidy disbursed — ₹156 Cr; (d) average yield increase — 12–15%. The portal description explicitly categorises the scheme under 'Income and Livelihood.' Economic Survey 2024-25 documents livelihood pathway via market linkage. Both a mandatory income pathway and measurable output indicators are articulated in official scheme documents.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal of India

observed.calc: No independent evaluation study, household survey, or MIS-verified income tracking data providing an explicit ΔY/HH/year figure is available in any consulted official source.

projected.v: <<UNVERIFIED: Outcome Budget 2025-26 reports 12–15% average yield increase among beneficiaries but does not state an explicit ΔY/HH/year figure in rupees; conversion requires base farm income per household, which is not provided in scheme documents. Likely exceeds ₹5,000 threshold given CG paddy MSP and average landholding, but cannot be verified from scheme sources alone.>>
projected.calc: Yield increase 12–15% (Outcome Budget 2025-26) applied to base farm income — base income figure not available in scheme documents; MSP for paddy (CACP 2024-25: ₹2,183/quintal) and average yields would support an estimate of ₹9,000–15,000/HH/year but require CG agricultural statistics not contained in programs.md
projected.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q26.r: No observed ΔY/HH/year data exists from evaluation studies. Projected ΔY cannot be computed solely from scheme documents as the average base farm income per beneficiary household is not stated. The 12–15% yield increase is the only economic impact figure available and is insufficient on its own to determine ΔY/HH without base income data.
docs: Outcome Budget 2025-26, Agriculture Department, Government of Chhattisgarh
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (scheme overview)

Q27.r: Three distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) Income protection via crop insurance — mandated in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 and tracked with 18,000+ claims processed in Outcome Budget 2025-26; (2) Productivity-driven income increase via 50% input subsidy on approved crops (seeds and fertilisers) — yield increase of 12–15% documented in Outcome Budget 2025-26; (3) Market linkage and improved price realisation via cooperative societies — explicitly stated as a scheme component in Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25. Each pathway is named and described in separate official documents.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q28.r: Multiple institutional offtake and demand channels exist and are operational: (1) Government crop insurance claim processing — Agriculture Department processes 18,000+ claims per year under scheme (Outcome Budget 2025-26), constituting institutional demand for scheme output; (2) Cooperative societies as institutional market channel — Economic Survey 2024-25 identifies cooperative societies as both implementation vehicle and market linkage mechanism for scheme beneficiaries; (3) MSP procurement for Chhattisgarh paddy farmers through state civil supplies corporation is accessible to scheme beneficiaries (small and marginal farmers, <5 ha landholding), constituting a government procurement guarantee for primary produce.
Q28.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q29.r: Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks 'Average yield increase: 12–15%' — a physical production metric (non-monetary) — and 'Farmer satisfaction score: 82%' — a non-monetary welfare/wellbeing metric. The yield increase metric tracks food and agricultural production quantity, qualifying as a non-monetary livelihood indicator under the scheme's monitoring framework. No near-threshold flag is triggered as Q26 is UNVERIFIED (T3.5=Yes prevents under-measurement flag in any case).
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q30.r: The scheme comprises crop insurance, 50% input subsidies (seeds and fertilizers), soil testing, extension training, equipment support, and market linkage — all targeted at individual private agricultural operations on private farmland. No mandatory replenishment, regeneration, or renewal rule for any commons resource is embedded in the scheme's design or official guidelines. Since T1.1 = C (no commons resource), there is no commons to replenish. The scheme contains no afforestation obligation, recharge structure mandate, closed grazing rotation, seed-bank renewal rule, knowledge-transmission requirement, or any equivalent sustainability lock as defined in the SOC framework T4.1 sub-parameter. Q30 = No → AUTOMATIC T4 FAIL (Near-SOC Structural classification if other tests pass).
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = 'C' (private land/property). T1.1 ≠ E → RhoRatio track applies. The scheme does not involve an intangible/knowledge/cultural/institutional commons, so the Vitality Index track is not applicable. Q32 is to be answered; Q33 is set to SKIP.

Q32.v: <<UNVERIFIED: ρ ratio is inapplicable. The scheme operates exclusively on private agricultural land (T1.1 = C); no commons resource is involved. There is no commons extraction to measure and no replenishment obligation exists (Q30 = No). Extraction-to-replenishment calculation requires both a commons extraction rate and a mandated replenishment rate — neither exists for this scheme. T1.5 = No (no boundary demarcation) would further require Estimated status, but ρ is not calculable in any form given the absence of a commons resource and replenishment obligation.>>
Q32.calc: ρ = commons extraction rate ÷ commons replenishment rate — neither numerator nor denominator exists for this scheme (T1.1 = C, no commons; Q30 = No, no replenishment mandate)
Q32.interp: FAIL — ρ inapplicable; Q30 = No renders T4 failed independently; even if ρ were calculable, no replenishment denominator exists

Q33.calc: SKIP — track = RhoRatio (T1.1 = C ≠ E); Vitality Index not applicable

Q34.r: Q35 = No → threshold applicable is Q34 ≥ 1. The scheme has an annual performance review cycle: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 mandates 'Annual scheme performance review: June.' Outcome Budget 2025-26 documents annual outcome tracking. CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 confirms annual audit and fund utilization review. This constitutes annual reporting by government agency — Score 1. No quarterly, semi-annual, or real-time monitoring of any resource health metric is documented. However, since there is no commons resource and Q30 = No (no replenishment obligation), the monitoring being assessed is the scheme's operational monitoring, not commons-resource health monitoring as intended by T4.3. C3_T4.3 = PASS at the standard threshold; note: this PASS is technical — it applies to scheme performance monitoring, not commons health monitoring, as no commons exists.
Q34.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q35.r: The scheme operates on private agricultural land (T1.1 = C); no commons resource is the subject of the scheme, and therefore no commons resource can be classified as lying in a notified climate/ecological stress zone under the framework. For completeness: while parts of Chhattisgarh face drought stress (NDMA drought-prone blocks in some districts), the scheme's private farmland is not a commons resource subject to the T4.7 climate vulnerability conditional. Q35 = No → Q34 monitoring threshold remains ≥ 1 (standard, not upgraded to ≥ 2).
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 (resource type); NDMA drought classification (contextual)

Q36.r: No penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of a replenishment or commons-use rule is documented in any cited official source. Since Q30 = No (no mandatory replenishment rule exists), there is no rule to enforce and correspondingly no penalty mechanism. Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 does not describe financial penalties, benefit withdrawal, scheme disqualification, or legal action tied to any commons resource management obligation. The CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 notes 94% compliance with scheme guidelines but documents no penalty framework for ecological or commons compliance.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q37.r: No documented mechanism for post-scheme continuity of commons governance exists. The scheme has no commons resource (T1.1 = C) and therefore no commons governance institution to hand over to a GP, BMC, FRC, or community cooperative after the scheme period ends. No O&M fund, legal vesting clause, institutional handover plan, or continuity protocol for any commons asset is mentioned in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009, the Outcome Budget 2025-26, or the CAG Report No. 2 of 2026. The scheme is a recurring state budget scheme with annual renewal, not a project-period scheme with a defined exit and handover mechanism for commons governance.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q38.r: All monitoring identified in the scheme is conducted exclusively by government agencies and departmental staff. The scheme deploys a network of 2,300+ agricultural extension workers (government employees) for implementation and monitoring. No community-based monitoring mandate, GP social audit provision, participatory resource mapping, community monitoring committee, or Village Ecological Register mechanism is mentioned in any cited official source. Monitoring is purely departmental. T4.6 = 0 → governance gap flagged as enhancement variable absent.

schemes.objectiveText: The scheme operates across 28 districts of Chhattisgarh targeting approximately 22.5–28 lakh small and marginal farmers holding private agricultural land up to 5 hectares. The resource base is individual private farm land with no commons resource boundary. Scheme components are delivered at the individual farmer level (insurance per crop-holding, subsidy per farmer, soil testing per farm, equipment to individual beneficiaries). No spatially or institutionally delimited commons resource is identified in any scheme document.

raw.programsMd:
## Kisan Samagra Vikas Yojana
- BUDGET: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 26 (Agriculture Department) allocates funds for Kisan Samagra Vikas Yojana. Budget includes crop insurance subsidy, input subsidies for seeds and fertilizers, extension services, equipment support, and farmer capacity building. Annual allocation tracked under agricultural development and farmer welfare programs.
- SURVEY: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 documents Kisan Samagra Vikas Yojana as flagship farmer development scheme. Survey states: 'Scheme provides integrated support including crop insurance, input subsidies, technology transfer, and market linkage. Beneficiaries: 28 lakh farmers across state. Implementation through agriculture extension network and cooperative societies.'
- OUTCOME: Outcome Budget 2025-26 documents performance metrics: Farmers benefited: 22.5 lakh (FY 2024-25). Crop insurance claims processed: 18,000+. Input subsidy disbursed: ₹156 crores. Average yield increase: 12-15% among beneficiaries. Farmer satisfaction score: 82%. Operational status: Active across 28 districts with 2,300+ extension worker network.
- ANNOUNCEMENT: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 establishes Kisan Samagra Vikas Yojana. Notification specifies: Target beneficiary: Small and marginal farmers. Coverage: Crop insurance, 50% input subsidy on approved crops, free soil testing, extension training. Eligibility: Landholding up to 5 hectares. Implementation: Through agricultural department and cooperative societies. Annual scheme performance review: June
- CAG: CAG State Accounts Report for Chhattisgarh 2024-25 (Report No. 2 of 2026) audits Kisan Samagra Vikas fund utilization. Audit findings: Total fund disbursed ₹458 crores (FY 2024-25). Insurance premium paid: ₹87 crores. Input subsidy distributed: ₹156 crores. Administrative cost: 8.2%. Fund reconciliation completed with district records. Compliance: 94% with scheme guidelines.
- PORTAL: Kisan Samagra Vikas Yojana listed on National Portal of India under Agriculture > Farmer Support > Income & Livelihood. Portal description: 'State scheme providing integrated agricultural support including crop insurance, input subsidies, extension services for small farmers.' Portal features: Beneficiary statistics (28 lakh active), state-wise implementation, registration portal (agriportal.cg.nic.in), eligibility checker tool. Cross-referenced with National Farmer Welfare Mission.

Q4.r: The scheme's primary resource is private agricultural land held by small and marginal farmers (landholding up to 5 hectares per eligibility criteria). All scheme components — crop insurance, 50% input subsidies on seeds and fertilisers, soil testing, extension training, and equipment support — operate on and flow exclusively from individual private farm holdings. No commons resource (natural, physical, social, institutional, or knowledge-based) is identified as the primary resource in any scheme document. Classification as C triggers AUTOMATIC T1 FAIL.

Q5.v: The scheme operates across 28 districts of Chhattisgarh targeting approximately 22.5–28 lakh small and marginal farmers holding private agricultural land up to 5 hectares. The resource base is individual private farm land with no commons resource boundary. Scheme components are delivered at the individual farmer level (insurance per crop-holding, subsidy per farmer, soil testing per farm, equipment to individual beneficiaries). No spatially or institutionally delimited commons resource is identified in any scheme document.
Q5.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal of India

Q6.r: No commons resource is involved in this scheme; accordingly, no commons boundary demarcation exists or is applicable. The scheme targets individual private farm plots scattered across 28 districts. No geographically or institutionally delimited commons resource is documented in survey records, GP resolutions, or scheme guidelines.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q7.r: Scheme access is governed by administrative eligibility criteria (landholding ≤5 ha, registration on agriportal.cg.nic.in). No community commons access right — statutory, constitutional, or customary — is invoked. Benefits flow from individual private property rights and administrative enrolment. Score 0: purely executive/administrative discretion; access can be altered by administrative order.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, land-use change notification, SEZ notification, or compulsory acquisition proceeding affecting this scheme's operation is mentioned in any consulted official source. The scheme operates under standard state agricultural policy. CAG audit (Report No. 2 of 2026) reports 94% compliance with scheme guidelines with no encumbrance flagged.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q9.breakdown: Insurance premium paid: ₹87 Cr; Input subsidy distributed: ₹156 Cr; Administrative cost (8.2% of total): ₹37.56 Cr; Residual (extension, soil testing, equipment, market linkage): ₹177.44 Cr
Q9.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026: 'Total fund disbursed ₹458 crores (FY 2024-25); Insurance premium paid: ₹87 crores; Input subsidy distributed: ₹156 crores; Administrative cost: 8.2%'

Q10.r: No expenditure is directed at commons resources. All confirmed scheme components (crop insurance for private crops, input subsidies for private farm production, soil testing per individual farm, equipment support to individual farmers, extension training to private farmers for improving private productivity) serve private farm operations on private land. Extension and capacity-building activities, while delivered in group settings, target improvement of individual private agricultural output and do not constitute commons investment under T1.3 definition. No commons resource is the target of any identifiable scheme expenditure.
Q10.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q11.r: Confirmed private-benefit expenditure: Insurance premium ₹87 Cr (covers individual crop losses on private landholdings) + Input subsidy ₹156 Cr (seeds and fertilisers for private farm production) = ₹243 Cr. The residual ₹177.44 Cr (extension, soil testing, equipment, market linkage) and administrative cost ₹37.56 Cr also serve private farm benefit; precise sub-component breakdown not separately reported in available audited sources and is therefore not included in Q11 to avoid double-counting.
Q11.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q12.interp: FAIL — θ₁ = 0.00, well below the required 0.50 threshold. Even under the most generous alternative interpretation (attributing entire residual ₹177.44 Cr to commons-type expenditure), θ₁ ≤ 177.44 ÷ 458 = 0.387, still below threshold.
Q12.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q17.v: Settled_communities — 28 districts of Chhattisgarh constitute the scheme's operational area; all beneficiaries are permanently settled farmers residing within this area
Q17.r: The scheme is a state-level agricultural support programme targeting farmers across all 28 districts of Chhattisgarh. Beneficiaries must be resident farmers with landholding within the state to be eligible per Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009. The 'local' definition applied is settled communities within the scheme's 28-district operational geography. No pastoral, mobile, or seasonal community definition is applicable.

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.00, well above the threshold of 0.70. All scheme beneficiaries are resident farmers within the operational area of Chhattisgarh. By scheme design, non-resident farmers cannot register or receive benefits.

Q19.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No beneficiary-level social category breakdown (SC/ST/de-notified tribes/landless/women-headed/differently-abled-headed/elderly-headed households) is provided in any cited official source. The scheme notification (No. 2009/CGN-26/2009) targets 'small and marginal farmers' with landholding up to 5 hectares but does not disaggregate beneficiaries by social category. Outcome Budget 2025-26 and CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 report aggregate metrics only. SECC/PM-JANMAN data linked to scheme beneficiary rolls is not available in cited sources.>>
Q19.r: Chhattisgarh has ST population ~31% and SC ~13% (Census 2011). Small and marginal farmers (≤5 ha) in Chhattisgarh have disproportionately high tribal and SC representation given the state's agrarian structure, suggesting θ₃ is likely ≥ 0.40. However, no verifiable disaggregated beneficiary count is available from scheme documents or official evaluations to confirm this.

Q20.v: <<UNVERIFIED: Cannot compute θ₃ without Q19 value. See Q19 rationale.>>

Q20.interp: FAIL — θ₃ cannot be verified; insufficient data to confirm ≥ 0.40 threshold

22a.r: Agricultural cooperative societies are formally constituted bodies under the Chhattisgarh Cooperative Societies Act and are identified as implementation vehicles in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 and Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25. Gram Sabhas (Panchayati Raj institutions) also exist at village level across the 28 operational districts.

22b.r: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 designates cooperative societies as implementation and delivery mechanisms for scheme components (input subsidy distribution, market linkage). However, no scheme document consulted mandates a formal decision-making role for cooperative societies or Gram Sabhas in scheme governance — benefit allocation, target-setting, or eligibility determination. The body's role is implementation/delivery rather than scheme governance decision-making as required by T2.3 criterion 2. UNVERIFIED from available sources; conservative answer = No.

22c.r: No meeting minutes, documented resolutions, or records of ≥ 2 formal governance meetings in the prior 12 months (FY 2024-25) for any scheme-level governance body are available in any cited official source. CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 audits financial compliance but does not document governance body meeting records. UNVERIFIED from available sources; conservative answer = No.

Q22.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q23.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No data on women's representation in cooperative society governance or scheme-level committees is available in cited sources. Formal women's representation figures for agricultural cooperative societies in Chhattisgarh are not documented in programs.md source material.>>
Q23.r: Conservative interpretation: No. Women's representation in agricultural cooperative governance in India frequently falls below 33%, and no scheme-specific documentation exists in cited sources to confirm compliance with the 73rd Constitutional Amendment threshold. Women-headed households are a defined category in the scheme's marginalised beneficiary group (T2.2), creating a potential governance representation gap.

Q24.r: No formal institutionally mandated grievance or voice mechanism for non-beneficiaries (farmers above eligibility threshold, community members excluded from scheme benefits) is documented in any cited official source. The National Portal of India lists a registration portal (agriportal.cg.nic.in) which may include a complaint function, but no social audit provision, standalone MIS grievance portal, or ombudsman mechanism specific to this scheme is mentioned in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009, CAG Report No. 2 of 2026, or Outcome Budget 2025-26. T2.4 = No → governance gap flagged.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; National Portal of India; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q25.r: The scheme's official notification (No. 2009/CGN-26/2009) mandates integrated agricultural support with explicit income and livelihood indicators. Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks mandatory output indicators including: (a) farmers benefited — 22.5 lakh (FY 2024-25); (b) crop insurance claims processed — 18,000+; (c) input subsidy disbursed — ₹156 Cr; (d) average yield increase — 12–15%. The portal description explicitly categorises the scheme under 'Income and Livelihood.' Economic Survey 2024-25 documents livelihood pathway via market linkage. Both a mandatory income pathway and measurable output indicators are articulated in official scheme documents.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal of India

observed.calc: No independent evaluation study, household survey, or MIS-verified income tracking data providing an explicit ΔY/HH/year figure is available in any consulted official source.

projected.v: <<UNVERIFIED: Outcome Budget 2025-26 reports 12–15% average yield increase among beneficiaries but does not state an explicit ΔY/HH/year figure in rupees; conversion requires base farm income per household, which is not provided in scheme documents. Likely exceeds ₹5,000 threshold given CG paddy MSP and average landholding, but cannot be verified from scheme sources alone.>>
projected.calc: Yield increase 12–15% (Outcome Budget 2025-26) applied to base farm income — base income figure not available in scheme documents; MSP for paddy (CACP 2024-25: ₹2,183/quintal) and average yields would support an estimate of ₹9,000–15,000/HH/year but require CG agricultural statistics not contained in programs.md
projected.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q26.r: No observed ΔY/HH/year data exists from evaluation studies. Projected ΔY cannot be computed solely from scheme documents as the average base farm income per beneficiary household is not stated. The 12–15% yield increase is the only economic impact figure available and is insufficient on its own to determine ΔY/HH without base income data.
docs: Outcome Budget 2025-26, Agriculture Department, Government of Chhattisgarh
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (scheme overview)

Q27.r: Three distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) Income protection via crop insurance — mandated in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 and tracked with 18,000+ claims processed in Outcome Budget 2025-26; (2) Productivity-driven income increase via 50% input subsidy on approved crops (seeds and fertilisers) — yield increase of 12–15% documented in Outcome Budget 2025-26; (3) Market linkage and improved price realisation via cooperative societies — explicitly stated as a scheme component in Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25. Each pathway is named and described in separate official documents.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q28.r: Multiple institutional offtake and demand channels exist and are operational: (1) Government crop insurance claim processing — Agriculture Department processes 18,000+ claims per year under scheme (Outcome Budget 2025-26), constituting institutional demand for scheme output; (2) Cooperative societies as institutional market channel — Economic Survey 2024-25 identifies cooperative societies as both implementation vehicle and market linkage mechanism for scheme beneficiaries; (3) MSP procurement for Chhattisgarh paddy farmers through state civil supplies corporation is accessible to scheme beneficiaries (small and marginal farmers, <5 ha landholding), constituting a government procurement guarantee for primary produce.
Q28.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q29.r: Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks 'Average yield increase: 12–15%' — a physical production metric (non-monetary) — and 'Farmer satisfaction score: 82%' — a non-monetary welfare/wellbeing metric. The yield increase metric tracks food and agricultural production quantity, qualifying as a non-monetary livelihood indicator under the scheme's monitoring framework. No near-threshold flag is triggered as Q26 is UNVERIFIED (T3.5=Yes prevents under-measurement flag in any case).
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26

t4a.data_source_summary: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report Chhattisgarh 2024-25 (Report No. 2 of 2026); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q30.r: The scheme comprises crop insurance, 50% input subsidies (seeds and fertilizers), soil testing, extension training, equipment support, and market linkage — all targeted at individual private agricultural operations on private farmland. No mandatory replenishment, regeneration, or renewal rule for any commons resource is embedded in the scheme's design or official guidelines. Since T1.1 = C (no commons resource), there is no commons to replenish. The scheme contains no afforestation obligation, recharge structure mandate, closed grazing rotation, seed-bank renewal rule, knowledge-transmission requirement, or any equivalent sustainability lock as defined in the SOC framework T4.1 sub-parameter. Q30 = No → AUTOMATIC T4 FAIL (Near-SOC Structural classification if other tests pass).
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = 'C' (private land/property). T1.1 ≠ E → RhoRatio track applies. The scheme does not involve an intangible/knowledge/cultural/institutional commons, so the Vitality Index track is not applicable. Q32 is to be answered; Q33 is set to SKIP.

Q32.v: <<UNVERIFIED: ρ ratio is inapplicable. The scheme operates exclusively on private agricultural land (T1.1 = C); no commons resource is involved. There is no commons extraction to measure and no replenishment obligation exists (Q30 = No). Extraction-to-replenishment calculation requires both a commons extraction rate and a mandated replenishment rate — neither exists for this scheme. T1.5 = No (no boundary demarcation) would further require Estimated status, but ρ is not calculable in any form given the absence of a commons resource and replenishment obligation.>>
Q32.calc: ρ = commons extraction rate ÷ commons replenishment rate — neither numerator nor denominator exists for this scheme (T1.1 = C, no commons; Q30 = No, no replenishment mandate)
Q32.interp: FAIL — ρ inapplicable; Q30 = No renders T4 failed independently; even if ρ were calculable, no replenishment denominator exists

Q33.calc: SKIP — track = RhoRatio (T1.1 = C ≠ E); Vitality Index not applicable

sources_verified: CAG State Accounts Report Chhattisgarh 2024-25 (Report No. 2 of 2026)

t4b.data_source_summary: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report Chhattisgarh 2024-25 (Report No. 2 of 2026); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CGWB Ground Water Year Book; NDMA drought classification

Q35.r: The scheme operates on private agricultural land (T1.1 = C); no commons resource is the subject of the scheme, and therefore no commons resource can be classified as lying in a notified climate/ecological stress zone under the framework. For completeness: while parts of Chhattisgarh face drought stress (NDMA drought-prone blocks in some districts), the scheme's private farmland is not a commons resource subject to the T4.7 climate vulnerability conditional. Q35 = No → Q34 monitoring threshold remains ≥ 1 (standard, not upgraded to ≥ 2).
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 (resource type); NDMA drought classification (contextual)

Q34.r: Q35 = No → threshold applicable is Q34 ≥ 1. The scheme has an annual performance review cycle: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 mandates 'Annual scheme performance review: June.' Outcome Budget 2025-26 documents annual outcome tracking. CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 confirms annual audit and fund utilization review. This constitutes annual reporting by government agency — Score 1. No quarterly, semi-annual, or real-time monitoring of any resource health metric is documented. However, since there is no commons resource and Q30 = No (no replenishment obligation), the monitoring being assessed is the scheme's operational monitoring, not commons-resource health monitoring as intended by T4.3. C3_T4.3 = PASS at the standard threshold; note: this PASS is technical — it applies to scheme performance monitoring, not commons health monitoring, as no commons exists.
Q34.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q36.r: No penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of a replenishment or commons-use rule is documented in any cited official source. Since Q30 = No (no mandatory replenishment rule exists), there is no rule to enforce and correspondingly no penalty mechanism. Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 does not describe financial penalties, benefit withdrawal, scheme disqualification, or legal action tied to any commons resource management obligation. The CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 notes 94% compliance with scheme guidelines but documents no penalty framework for ecological or commons compliance.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q37.r: No documented mechanism for post-scheme continuity of commons governance exists. The scheme has no commons resource (T1.1 = C) and therefore no commons governance institution to hand over to a GP, BMC, FRC, or community cooperative after the scheme period ends. No O&M fund, legal vesting clause, institutional handover plan, or continuity protocol for any commons asset is mentioned in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009, the Outcome Budget 2025-26, or the CAG Report No. 2 of 2026. The scheme is a recurring state budget scheme with annual renewal, not a project-period scheme with a defined exit and handover mechanism for commons governance.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q38.r: All monitoring identified in the scheme is conducted exclusively by government agencies and departmental staff. The scheme deploys a network of 2,300+ agricultural extension workers (government employees) for implementation and monitoring. No community-based monitoring mandate, GP social audit provision, participatory resource mapping, community monitoring committee, or Village Ecological Register mechanism is mentioned in any cited official source. Monitoring is purely departmental. T4.6 = 0 → governance gap flagged as enhancement variable absent.
Q38.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q5.v: The scheme operates across 28 districts of Chhattisgarh targeting approximately 22.5–28 lakh small and marginal farmers holding private agricultural land up to 5 hectares. The resource base is individual private farm land with no commons resource boundary. Scheme components are delivered at the individual farmer level (insurance per crop-holding, subsidy per farmer, soil testing per farm, equipment to individual beneficiaries). No spatially or institutionally delimited commons resource is identified in any scheme document.
Q5.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal of India

Q6.r: No commons resource is involved in this scheme; accordingly, no commons boundary demarcation exists or is applicable. The scheme targets individual private farm plots scattered across 28 districts. No geographically or institutionally delimited commons resource is documented in survey records, GP resolutions, or scheme guidelines.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q7.r: Scheme access is governed by administrative eligibility criteria (landholding ≤5 ha, registration on agriportal.cg.nic.in). No community commons access right — statutory, constitutional, or customary — is invoked. Benefits flow from individual private property rights and administrative enrolment. Score 0: purely executive/administrative discretion; access can be altered by administrative order.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, land-use change notification, SEZ notification, or compulsory acquisition proceeding affecting this scheme's operation is mentioned in any consulted official source. The scheme operates under standard state agricultural policy. CAG audit (Report No. 2 of 2026) reports 94% compliance with scheme guidelines with no encumbrance flagged.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q9.breakdown: Insurance premium paid: ₹87 Cr; Input subsidy distributed: ₹156 Cr; Administrative cost (8.2% of total): ₹37.56 Cr; Residual (extension, soil testing, equipment, market linkage): ₹177.44 Cr
Q9.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026: 'Total fund disbursed ₹458 crores (FY 2024-25); Insurance premium paid: ₹87 crores; Input subsidy distributed: ₹156 crores; Administrative cost: 8.2%'

Q10.r: No expenditure is directed at commons resources. All confirmed scheme components (crop insurance for private crops, input subsidies for private farm production, soil testing per individual farm, equipment support to individual farmers, extension training to private farmers for improving private productivity) serve private farm operations on private land. Extension and capacity-building activities, while delivered in group settings, target improvement of individual private agricultural output and do not constitute commons investment under T1.3 definition. No commons resource is the target of any identifiable scheme expenditure.
Q10.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q11.r: Confirmed private-benefit expenditure: Insurance premium ₹87 Cr (covers individual crop losses on private landholdings) + Input subsidy ₹156 Cr (seeds and fertilisers for private farm production) = ₹243 Cr. The residual ₹177.44 Cr (extension, soil testing, equipment, market linkage) and administrative cost ₹37.56 Cr also serve private farm benefit; precise sub-component breakdown not separately reported in available audited sources and is therefore not included in Q11 to avoid double-counting.
Q11.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q12.interp: FAIL — θ₁ = 0.00, well below the required 0.50 threshold. Even under the most generous alternative interpretation (attributing entire residual ₹177.44 Cr to commons-type expenditure), θ₁ ≤ 177.44 ÷ 458 = 0.387, still below threshold.
Q12.src: CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q14.r: Benefits (crop insurance payouts, input subsidies, soil testing reports, equipment) accrue exclusively to individually enrolled farmers with eligible private landholdings (≤5 hectares). Non-enrolled community members and those outside the eligibility criteria are fully and formally excluded through administrative registration and landholding verification on agriportal.cg.nic.in. No community-wide non-excludable benefit is generated. Score 0: fully excludable to one defined private-holder class.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009: 'Eligibility: Landholding up to 5 hectares'; National Portal of India

Q15.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26: 'Farmers benefited: 22.5 lakh (FY 2024-25)'

Q17.v: Settled_communities — 28 districts of Chhattisgarh constitute the scheme's operational area; all beneficiaries are permanently settled farmers residing within this area
Q17.r: The scheme is a state-level agricultural support programme targeting farmers across all 28 districts of Chhattisgarh. Beneficiaries must be resident farmers with landholding within the state to be eligible per Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009. The 'local' definition applied is settled communities within the scheme's 28-district operational geography. No pastoral, mobile, or seasonal community definition is applicable.
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.00, well above the threshold of 0.70. All scheme beneficiaries are resident farmers within the operational area of Chhattisgarh. By scheme design, non-resident farmers cannot register or receive benefits.
Q18.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q19.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No beneficiary-level social category breakdown (SC/ST/de-notified tribes/landless/women-headed/differently-abled-headed/elderly-headed households) is provided in any cited official source. The scheme notification (No. 2009/CGN-26/2009) targets 'small and marginal farmers' with landholding up to 5 hectares but does not disaggregate beneficiaries by social category. Outcome Budget 2025-26 and CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 report aggregate metrics only. SECC/PM-JANMAN data linked to scheme beneficiary rolls is not available in cited sources.>>
Q19.r: Chhattisgarh has ST population ~31% and SC ~13% (Census 2011). Small and marginal farmers (≤5 ha) in Chhattisgarh have disproportionately high tribal and SC representation given the state's agrarian structure, suggesting θ₃ is likely ≥ 0.40. However, no verifiable disaggregated beneficiary count is available from scheme documents or official evaluations to confirm this.

22a.r: Agricultural cooperative societies are formally constituted bodies under the Chhattisgarh Cooperative Societies Act and are identified as implementation vehicles in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 and Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25. Gram Sabhas (Panchayati Raj institutions) also exist at village level across the 28 operational districts.

22b.r: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 designates cooperative societies as implementation and delivery mechanisms for scheme components (input subsidy distribution, market linkage). However, no scheme document consulted mandates a formal decision-making role for cooperative societies or Gram Sabhas in scheme governance — benefit allocation, target-setting, or eligibility determination. The body's role is implementation/delivery rather than scheme governance decision-making as required by T2.3 criterion 2. UNVERIFIED from available sources; conservative answer = No.

22c.r: No meeting minutes, documented resolutions, or records of ≥ 2 formal governance meetings in the prior 12 months (FY 2024-25) for any scheme-level governance body are available in any cited official source. CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 audits financial compliance but does not document governance body meeting records. UNVERIFIED from available sources; conservative answer = No.

Q22.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q23.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No data on women's representation in cooperative society governance or scheme-level committees is available in cited sources. Formal women's representation figures for agricultural cooperative societies in Chhattisgarh are not documented in programs.md source material.>>
Q23.r: Conservative interpretation: No. Women's representation in agricultural cooperative governance in India frequently falls below 33%, and no scheme-specific documentation exists in cited sources to confirm compliance with the 73rd Constitutional Amendment threshold. Women-headed households are a defined category in the scheme's marginalised beneficiary group (T2.2), creating a potential governance representation gap.

Q24.r: No formal institutionally mandated grievance or voice mechanism for non-beneficiaries (farmers above eligibility threshold, community members excluded from scheme benefits) is documented in any cited official source. The National Portal of India lists a registration portal (agriportal.cg.nic.in) which may include a complaint function, but no social audit provision, standalone MIS grievance portal, or ombudsman mechanism specific to this scheme is mentioned in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009, CAG Report No. 2 of 2026, or Outcome Budget 2025-26. T2.4 = No → governance gap flagged.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; National Portal of India; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q25.r: The scheme's official notification (No. 2009/CGN-26/2009) mandates integrated agricultural support with explicit income and livelihood indicators. Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks mandatory output indicators including: (a) farmers benefited — 22.5 lakh (FY 2024-25); (b) crop insurance claims processed — 18,000+; (c) input subsidy disbursed — ₹156 Cr; (d) average yield increase — 12–15%. The portal description explicitly categorises the scheme under 'Income and Livelihood.' Economic Survey 2024-25 documents livelihood pathway via market linkage. Both a mandatory income pathway and measurable output indicators are articulated in official scheme documents.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal of India

observed.calc: No independent evaluation study, household survey, or MIS-verified income tracking data providing an explicit ΔY/HH/year figure is available in any consulted official source.

projected.v: <<UNVERIFIED: Outcome Budget 2025-26 reports 12–15% average yield increase among beneficiaries but does not state an explicit ΔY/HH/year figure in rupees; conversion requires base farm income per household, which is not provided in scheme documents. Likely exceeds ₹5,000 threshold given CG paddy MSP and average landholding, but cannot be verified from scheme sources alone.>>
projected.calc: Yield increase 12–15% (Outcome Budget 2025-26) applied to base farm income — base income figure not available in scheme documents; MSP for paddy (CACP 2024-25: ₹2,183/quintal) and average yields would support an estimate of ₹9,000–15,000/HH/year but require CG agricultural statistics not contained in programs.md
projected.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q26.r: No observed ΔY/HH/year data exists from evaluation studies. Projected ΔY cannot be computed solely from scheme documents as the average base farm income per beneficiary household is not stated. The 12–15% yield increase is the only economic impact figure available and is insufficient on its own to determine ΔY/HH without base income data.
docs: Outcome Budget 2025-26, Agriculture Department, Government of Chhattisgarh
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (scheme overview)

Q27.r: Three distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) Income protection via crop insurance — mandated in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 and tracked with 18,000+ claims processed in Outcome Budget 2025-26; (2) Productivity-driven income increase via 50% input subsidy on approved crops (seeds and fertilisers) — yield increase of 12–15% documented in Outcome Budget 2025-26; (3) Market linkage and improved price realisation via cooperative societies — explicitly stated as a scheme component in Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25. Each pathway is named and described in separate official documents.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q28.r: Multiple institutional offtake and demand channels exist and are operational: (1) Government crop insurance claim processing — Agriculture Department processes 18,000+ claims per year under scheme (Outcome Budget 2025-26), constituting institutional demand for scheme output; (2) Cooperative societies as institutional market channel — Economic Survey 2024-25 identifies cooperative societies as both implementation vehicle and market linkage mechanism for scheme beneficiaries; (3) MSP procurement for Chhattisgarh paddy farmers through state civil supplies corporation is accessible to scheme beneficiaries (small and marginal farmers, <5 ha landholding), constituting a government procurement guarantee for primary produce.
Q28.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009

Q29.r: Outcome Budget 2025-26 tracks 'Average yield increase: 12–15%' — a physical production metric (non-monetary) — and 'Farmer satisfaction score: 82%' — a non-monetary welfare/wellbeing metric. The yield increase metric tracks food and agricultural production quantity, qualifying as a non-monetary livelihood indicator under the scheme's monitoring framework. No near-threshold flag is triggered as Q26 is UNVERIFIED (T3.5=Yes prevents under-measurement flag in any case).
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q30.r: The scheme comprises crop insurance, 50% input subsidies (seeds and fertilizers), soil testing, extension training, equipment support, and market linkage — all targeted at individual private agricultural operations on private farmland. No mandatory replenishment, regeneration, or renewal rule for any commons resource is embedded in the scheme's design or official guidelines. Since T1.1 = C (no commons resource), there is no commons to replenish. The scheme contains no afforestation obligation, recharge structure mandate, closed grazing rotation, seed-bank renewal rule, knowledge-transmission requirement, or any equivalent sustainability lock as defined in the SOC framework T4.1 sub-parameter. Q30 = No → AUTOMATIC T4 FAIL (Near-SOC Structural classification if other tests pass).
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = 'C' (private land/property). T1.1 ≠ E → RhoRatio track applies. The scheme does not involve an intangible/knowledge/cultural/institutional commons, so the Vitality Index track is not applicable. Q32 is to be answered; Q33 is set to SKIP.

Q32.v: <<UNVERIFIED: ρ ratio is inapplicable. The scheme operates exclusively on private agricultural land (T1.1 = C); no commons resource is involved. There is no commons extraction to measure and no replenishment obligation exists (Q30 = No). Extraction-to-replenishment calculation requires both a commons extraction rate and a mandated replenishment rate — neither exists for this scheme. T1.5 = No (no boundary demarcation) would further require Estimated status, but ρ is not calculable in any form given the absence of a commons resource and replenishment obligation.>>
Q32.calc: ρ = commons extraction rate ÷ commons replenishment rate — neither numerator nor denominator exists for this scheme (T1.1 = C, no commons; Q30 = No, no replenishment mandate)
Q32.interp: FAIL — ρ inapplicable; Q30 = No renders T4 failed independently; even if ρ were calculable, no replenishment denominator exists

Q33.calc: SKIP — track = RhoRatio (T1.1 = C ≠ E); Vitality Index not applicable

Q35.r: The scheme operates on private agricultural land (T1.1 = C); no commons resource is the subject of the scheme, and therefore no commons resource can be classified as lying in a notified climate/ecological stress zone under the framework. For completeness: while parts of Chhattisgarh face drought stress (NDMA drought-prone blocks in some districts), the scheme's private farmland is not a commons resource subject to the T4.7 climate vulnerability conditional. Q35 = No → Q34 monitoring threshold remains ≥ 1 (standard, not upgraded to ≥ 2).
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 (resource type); NDMA drought classification (contextual)

Q34.r: Q35 = No → threshold applicable is Q34 ≥ 1. The scheme has an annual performance review cycle: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 mandates 'Annual scheme performance review: June.' Outcome Budget 2025-26 documents annual outcome tracking. CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 confirms annual audit and fund utilization review. This constitutes annual reporting by government agency — Score 1. No quarterly, semi-annual, or real-time monitoring of any resource health metric is documented. However, since there is no commons resource and Q30 = No (no replenishment obligation), the monitoring being assessed is the scheme's operational monitoring, not commons-resource health monitoring as intended by T4.3. C3_T4.3 = PASS at the standard threshold; note: this PASS is technical — it applies to scheme performance monitoring, not commons health monitoring, as no commons exists.
Q34.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q36.r: No penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of a replenishment or commons-use rule is documented in any cited official source. Since Q30 = No (no mandatory replenishment rule exists), there is no rule to enforce and correspondingly no penalty mechanism. Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009 does not describe financial penalties, benefit withdrawal, scheme disqualification, or legal action tied to any commons resource management obligation. The CAG Report No. 2 of 2026 notes 94% compliance with scheme guidelines but documents no penalty framework for ecological or commons compliance.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; CAG State Accounts Report No. 2 of 2026

Q37.r: No documented mechanism for post-scheme continuity of commons governance exists. The scheme has no commons resource (T1.1 = C) and therefore no commons governance institution to hand over to a GP, BMC, FRC, or community cooperative after the scheme period ends. No O&M fund, legal vesting clause, institutional handover plan, or continuity protocol for any commons asset is mentioned in Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009, the Outcome Budget 2025-26, or the CAG Report No. 2 of 2026. The scheme is a recurring state budget scheme with annual renewal, not a project-period scheme with a defined exit and handover mechanism for commons governance.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q38.r: All monitoring identified in the scheme is conducted exclusively by government agencies and departmental staff. The scheme deploys a network of 2,300+ agricultural extension workers (government employees) for implementation and monitoring. No community-based monitoring mandate, GP social audit provision, participatory resource mapping, community monitoring committee, or Village Ecological Register mechanism is mentioned in any cited official source. Monitoring is purely departmental. T4.6 = 0 → governance gap flagged as enhancement variable absent.
Q38.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Official Notification No. 2009/CGN-26/2009; Outcome Budget 2025-26

upgradePathway: Define commons boundary and legal access rights before implementation cycles.
Raise local and marginalized beneficiary share with explicit inclusion rules.
Set measurable livelihood outcomes and guaranteed offtake pathways.
Add enforceable replenishment and monitoring mechanisms to lock sustainability.
