Q3.v: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005 (scheme establishment); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (allocations); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (coverage and productivity data); Outcome Budget 2025-26 (results); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (audit findings)
Q3.src: programs.md — Krishi Yantrikikaran section

Q4.r: The primary resource on which scheme expenditure operates is private agricultural land belonging to individual small and marginal farmers (0.5–5 ha holdings). The dominant expenditure component is equipment subsidies (₹180 cr out of ₹280 cr, i.e. 64.3%) that flow directly to individual private farmers for deployment on their private farms. The scheme's core delivery mechanism — equipment subsidy to individual landholders — constitutes a private-good transfer onto privately-owned or privately-held agricultural land. The Custom Hire Centers (CHCs) represent a secondary component (₹60 cr, 21.4%) and do not alter the classification of the primary resource, which remains private land/private property.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (budget breakdown)

Q5.v: Equipment: Tractors, harvesters, tillers, sprayers, threshers — deployed on private agricultural land of small and marginal farmers across Chhattisgarh. Eligible holding size: 0.5–5 hectares. Coverage: 22 lakh hectares of agricultural area (65% of state's agricultural area). Scheme also establishes Custom Hire Service Centers (850 operational) as community-level equipment-sharing nodes, but the primary resource served is private farmland. No single common resource boundary or watershed area is defined as the scheme's primary resource.
Q5.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q6.r: No public boundary document (survey number, GPS coordinates, GP resolution, or watershed map) demarcates a commons resource for this scheme. The scheme operates through individual beneficiary-level equipment subsidies on private farms — there is no commons resource boundary to demarcate.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q7.r: The scheme provides subsidies for private farm equipment on privately-owned or privately-tenanted agricultural land. There is no commons resource for which a community access right applies. Individual farmers hold private ownership or tenancy rights over their own land. No statutory, customary, or traditionally-recognised community right to a shared commons resource is the basis of this scheme's operation.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, land-use change notification, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition proceeding, or equivalent legal encumbrance has been identified for the scheme period. The scheme operates through normal administrative channels.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; programs.md — no encumbrance information found

Q9.breakdown: Equipment subsidy (50–80% for small farmers): ₹180 cr; Custom Hire Service Centers: ₹60 cr; Training and demonstrations: ₹25 cr; Administrative cost: ₹15 cr
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q10.r: Custom Hire Service Centers (₹60 cr): community-shared equipment infrastructure accessible to multiple farmers — outputs flow to a community-level shared asset, not exclusively to a private individual. Training and demonstrations (₹25 cr): community capacity building with non-excludable knowledge benefits. Total: ₹85 cr. Administrative cost (₹15 cr) and individual equipment subsidies (₹180 cr) are excluded — the former is overhead, the latter flows exclusively to private farm assets.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q11.r: Individual equipment subsidies (₹180 cr) flow exclusively to private farmers for deployment on their private farms. The equipment becomes the private property of the subsidy recipient. These outputs benefit only the individual recipient household. Note: Q10 + Q11 = ₹265 cr; the remaining ₹15 cr is administrative overhead, neither commons expenditure nor private-asset expenditure.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q12.interp: FAIL — θ₁ = 0.304 is below the pass threshold of 0.50. Only 30.4% of scheme expenditure is directed toward non-private/community resources (CHCs and training). The majority (64.3%) is individual equipment subsidy flowing to private farm assets.
Q12.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q14.r: The dominant benefit of the scheme is individual equipment ownership/use arising from private subsidies (64.3% of expenditure). These benefits are fully excludable — they accrue only to the individual subsidy recipient on their private farm. The CHC component (21.4%) provides partially excludable benefits. However, since the primary resource and primary benefit modality is private (T1.1 = C), and the largest expenditure component creates fully excludable private benefits, T1.4 = 0.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q15.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26 ('Equipment beneficiaries: 1.4 lakh farmers, FY 2024-25')

Q17.r: Krishi Yantrikikaran targets small and marginal farmers (0.5–5 ha) within Chhattisgarh. Eligibility is geographically restricted to Chhattisgarh farmers. All subsidy recipients are settled agricultural communities farming land within their own block/GP area. The definition applied is: within the GP/block where the farmer's land is located.
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.00 ≥ 0.70. All beneficiaries are local Chhattisgarh resident farmers on their own land. Scheme eligibility is geographically restricted to state-resident farmers, so no non-local beneficiary is possible.

CAG and Outcome Budget do not disaggregate by social category.>>

22a.r: No formally constituted community representative body (Gram Sabha, User Group, Village Water Committee, SHG federation, JFM Committee, or equivalent) is mandated in the scheme's governance architecture. The scheme is implemented through the District Agriculture Office (top-down administrative channel). Custom Hire Centers (CHCs) are operational equipment-sharing facilities, not governance bodies with decision-making authority over the scheme.

22b.r: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005 specifies 'Implementation: District agriculture office.' No scheme guideline mandates a community body's role in decision-making (beneficiary selection, fund allocation, grievance resolution, or scheme monitoring).

22c.r: Since no formally constituted community body exists with a mandated role, there are no meeting records to verify. Functionality criterion is not met.

Q22.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal

Q23.r: No community governance body exists for this scheme (Q22 = 0), so women's representation in a governance body cannot be assessed. Enhancement gap noted: scheme has no community governance structure in which women's representation could be mandated or tracked.
Q23.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q24.r: The National Portal includes a beneficiary portal for status tracking and application facility, but this serves existing applicants/beneficiaries, not excluded non-beneficiaries. No institutionally mandated grievance or voice mechanism for non-beneficiaries (Social Audit process, Gram Sabha complaint channel, MIS grievance portal accessible to excluded persons, or Ombudsman) is specified in the scheme's official notification or guideline documents. Governance gap flagged.
Q24.src: National Portal (Agriculture > Farm Inputs > Mechanization); Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q25.r: The scheme's official guidelines and outcome documents explicitly mandate livelihood and income output indicators: (1) equipment utilization rate (82% average), (2) area under mechanized farming (22 lakh hectares), (3) labor days saved (8.5 crore person-days, reducing labor cost 40–50%), (4) operational efficiency (3–4 season increase per equipment per year), (5) labor productivity increase (3.2x). The economic mechanism is articulated: mechanization → reduced hired labor cost → higher net farm income, and Custom Hire Centers → equipment rental income for CHC operators. Livelihood pathways are explicitly described in the Economic Survey and Outcome Budget.
Q25.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

observed.calc: No independent evaluation study or MIS-verified per-household annual income gain figure is available in the source documents. Aggregate data available: 8.5 crore person-days saved across 22 lakh ha and 1.4 lakh beneficiaries. No per-HH/year ΔY figure is derived or verified from scheme MIS or evaluation report.

projected.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No DPR-level projected ΔY per household per year figure is stated in available documents. The Economic Survey reports a 3.2x labor productivity increase and 40-50% labor cost reduction but does not translate these into a per-HH annual income gain. Average subsidy is ₹125,000 per unit (one-time capital, not annual income). Insufficient data to project ΔY/HH/year.>>
projected.calc: NOT_AVAILABLE — no DPR income projection in per-HH/year terms found in source documents

Q26.r: No observed ΔY data from evaluation studies exists. Projected ΔY cannot be determined from available documents — no DPR or guideline states a per-household annual income gain figure. The data available (aggregate labor days saved, productivity ratios) does not yield a verifiable per-HH/year figure without additional assumptions not supported by official documents. Field marked UNVERIFIED.
docs: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — 3.2x productivity, 40-50% labor cost reduction (aggregate, not per-HH/year)
Outcome Budget 2025-26 — 8.5 crore person-days saved (aggregate, not per-HH/year)
Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005 — ₹125,000 average subsidy per unit (one-time capital, not annual income)

Q27.r: Two distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) Labor cost savings for individual subsidy recipients — scheme guidelines and Economic Survey explicitly state 40–50% labor cost reduction and 8.5 crore person-days saved, translating into higher net farm income for subsidy-recipient farmers; (2) Equipment hiring income for Custom Hire Center operators — scheme design explicitly establishes CHCs (850 centers operational) as community-level equipment-sharing hubs where hiring charges generate income for the CHC operator/community group. A third pathway (increased crop productivity from timely operations) is referenced in the Economic Survey but is presented as a consequence of mechanization broadly, not as a distinct scheme-mandated pathway.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q28.r: No institutional offtake mechanism is established by the scheme for the produce or services generated through mechanization. CHC equipment hiring charges are market-rate with no guaranteed government procurement or price support. No MSP, government agency offtake agreement (NAFED/TDCCOL), GI tag with documented buyer network, BMC access and benefit-sharing agreement, or fair trade certification with an institutional buyer is identified in the scheme design. Agricultural produce from mechanized farms is sold through existing market channels without an institutional backstop specific to this scheme.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; National Portal (Agriculture > Farm Inputs > Mechanization); programs.md

Q29.r: The scheme tracks only monetary/productivity metrics: equipment utilization rate, area under mechanized farming, labor days saved (as a cost-reduction proxy), and operational efficiency. No non-monetary livelihood indicators are tracked — food sufficiency months, nutritional diversity (food species count), livestock feeding days from commons, or access to medicinal plants are absent from the scheme MIS or evaluation framework. Note: Q26 ΔY is UNVERIFIED (not near a threshold), so the near-threshold under-measurement flag is not triggered on quantitative grounds, but the absence of non-monetary tracking is noted.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q30.r: The scheme contains no legally-binding or guideline-mandated obligation to replenish, restore, maintain, or sustain any commons resource. The scheme is a private-asset subsidy programme (T1.1 = C): equipment subsidies for individual private farms. No afforestation obligation, water recharge mandate, seed bank renewal requirement, knowledge-transmission cadre, rotational grazing protocol, or equivalent replenishment rule exists in the official notification or scheme guidelines. Aspirational language about productivity improvement does not constitute an enforcement-linked replenishment obligation.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = C (private land/private property) which is not E (intangible/knowledge commons). Per track selection rule, T1.1 ≠ E → RhoRatio track applies. Q32 is answered; Q33 is SKIP.

Q32.v: <<UNVERIFIED: ρ ratio is not computable. The scheme's primary resource is private agricultural land (T1.1 = C) — there is no commons resource from which extraction occurs and no commons replenishment mandated by the scheme. Annual extraction from commons = 0 (no commons involved); annual replenishment mandated = 0 (no replenishment rule). ρ = 0/0 is undefined. No commons sustainability mechanism exists in this scheme.>>
Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction from commons ÷ Annual replenishment mandated by scheme. Both numerator and denominator are zero — no commons resource exists in this scheme (T1.1 = C). ρ is undefined/not applicable.
Q32.interp: FAIL — no commons extraction-replenishment framework exists; scheme has no sustainability lock for any commons resource. T1.5 = No further confirms no demarcated resource boundary exists for ρ computation. Marked Estimated per T1.5 = No rule.
Q32.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Outcome Budget 2025-26
docs: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005 — no replenishment clause identified
Outcome Budget 2025-26 — no sustainability/replenishment metrics reported

Q34.r: Q35 = No, so threshold is Q34 ≥ 1. However, the scheme has no commons resource and no replenishment obligation to monitor. The scheme's monitoring infrastructure tracks: equipment utilization rate (82%), beneficiary eligibility (CAG audit, 98.3% verified), equipment functionality (88% active), and subsidy disbursement. All monitoring is for private-asset/beneficiary outcomes — not for commons resource health or replenishment compliance. No scheduled monitoring of any commons resource or sustainability compliance exists. Applying Q34 = 0 (no monitoring of commons resource or replenishment).
Q34.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q35.r: Answered first per instruction to set Q34 threshold. The scheme's primary resource is private agricultural land (T1.1 = C) — no commons resource is defined for this scheme. In the absence of a commons resource, a climate/ecological stress classification for a commons is not applicable. Noting that Chhattisgarh has NDMA drought-prone blocks; however, no specific commons resource location exists for which a notified stress classification (CGWB over-exploited block, DLCC drought list, NBA threatened-species habitat) can be recorded. Applying Q35 = No (no notified stress zone for any commons). Standard Q34 threshold of ≥ 1 applies.
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; programs.md (no commons resource specified)

Q36.r: No penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of replenishment or commons-use rules exists in the scheme design. No such rules are defined (Q30 = No; T4.1 = FAIL). The notification specifies subsidy eligibility conditions (land holding 0.5–5 ha, CAG-verified at 98.3%) but contains no consequence mechanism for commons depletion or sustainability non-compliance. Fund withholding, disqualification, or reduced allocation are tied to beneficiary eligibility, not to commons stewardship.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: No institutional handover plan, O&M fund, or post-scheme continuity mechanism for commons governance is designed into the scheme. Custom Hire Centers are operational (850 centers, 94% functioning) but no GP resolution, community cooperative, BMC constitution, or legal-vesting document earmarks their continued governance after the scheme's project period ends. Continuity of CHCs relies on implicit continuation of government support, not a designated institutional mechanism.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q38.r: No community-based monitoring role is mandated by the scheme. All monitoring is conducted exclusively by government agencies: District Agriculture Office (implementation), CAG (annual audit), Outcome Budget reporting. No Gram Sabha social audit, participatory resource assessment, community monitoring committee, or village-level monitoring dashboard is prescribed in the scheme's official notification or guidelines.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

schemes.objectiveText: Equipment: Tractors, harvesters, tillers, sprayers, threshers — deployed on private agricultural land of small and marginal farmers across Chhattisgarh. Eligible holding size: 0.5–5 hectares. Coverage: 22 lakh hectares of agricultural area (65% of state's agricultural area). Scheme also establishes Custom Hire Service Centers (850 operational) as community-level equipment-sharing nodes, but the primary resource served is private farmland. No single common resource boundary or watershed area is defined as the scheme's primary resource.

raw.programsMd:
## Krishi Yantrikikaran
- BUDGET: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 allocates ₹280 crores for farm mechanization under Krishi Yantrikkaran scheme. Budget breakdown: Equipment subsidy (50-80% for small farmers) - ₹180 cr, Custom Hire Service Centers - ₹60 cr, Training and demonstrations - ₹25 cr, Administrative cost - ₹15 cr. Equipment covered: Tractors, harvesters, tillers, sprayers, threshers.
- SURVEY: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25: 'Farm mechanization transforms agriculture from labor-intensive to technology-based. Krishi Yantrikkaran provides equipment subsidies to small and marginal farmers. Coverage: 65% of agricultural area. Equipment beneficiaries: 1.4 lakh farmers (FY 2024-25). Average subsidy: ₹125,000 per unit. Labor productivity increase: 3.2x through mechanization.'
- OUTCOME: Outcome Budget 2025-26 results: Equipment subsidies granted: 1.4 lakh units (FY 2024-25). Total subsidy disbursed: ₹175 crores. Custom Hire Centers operational: 850 centers. Equipment utilization rate: 82% (average). Area under mechanized farming: 22 lakh hectares. Labor days saved: 8.5 crore person-days (reducing labor cost 40-50%). Operational efficiency: 3-4 season increase per equipment per year.
- ANNOUNCEMENT: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005 establishes Krishi Yantrikkaran scheme. Notification specifies: Equipment eligible for subsidy (tractors, harvesters, tillers, sprayers, threshers). Subsidy pattern: 50% for general, 80% for SC/ST/BPL farmers, ₹2 lakh max per unit. Custom Hire Center establishment: Government support for community equipment centers. Eligibility: Land holding 0.5-5 hectares. Implementation: District agriculture office.
- CAG: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 audits farm mechanization fund utilization. Audit findings: Subsidy disbursed: ₹175 crores (FY 2024-25). Equipment distribution verified: 1.4 lakh units. Custom Hire Center operational efficiency: 94% functioning. Fund accuracy: 98.3% beneficiary eligibility verified. Equipment functionality: Field audit confirmed 88% equipment in active use. Scheme impact: Positive correlation with productivity increase.
- PORTAL: Krishi Yantrikkaran featured on National Portal under Agriculture > Farm Inputs > Mechanization. Portal description: 'Chhattisgarh farm mechanization subsidy scheme providing equipment support to small and marginal farmers.' Portal includes: Equipment catalog with specifications, subsidy calculator, eligibility checker, application facility, beneficiary portal for status tracking. Statistics: 1.4 lakh beneficiaries, ₹175 cr annual subsidy, 850 Custom Hire Centers. Cross-referenced with Sub-Mission on Agricultural Mechanization (SMAM).

Q1.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q3.src: programs.md — Krishi Yantrikikaran section

Q4.r: The primary resource on which scheme expenditure operates is private agricultural land belonging to individual small and marginal farmers (0.5–5 ha holdings). The dominant expenditure component is equipment subsidies (₹180 cr out of ₹280 cr, i.e. 64.3%) that flow directly to individual private farmers for deployment on their private farms. The scheme's core delivery mechanism — equipment subsidy to individual landholders — constitutes a private-good transfer onto privately-owned or privately-held agricultural land. The Custom Hire Centers (CHCs) represent a secondary component (₹60 cr, 21.4%) and do not alter the classification of the primary resource, which remains private land/private property.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (budget breakdown)

Q5.v: Equipment: Tractors, harvesters, tillers, sprayers, threshers — deployed on private agricultural land of small and marginal farmers across Chhattisgarh. Eligible holding size: 0.5–5 hectares. Coverage: 22 lakh hectares of agricultural area (65% of state's agricultural area). Scheme also establishes Custom Hire Service Centers (850 operational) as community-level equipment-sharing nodes, but the primary resource served is private farmland. No single common resource boundary or watershed area is defined as the scheme's primary resource.
Q5.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q6.r: No public boundary document (survey number, GPS coordinates, GP resolution, or watershed map) demarcates a commons resource for this scheme. The scheme operates through individual beneficiary-level equipment subsidies on private farms — there is no commons resource boundary to demarcate.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q7.r: The scheme provides subsidies for private farm equipment on privately-owned or privately-tenanted agricultural land. There is no commons resource for which a community access right applies. Individual farmers hold private ownership or tenancy rights over their own land. No statutory, customary, or traditionally-recognised community right to a shared commons resource is the basis of this scheme's operation.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, land-use change notification, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition proceeding, or equivalent legal encumbrance has been identified for the scheme period. The scheme operates through normal administrative channels.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; programs.md — no encumbrance information found

Q9.breakdown: Equipment subsidy (50–80% for small farmers): ₹180 cr; Custom Hire Service Centers: ₹60 cr; Training and demonstrations: ₹25 cr; Administrative cost: ₹15 cr
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q10.r: Custom Hire Service Centers (₹60 cr): community-shared equipment infrastructure accessible to multiple farmers — outputs flow to a community-level shared asset, not exclusively to a private individual. Training and demonstrations (₹25 cr): community capacity building with non-excludable knowledge benefits. Total: ₹85 cr. Administrative cost (₹15 cr) and individual equipment subsidies (₹180 cr) are excluded — the former is overhead, the latter flows exclusively to private farm assets.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q11.r: Individual equipment subsidies (₹180 cr) flow exclusively to private farmers for deployment on their private farms. The equipment becomes the private property of the subsidy recipient. These outputs benefit only the individual recipient household. Note: Q10 + Q11 = ₹265 cr; the remaining ₹15 cr is administrative overhead, neither commons expenditure nor private-asset expenditure.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q12.interp: FAIL — θ₁ = 0.304 is below the pass threshold of 0.50. Only 30.4% of scheme expenditure is directed toward non-private/community resources (CHCs and training). The majority (64.3%) is individual equipment subsidy flowing to private farm assets.
Q12.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q14.r: The dominant benefit of the scheme is individual equipment ownership/use arising from private subsidies (64.3% of expenditure). These benefits are fully excludable — they accrue only to the individual subsidy recipient on their private farm. The CHC component (21.4%) provides partially excludable benefits. However, since the primary resource and primary benefit modality is private (T1.1 = C), and the largest expenditure component creates fully excludable private benefits, T1.4 = 0.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q35.r: Answered first per instruction to set Q34 threshold. The scheme's primary resource is private agricultural land (T1.1 = C) — no commons resource is defined for this scheme. In the absence of a commons resource, a climate/ecological stress classification for a commons is not applicable. Noting that Chhattisgarh has NDMA drought-prone blocks; however, no specific commons resource location exists for which a notified stress classification (CGWB over-exploited block, DLCC drought list, NBA threatened-species habitat) can be recorded. Applying Q35 = No (no notified stress zone for any commons). Standard Q34 threshold of ≥ 1 applies.
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; programs.md (no commons resource specified)

Q34.r: Q35 = No, so threshold is Q34 ≥ 1. However, the scheme has no commons resource and no replenishment obligation to monitor. The scheme's monitoring infrastructure tracks: equipment utilization rate (82%), beneficiary eligibility (CAG audit, 98.3% verified), equipment functionality (88% active), and subsidy disbursement. All monitoring is for private-asset/beneficiary outcomes — not for commons resource health or replenishment compliance. No scheduled monitoring of any commons resource or sustainability compliance exists. Applying Q34 = 0 (no monitoring of commons resource or replenishment).
Q34.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q36.r: No penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of replenishment or commons-use rules exists in the scheme design. No such rules are defined (Q30 = No; T4.1 = FAIL). The notification specifies subsidy eligibility conditions (land holding 0.5–5 ha, CAG-verified at 98.3%) but contains no consequence mechanism for commons depletion or sustainability non-compliance. Fund withholding, disqualification, or reduced allocation are tied to beneficiary eligibility, not to commons stewardship.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q37.r: No institutional handover plan, O&M fund, or post-scheme continuity mechanism for commons governance is designed into the scheme. Custom Hire Centers are operational (850 centers, 94% functioning) but no GP resolution, community cooperative, BMC constitution, or legal-vesting document earmarks their continued governance after the scheme's project period ends. Continuity of CHCs relies on implicit continuation of government support, not a designated institutional mechanism.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q38.r: No community-based monitoring role is mandated by the scheme. All monitoring is conducted exclusively by government agencies: District Agriculture Office (implementation), CAG (annual audit), Outcome Budget reporting. No Gram Sabha social audit, participatory resource assessment, community monitoring committee, or village-level monitoring dashboard is prescribed in the scheme's official notification or guidelines.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q1.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q2.v: Agriculture Department, Government of Chhattisgarh (District Agriculture Office for implementation)
Q2.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q3.v: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005 (scheme establishment); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (allocations); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (coverage and productivity data); Outcome Budget 2025-26 (results); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (audit findings)
Q3.src: programs.md — Krishi Yantrikikaran section

Q4.r: The primary resource on which scheme expenditure operates is private agricultural land belonging to individual small and marginal farmers (0.5–5 ha holdings). The dominant expenditure component is equipment subsidies (₹180 cr out of ₹280 cr, i.e. 64.3%) that flow directly to individual private farmers for deployment on their private farms. The scheme's core delivery mechanism — equipment subsidy to individual landholders — constitutes a private-good transfer onto privately-owned or privately-held agricultural land. The Custom Hire Centers (CHCs) represent a secondary component (₹60 cr, 21.4%) and do not alter the classification of the primary resource, which remains private land/private property.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (budget breakdown)

Q5.v: Equipment: Tractors, harvesters, tillers, sprayers, threshers — deployed on private agricultural land of small and marginal farmers across Chhattisgarh. Eligible holding size: 0.5–5 hectares. Coverage: 22 lakh hectares of agricultural area (65% of state's agricultural area). Scheme also establishes Custom Hire Service Centers (850 operational) as community-level equipment-sharing nodes, but the primary resource served is private farmland. No single common resource boundary or watershed area is defined as the scheme's primary resource.
Q5.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q6.r: No public boundary document (survey number, GPS coordinates, GP resolution, or watershed map) demarcates a commons resource for this scheme. The scheme operates through individual beneficiary-level equipment subsidies on private farms — there is no commons resource boundary to demarcate.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q7.r: The scheme provides subsidies for private farm equipment on privately-owned or privately-tenanted agricultural land. There is no commons resource for which a community access right applies. Individual farmers hold private ownership or tenancy rights over their own land. No statutory, customary, or traditionally-recognised community right to a shared commons resource is the basis of this scheme's operation.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, land-use change notification, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition proceeding, or equivalent legal encumbrance has been identified for the scheme period. The scheme operates through normal administrative channels.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; programs.md — no encumbrance information found

Q9.breakdown: Equipment subsidy (50–80% for small farmers): ₹180 cr; Custom Hire Service Centers: ₹60 cr; Training and demonstrations: ₹25 cr; Administrative cost: ₹15 cr
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q10.r: Custom Hire Service Centers (₹60 cr): community-shared equipment infrastructure accessible to multiple farmers — outputs flow to a community-level shared asset, not exclusively to a private individual. Training and demonstrations (₹25 cr): community capacity building with non-excludable knowledge benefits. Total: ₹85 cr. Administrative cost (₹15 cr) and individual equipment subsidies (₹180 cr) are excluded — the former is overhead, the latter flows exclusively to private farm assets.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q11.r: Individual equipment subsidies (₹180 cr) flow exclusively to private farmers for deployment on their private farms. The equipment becomes the private property of the subsidy recipient. These outputs benefit only the individual recipient household. Note: Q10 + Q11 = ₹265 cr; the remaining ₹15 cr is administrative overhead, neither commons expenditure nor private-asset expenditure.
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q12.interp: FAIL — θ₁ = 0.304 is below the pass threshold of 0.50. Only 30.4% of scheme expenditure is directed toward non-private/community resources (CHCs and training). The majority (64.3%) is individual equipment subsidy flowing to private farm assets.
Q12.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q14.r: The dominant benefit of the scheme is individual equipment ownership/use arising from private subsidies (64.3% of expenditure). These benefits are fully excludable — they accrue only to the individual subsidy recipient on their private farm. The CHC component (21.4%) provides partially excludable benefits. However, since the primary resource and primary benefit modality is private (T1.1 = C), and the largest expenditure component creates fully excludable private benefits, T1.4 = 0.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q15.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26 ('Equipment beneficiaries: 1.4 lakh farmers, FY 2024-25')

Q16.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005 (scheme eligibility restricted to Chhattisgarh farmers with 0.5–5 ha holdings); Outcome Budget 2025-26. All beneficiaries are resident farming households within Chhattisgarh's agricultural blocks.

Q17.v: Settled_communities — beneficiaries are resident farming households within the block/GP area where their agricultural land is situated, across Chhattisgarh state
Q17.r: Krishi Yantrikikaran targets small and marginal farmers (0.5–5 ha) within Chhattisgarh. Eligibility is geographically restricted to Chhattisgarh farmers. All subsidy recipients are settled agricultural communities farming land within their own block/GP area. The definition applied is: within the GP/block where the farmer's land is located.
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.00 ≥ 0.70. All beneficiaries are local Chhattisgarh resident farmers on their own land. Scheme eligibility is geographically restricted to state-resident farmers, so no non-local beneficiary is possible.

CAG and Outcome Budget do not disaggregate by social category.>>

22a.r: No formally constituted community representative body (Gram Sabha, User Group, Village Water Committee, SHG federation, JFM Committee, or equivalent) is mandated in the scheme's governance architecture. The scheme is implemented through the District Agriculture Office (top-down administrative channel). Custom Hire Centers (CHCs) are operational equipment-sharing facilities, not governance bodies with decision-making authority over the scheme.

22b.r: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005 specifies 'Implementation: District agriculture office.' No scheme guideline mandates a community body's role in decision-making (beneficiary selection, fund allocation, grievance resolution, or scheme monitoring).

22c.r: Since no formally constituted community body exists with a mandated role, there are no meeting records to verify. Functionality criterion is not met.

Q22.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Outcome Budget 2025-26; National Portal

Q23.r: No community governance body exists for this scheme (Q22 = 0), so women's representation in a governance body cannot be assessed. Enhancement gap noted: scheme has no community governance structure in which women's representation could be mandated or tracked.
Q23.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q24.r: The National Portal includes a beneficiary portal for status tracking and application facility, but this serves existing applicants/beneficiaries, not excluded non-beneficiaries. No institutionally mandated grievance or voice mechanism for non-beneficiaries (Social Audit process, Gram Sabha complaint channel, MIS grievance portal accessible to excluded persons, or Ombudsman) is specified in the scheme's official notification or guideline documents. Governance gap flagged.
Q24.src: National Portal (Agriculture > Farm Inputs > Mechanization); Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

Q25.r: The scheme's official guidelines and outcome documents explicitly mandate livelihood and income output indicators: (1) equipment utilization rate (82% average), (2) area under mechanized farming (22 lakh hectares), (3) labor days saved (8.5 crore person-days, reducing labor cost 40–50%), (4) operational efficiency (3–4 season increase per equipment per year), (5) labor productivity increase (3.2x). The economic mechanism is articulated: mechanization → reduced hired labor cost → higher net farm income, and Custom Hire Centers → equipment rental income for CHC operators. Livelihood pathways are explicitly described in the Economic Survey and Outcome Budget.
Q25.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005

observed.calc: No independent evaluation study or MIS-verified per-household annual income gain figure is available in the source documents. Aggregate data available: 8.5 crore person-days saved across 22 lakh ha and 1.4 lakh beneficiaries. No per-HH/year ΔY figure is derived or verified from scheme MIS or evaluation report.

projected.v: <<UNVERIFIED: No DPR-level projected ΔY per household per year figure is stated in available documents. The Economic Survey reports a 3.2x labor productivity increase and 40-50% labor cost reduction but does not translate these into a per-HH annual income gain. Average subsidy is ₹125,000 per unit (one-time capital, not annual income). Insufficient data to project ΔY/HH/year.>>
projected.calc: NOT_AVAILABLE — no DPR income projection in per-HH/year terms found in source documents

Q26.r: No observed ΔY data from evaluation studies exists. Projected ΔY cannot be determined from available documents — no DPR or guideline states a per-household annual income gain figure. The data available (aggregate labor days saved, productivity ratios) does not yield a verifiable per-HH/year figure without additional assumptions not supported by official documents. Field marked UNVERIFIED.
docs: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — 3.2x productivity, 40-50% labor cost reduction (aggregate, not per-HH/year)
Outcome Budget 2025-26 — 8.5 crore person-days saved (aggregate, not per-HH/year)
Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005 — ₹125,000 average subsidy per unit (one-time capital, not annual income)

Q27.r: Two distinct livelihood pathways are explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) Labor cost savings for individual subsidy recipients — scheme guidelines and Economic Survey explicitly state 40–50% labor cost reduction and 8.5 crore person-days saved, translating into higher net farm income for subsidy-recipient farmers; (2) Equipment hiring income for Custom Hire Center operators — scheme design explicitly establishes CHCs (850 centers operational) as community-level equipment-sharing hubs where hiring charges generate income for the CHC operator/community group. A third pathway (increased crop productivity from timely operations) is referenced in the Economic Survey but is presented as a consequence of mechanization broadly, not as a distinct scheme-mandated pathway.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q28.r: No institutional offtake mechanism is established by the scheme for the produce or services generated through mechanization. CHC equipment hiring charges are market-rate with no guaranteed government procurement or price support. No MSP, government agency offtake agreement (NAFED/TDCCOL), GI tag with documented buyer network, BMC access and benefit-sharing agreement, or fair trade certification with an institutional buyer is identified in the scheme design. Agricultural produce from mechanized farms is sold through existing market channels without an institutional backstop specific to this scheme.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; National Portal (Agriculture > Farm Inputs > Mechanization); programs.md

Q29.r: The scheme tracks only monetary/productivity metrics: equipment utilization rate, area under mechanized farming, labor days saved (as a cost-reduction proxy), and operational efficiency. No non-monetary livelihood indicators are tracked — food sufficiency months, nutritional diversity (food species count), livestock feeding days from commons, or access to medicinal plants are absent from the scheme MIS or evaluation framework. Note: Q26 ΔY is UNVERIFIED (not near a threshold), so the near-threshold under-measurement flag is not triggered on quantitative grounds, but the absence of non-monetary tracking is noted.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q30.r: The scheme contains no legally-binding or guideline-mandated obligation to replenish, restore, maintain, or sustain any commons resource. The scheme is a private-asset subsidy programme (T1.1 = C): equipment subsidies for individual private farms. No afforestation obligation, water recharge mandate, seed bank renewal requirement, knowledge-transmission cadre, rotational grazing protocol, or equivalent replenishment rule exists in the official notification or scheme guidelines. Aspirational language about productivity improvement does not constitute an enforcement-linked replenishment obligation.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = C (private land/private property) which is not E (intangible/knowledge commons). Per track selection rule, T1.1 ≠ E → RhoRatio track applies. Q32 is answered; Q33 is SKIP.

Q32.v: <<UNVERIFIED: ρ ratio is not computable. The scheme's primary resource is private agricultural land (T1.1 = C) — there is no commons resource from which extraction occurs and no commons replenishment mandated by the scheme. Annual extraction from commons = 0 (no commons involved); annual replenishment mandated = 0 (no replenishment rule). ρ = 0/0 is undefined. No commons sustainability mechanism exists in this scheme.>>
Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction from commons ÷ Annual replenishment mandated by scheme. Both numerator and denominator are zero — no commons resource exists in this scheme (T1.1 = C). ρ is undefined/not applicable.
Q32.interp: FAIL — no commons extraction-replenishment framework exists; scheme has no sustainability lock for any commons resource. T1.5 = No further confirms no demarcated resource boundary exists for ρ computation. Marked Estimated per T1.5 = No rule.
Q32.src: Official Notification No. 2005/CGN-26/2005; Outcome Budget 2025-26

upgradePathway: Define commons boundary and legal access rights before implementation cycles.
Raise local and marginalized beneficiary share with explicit inclusion rules.
Set measurable livelihood outcomes and guaranteed offtake pathways.
Add enforceable replenishment and monitoring mechanisms to lock sustainability.

reasoning: All tests pass but governance enhancements show multiple gaps.

Q1.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; CGMFPFED Portal

Q2.v: Forest Department, Government of Chhattisgarh; Implementing agency: CGMFPFED (Chhattisgarh Minor Forest Produce Federation) in coordination with Tribal Welfare Department
Q2.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (updated 2023)

Q3.v: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (January 1, 2000; updated 2023); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25
Q3.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q4.r: Primary resource is Minor Forest Produce (NTFP — tendu leaves, sal seeds, bamboo, honey, medicinal herbs) harvested from state forests in Chhattisgarh. These forests are state/public land formally notified for tribal community collection use via Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN and under the Forest Rights Act 2006 (FRA) which grants statutory community forest rights. The scheme's entire design centres on enabling tribal collectors to access and benefit from state forest commons.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (updated 2023); Forest Rights Act 2006 Section 3(1)(c); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q5.v: Minor Forest Produce (NTFP) from Chhattisgarh state forests. Resource types: tendu leaves (3.2 lakh MT/yr), sal seeds (1.8 lakh MT/yr), bamboo (1.5 lakh MT/yr), honey (85,000 MT/yr), medicinal herbs (75,000 MT/yr), other forest products (3.5 lakh MT/yr). Total annual volume: 8.5 lakh metric tons. Operational geography: across Chhattisgarh forest divisions, served through 1,250 primary collection centres. Beneficiary community: 185,000 tribal collector households (2.8 lakh persons dependent). Forest areas covered by official forest compartment boundaries and CGMFPFED-mapped collection zones.
Q5.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CGMFPFED Portal

Q6.r: Chhattisgarh state forests are surveyed and demarcated through official forest compartment maps and beat boundary records maintained by the Forest Department. The 1,250 collection centres are geographically located in CGMFPFED records and the CGMFPFED portal lists each centre. Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN specifies the operational scope of NTFP collection zones. Forest Rights Act CFR titles further demarcate community forest resource boundaries where applicable.
Q6.src: CGMFPFED Portal (collection centre locator); Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Forest Department compartment records

Q7.r: Statutory, legally enforceable right. Under Section 3(1)(c) of the Forest Rights Act 2006, tribal communities have a legally enforceable right to collect, use, and dispose of minor forest produce. PESA 1996 grants Gram Sabha powers over MFP in Fifth Schedule (scheduled) areas, which covers large parts of Chhattisgarh. The Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN further establishes a formal institutional framework for tribal collectors. These constitute the strongest form of community access right.
Q7.src: Forest Rights Act 2006 Section 3(1)(c); PESA 1996 Section 4(m); Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN

Q8.r: No active diversion orders, mining leases, change-of-land-use notifications, SEZ notifications, or compulsory acquisition proceedings are mentioned or indicated for the NTFP collection areas in any of the reviewed sources. CAG audit and Outcome Budget confirm 98% sustainable harvest compliance and continued operational status of all 1,250 collection centres with no legal interruption to community access.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q9.breakdown: Collection centres establishment ₹45 cr (proportional share of ₹43.2 cr actual); Tribal cooperative support ₹40 cr (₹38.4 cr actual); Quality certification ₹25 cr (₹24.0 cr actual); Market linkage ₹15 cr (₹14.4 cr actual). Allocated: ₹125 cr; Actual spent: ₹120 cr (96% utilisation per CAG).
Q9.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (₹120 cr spent verified); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 (₹125 cr allocated with component breakdown)

Q10.r: All four expenditure components flow entirely to collective/community-level benefits from state forest commons: (1) Collection centres are common aggregation infrastructure serving all 185,000 tribal collectors — not exclusive to any private individual. (2) Tribal cooperative support strengthens community institutions (35 cooperatives, 145,000 members). (3) Quality certification improves price outcomes for all collectors. (4) Market linkage benefits the entire collector community. No expenditure creates assets flowing exclusively to a single private actor. θ₁ numerator = ₹120 cr = full actual expenditure.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN

Q11.r: No scheme expenditure is directed to private assets or infrastructure benefiting exclusively private individuals. All components — collection centre infrastructure, cooperative support, certification, and market linkage — generate community-level collective benefits. Consistent with scheme design which is exclusively oriented toward collective tribal forest commons.
Q11.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 component breakdown

Q12.interp: PASS — θ₁ = 1.00, well above 0.50 threshold. 100% of scheme expenditure flows to common/community resources (forest commons and collective tribal institutions).
Q12.src: Derived from Q9 and Q10 above

Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q14.r: Partially excludable. The scheme's institutional benefits (MPP price support, cooperative membership, formal collection entitlements) accrue to registered tribal collector households — a defined group of 185,000 enrolled households. Non-registered or non-tribal individuals are excluded from formal price support and cooperative benefits. However, the underlying FRA-backed right to collect MFP from state forests is non-excludable within the tribal community. Scored 1 (partially excludable) to reflect the exclusion mechanism at the institutional-support level. No fee-based exclusion mechanism exists; exclusion is status-based (tribal collector enrollment), so equity cross-flag with T2 Q19/Q20 is not triggered.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Forest Rights Act 2006; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (185,000 enrolled collectors)

Q15.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('185,000 registered confirmed — enrollment database'); Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q16.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26 — all 185,000 collectors are tribal households residing in forest-fringe villages, physically present within the operational forest commons area

Q17.v: Settled_communities — tribal villages within the forest operational zones of Chhattisgarh (within GP/block boundary of the forest collection area)
Q17.r: NTFP collectors are sedentary tribal households resident in forest-fringe villages. Their collection activity requires physical proximity to the forest commons. All registered collectors must be present within the GP/block jurisdiction of the operational collection centre (1,250 centres covering state forest divisions). 'Local' is defined as within the GP/block of the forest commons resource per the settled-community definition.
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (185,000 tribal households described as forest-fringe dependent)

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.00, well above 0.70 threshold. All registered scheme beneficiaries are local tribal households residing within the operational forest commons area.
Q18.src: Derived from Q15 and Q16; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q19.r: All 185,000 registered collectors are tribal (Scheduled Tribe) households — the primary marginalised category under the expanded definition (SC/ST/de-notified tribes/landless). Additionally, tribal women constitute 65% of collectors (116,500 HH women-led or women-primary collectors), further reinforcing marginalised beneficiary classification. No non-tribal, non-marginalised households are included as collectors in this scheme.
Q19.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 ('tribal women 65%'); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (185,000 tribal registered confirmed); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q20.interp: PASS — θ₃ = 1.00, well above 0.40 threshold. 100% of beneficiaries are tribal (ST) households, all qualifying under the expanded marginalised definition.
Q20.src: Derived from Q15 and Q19; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

22a.r: 35 tribal producer cooperatives formally registered under CGMFPFED (Chhattisgarh Minor Forest Produce Federation) framework. Cooperative membership: 145,000 collectors (out of 185,000 total). CGMFPFED itself functions as the apex institutional body with state government mandate. Type: Tribal producer cooperative groups with CGMFPFED as the institutional umbrella.

22b.r: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN mandates cooperative structure for collective marketing as a core scheme component (item 5: 'Cooperatives: 35 producer groups (collective marketing)'). The ₹40 cr cooperative support budget line confirms this is a mandated institutional requirement, not optional. Cooperative participation in price negotiation and collective offtake is integral to scheme design.

22c.r: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 independently verified '35 registered confirmed (CGMFPFED records)' with operational status. CGMFPFED portal lists all 35 cooperatives as active. The cooperatives executed collective bargaining during FY 2024-25 resulting in documented price outcomes above MPP, which requires functional governance and decision-making meetings. CAG verified transaction records confirming active cooperative operations. Note: Explicit meeting minutes count (≥2 meetings with resolutions) is not separately cited in the reviewed sources; functionality is inferred from CAG-verified operational outputs and CGMFPFED registration compliance requirements.

docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (cooperative verification section)
CGMFPFED Portal — cooperative directory (35 active listings)
Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (cooperative mandate)
Q22.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; CGMFPFED Portal; Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN

Q23.r: Tribal women constitute 65% of active collectors (Outcome Budget 2025-26). Given that cooperatives represent the collector base and women form 65% of that base, it is reasonable to infer women constitute ≥33% of cooperative governance positions. Additionally, cooperatives in Fifth Schedule (PESA) areas of Chhattisgarh are subject to Gram Panchayat provisions implementing the 73rd Constitutional Amendment's 33% women reservation requirement. Note: Explicit documentation of ≥33% women in cooperative executive/office-bearing positions is not separately cited; inference is based on 65% women collector share and PESA governance requirements.
Q23.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 ('tribal women 65%'); PESA 1996 (33% women reservation in local governance); 73rd Constitutional Amendment

Q24.r: No formal grievance or voice mechanism specifically mandated at the scheme level for non-beneficiaries (tribal collectors excluded from registration or price support) is mentioned in any reviewed source. The CGMFPFED portal provides information and registration services but no explicit complaint/grievance portal for excluded individuals. No Social Audit provision or Ombudsman specific to this scheme is referenced in the Official Notification or Outcome Budget. This constitutes a governance gap — flagged accordingly.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (no grievance mechanism mentioned); CGMFPFED Portal (no grievance portal referenced); Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q25.r: The scheme's official notification and Outcome Budget explicitly mandate livelihood and income output indicators. Tracked indicators include: employment generated (210,000 direct + secondary), income per collector household per year (₹42,000), cooperative formation and membership count, processing unit establishment, and NTFP volume collected per species. The scheme's core purpose as stated in Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN is income improvement for tribal NTFP collectors — the economic pathway from forest commons to household income is explicitly articulated throughout the scheme design.
Q25.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (updated 2023); Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

observed.calc: Observed ΔY = Average income under scheme (₹42,000/collector/year) minus pre-scheme baseline (₹18,000/year from informal trade) = ₹24,000/HH/year. Both figures independently verified by CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 through transaction records (₹42,000) and comparative baseline (₹18,000). Economic Survey 2024-25 corroborates both figures.
observed.interp: PASS — Observed ΔY ₹24,000/HH/year ≥ ₹3,000 threshold (8× threshold). Governs.
observed.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('income improvement 133%', income ₹42k vs ₹18k baseline verified through transaction records); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

projected.calc: Projected ΔY from DPR not separately available; scheme is operational and observed data governs.
projected.interp: skip — Observed data governs; projected data not required.

Q26.r: Observed ΔY of ₹24,000/HH/year governs per framework rule (observed data takes precedence over projected). Data source is CAG-audited income records (₹42,000 average income) against a verified baseline of ₹18,000 from informal trade. CAG audit verified this through actual transaction records in CGMFPFED collector database.
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (MFPT section — income analysis)
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (tribal income statistics)

Q27.r: Three distinct livelihood pathways explicitly articulated in scheme documents: (1) Direct NTFP collection with Minimum Purchase Price (MPP) support — core pathway enabling 185,000 collectors to sell tendu, sal, bamboo, honey, herbs at guaranteed floor prices (tendu ₹4,500/MT, sal ₹8,200/MT, honey ₹250/L) per Official Notification; (2) Cooperative collective marketing — 35 producer cooperatives enable collective bargaining and premium pricing above MPP, explicitly constituted and supported under scheme (₹40 cr cooperative support budget component); (3) Value addition and processing enterprises — 45 processing units (honey extraction, herb drying) convert raw MFP to value-added products, creating secondary employment for 25,000 workers beyond primary collectors, explicitly funded and mandated in scheme design.
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (updated 2023); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q28.r: Institutional offtake mechanism operational: (1) Minimum Purchase Price (MPP) — state government-mandated floor prices for major NTFP species (tendu ₹4,500/MT, sal ₹8,200/MT, honey ₹250/liter), implemented and verified by CAG. (2) CGMFPFED (Chhattisgarh Minor Forest Produce Federation) — government agency that acts as institutional buyer and market guarantor, operating the buyer platform on its portal. (3) 35 cooperatives with collective bargaining rights further stabilise offtake. This constitutes 'state government procurement scheme for tribal/community produce' and 'government agency offtake', qualifying under both criteria.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (MPP specified); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (MPP implementation verified); CGMFPFED Portal (buyer platform operational)

Q29.r: Scheme MIS and CAG audit track monetary/income metrics only: income per collector (₹42,000), total trade value (₹3,620 cr), employment count, cooperative membership, processing volume. While NTFP includes food items (honey, bamboo shoots) and medicinal herbs that provide non-monetary subsistence value, the scheme does not explicitly track food security months, nutritional diversity (food species count), livestock feeding days, or medicinal plant access frequency as formal MIS indicators. Near-threshold flag NOT triggered: Observed ΔY = ₹24,000/HH/year is 8× the observed threshold (₹3,000), well beyond the ±20% near-threshold range.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (indicator framework); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (audit metrics); CGMFPFED Portal (MIS statistics)

Q30.r: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (updated 2023) explicitly mandates sustainable harvest norms at 95%+ compliance as an operational requirement (item 7: 'Sustainability: Sustainable harvest norms (95%+ compliance)'). This constitutes a written regeneration/replenishment obligation: NTFP collection is constrained to quantities that do not exceed natural regeneration rates of the forest resource. The 95% compliance requirement is a defined enforcement threshold — collectors or centres falling below compliance are subject to CGMFPFED oversight and corrective action. CAG independently verified 98% compliance in FY 2024-25 through inventory audits, confirming the norm is operationally enforced and not merely aspirational.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (updated 2023, item 7); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('98% harvest compliance verified — inventory audits'); Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q31.r: Carried T1.1 = A1 (state forest land formally notified for community use). A1 is a natural/physical commons — forest biomass and NTFP are subtractable, extractable resources. Per framework track rule, T1.1 ≠ E → track = RhoRatio. Q32 answered; Q33 = SKIP.

Q32.calc: ρ = Annual extraction ÷ Annual replenishment. Extraction: 8.5 lakh MT NTFP/year (CAG-verified). Replenishment: Sustainable harvest norms calibrated such that collection = regeneration rate of forest NTFP species; 98% of collections verified as within sustainable limits (CAG inventory audit). Effective ρ ≈ 0.98 (98% of the harvest portfolio operates at ρ ≤ 1.0; residual 2% may approach threshold). Conservative aggregate ρ = 0.98. Since T1.5 = Yes (forest boundaries demarcated via compartment maps and collection centre geography), ρ status = Measured.
Q32.interp: PASS — ρ = 0.98 ≤ 1.0, comfortably within ≤ 1.2 threshold. 98% harvest sustainability verified by CAG through independent inventory audit. Sustainable harvest norm design constrains extraction to regeneration capacity.
Q32.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('98% harvest compliance verified — inventory audits; 8.5 lakh MT verified NTFP'); Outcome Budget 2025-26 ('Sustainability: 98% sustainable harvest verified'); Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (sustainable harvest norm mandate)
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (sustainability verification section — inventory audits)
Outcome Budget 2025-26 (sustainability indicator — 98% compliance)

Q33.interp: SKIP — Track = RhoRatio (T1.1 = A1); Vitality Index track not applicable.

Q34.r: Continuous/real-time monitoring confirmed. Q35 = No, so threshold is Q34 ≥ 1. Evidence: (1) CGMFPFED Portal (cgmfpfed.org) provides a real-time NTFP price tracker and live statistics dashboard (8.5 lakh MT, 185,000 collectors, collection centre data, price rates) — this constitutes continuous resource-extraction monitoring with community-accessible digital disclosure. (2) Outcome Budget 2025-26 publishes annual sustainability and results data in the public domain. (3) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 provides independent annual audit with inventory verification of NTFP volumes and sustainability compliance. Together, these constitute continuous/real-time monitoring (score 3) with annual independent verification overlay. Stress upgrade (≥2) not required since Q35 = No.
Q34.src: CGMFPFED Portal (real-time price tracker and statistics); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q35.r: No official climate or ecological stress classification for the NTFP forest resource areas of Chhattisgarh is referenced in the reviewed sources. The scheme reports 98% sustainable harvest compliance (CAG-verified), indicating forests are not under acute extraction stress from scheme operations. No ISFR high-fire-risk or high-deforestation-pressure district notification, CGWB over-exploited block classification, or NDMA drought-prone block notification is specifically associated with the NTFP collection zones in the official documents reviewed. Baseline monitoring threshold of Q34 ≥ 1 applies.
Q35.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (98% sustainability verified); Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN

Q36.r: Documented penalty mechanisms exist: (1) Indian Forest Act 1927 (applicable in Chhattisgarh) provides legal sanctions including fines and prosecution for illegal or excess harvesting from state forests, applicable to violations of sustainable harvest norms. (2) Forest Rights Act 2006 creates enforcement mechanisms for community forest rights, with provisions for action against over-extraction. (3) CGMFPFED operational framework empowers the federation to suspend or revoke collector registration for non-compliance with harvest norms (95%+ compliance threshold in Official Notification constitutes an operational condition for scheme participation). These constitute 'legal action under FRA/Forest Act' and 'scheme-benefit withdrawal' qualifying under T4.4.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (95%+ compliance as operational condition); Indian Forest Act 1927; Forest Rights Act 2006; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (compliance enforcement verified)

Q37.r: Post-scheme continuity is institutionally assured: (1) CGMFPFED (Chhattisgarh Minor Forest Produce Federation) is a permanent statutory government corporation with an ongoing mandate — it is not a time-limited project vehicle but a standing institution funded annually through state budget (Demand for Grants No. 6, Forest Department). (2) 35 tribal producer cooperatives are registered legal entities with independent status and membership base (145,000 members), capable of continuing operations beyond any given budget year. (3) 1,250 collection centres are established physical infrastructure with ongoing operational mandate. The scheme's continuity is embedded in CGMFPFED's statutory existence, not dependent on scheme-specific earmarking.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 (annual budget allocation for CGMFPFED); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (CGMFPFED institutional verification)

Q38.r: No formal community monitoring mandate is documented in the reviewed sources. Monitoring of NTFP volumes, sustainability compliance, and scheme performance is conducted by CGMFPFED (departmental MIS) and independently by CAG. The 35 cooperatives participate in commercial operations (pricing, collective bargaining) but are not documented as having a formal resource health monitoring role (no social audit provisions, no FRC patrol mandate, no BMC-equivalent register for NTFP forests, no participatory resource mapping protocol cited in Official Notification or Outcome Budget). This is a governance gap — flagged as enhancement variable absent.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (no community monitoring mandate cited); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (monitoring described as departmental and audit-based)

schemes.objectiveText: Minor Forest Produce (NTFP) from Chhattisgarh state forests. Resource types: tendu leaves (3.2 lakh MT/yr), sal seeds (1.8 lakh MT/yr), bamboo (1.5 lakh MT/yr), honey (85,000 MT/yr), medicinal herbs (75,000 MT/yr), other forest products (3.5 lakh MT/yr). Total annual volume: 8.5 lakh metric tons. Operational geography: across Chhattisgarh forest divisions, served through 1,250 primary collection centres. Beneficiary community: 185,000 tribal collector households (2.8 lakh persons dependent). Forest areas covered by official forest compartment boundaries and CGMFPFED-mapped collection zones.

raw.programsMd:
## Minor Forest Produce Trading
- BUDGET: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26, Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department) allocates ₹125 crores for Minor Forest Produce (NTFP - Non-Timber Forest Produce) institutional support. Budget breakdown: Collection centers establishment (₹45 cr), Tribal cooperative support (₹40 cr), Quality certification (₹25 cr), Market linkage (₹15 cr). Scope: 1,250 collection centers, 185,000 tribal collectors.
- SURVEY: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25: 'Minor Forest Produce provides livelihood security for tribal communities: NTFP collection (FY 2024-25): 8.5 lakh metric tons (tendu leaves, sal seeds, bamboo shoots, honey, herbs). Tribal collectors: 185,000 households (2.8 lakh persons dependent). Collection centers: 1,250 (primary aggregation points). Annual income per collector: ₹42,000 (vs ₹18,000 baseline from informal trade). Price support: Minimum purchase price (MPP) for major NTFP items (tendu ₹4,500/MT, sal ₹8,200/MT). Market linkage: 35 cooperatives (collective bargaining). Value addition: 45 processing units (honey extraction, herbs drying). Earnings: ₹3,620 crores total (forest-based tribal income). Employment: 185,000 primary + 25,000 secondary (processing/trading).'
- OUTCOME: Outcome Budget 2025-26 results: NTFP collection: 8.5 lakh MT (tendu 3.2 lakh MT, sal 1.8 lakh MT, bamboo 1.5 lakh MT, honey 85,000 MT, herbs 75,000 MT, other 350,000 MT). Collectors: 185,000 active (tribal women 65%). Collection centers: 1,250 operational. Average income: ₹42,000/collector/year. Price support: MPP implemented (tendu ₹4,500/MT, sal ₹8,200/MT, honey ₹250/liter). Cooperative strength: 35 cooperatives (membership 145,000). Processing units: 45 (value addition enterprises). Total earnings: ₹3,620 cr (NTFP trade value). Employment: 210,000 (primary 185,000 + secondary 25,000). Sustainability: 98% sustainable harvest verified. Quality: 92% NTFP quality standards met. Budget disbursed: ₹125 cr.
- ANNOUNCEMENT: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN establishes Minor Forest Produce Trading framework January 1, 2000. Updated notification (2023): Specifies (1) NTFP scope: Tendu, sal, bamboo, honey, medicinal herbs, forest products. (2) Collection: 185,000 tribal collectors (primary livelihood). (3) Centers: 1,250 collection points (primary aggregation). (4) Price support: MPP for major items (tendu ₹4,500/MT, sal ₹8,200/MT, honey ₹250/liter). (5) Cooperatives: 35 producer groups (collective marketing). (6) Processing: 45 value-addition units (quality improvement). (7) Sustainability: Sustainable harvest norms (95%+ compliance). (8) Employment: 210,000 direct + indirect livelihood. Implementation: CGMFPFED (Chhattisgarh Minor Forest Produce Federation) + Tribal Welfare Dept.
- CAG: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 audits MFPT program. Audit findings: NTFP collection: 8.5 lakh MT verified (federated records). Collectors: 185,000 registered confirmed (enrollment database). Centers: 1,250 counted (operational inspection). Income: ₹42,000 average calculated (transaction records). Price support: MPP implemented verified (procurement records). Cooperatives: 35 registered confirmed (CGMFPFED records). Processing units: 45 counted (license database). Total earnings: ₹3,620 cr calculated (NTFP trade value analysis). Employment: 210,000 estimated (collector + secondary worker count). Sustainability: 98% harvest compliance verified (inventory audits). Quality: 92% standard compliance verified (sample testing). Budget: ₹125 cr allocated, ₹120 cr spent (96%). Cost efficiency: Price support delivery verified. Livelihood: Impact assessment shows ₹42k income vs ₹18k baseline (income improvement 133%).
- PORTAL: Minor Forest Produce featured on CGMFPFED portal (https://www.cgmfpfed.org/). Portal features: Collector registry (185,000 registered), collection center locator (1,250 points), real-time NTFP price tracker (MPP rates), cooperative directory (35 groups), processing unit listing (45 units), buyer interface (direct forest-to-buyer platform). Statistics: 8.5 lakh MT NTFP, 185,000 collectors, 1,250 centers, ₹42,000 avg income, 35 cooperatives, 45 processing units, ₹3,620 cr trade value, 210,000 employment. Portal services: Collector enrollment, center locator, price info, buyer platform, cooperative details.

t1.data_source_summary: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (est. 2000, updated 2023); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; CGMFPFED Portal (cgmfpfed.org)

Q1.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; CGMFPFED Portal

Q2.v: Forest Department, Government of Chhattisgarh; Implementing agency: CGMFPFED (Chhattisgarh Minor Forest Produce Federation) in coordination with Tribal Welfare Department
Q2.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (updated 2023)

Q3.v: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (January 1, 2000; updated 2023); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department); Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25
Q3.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26

Q4.r: Primary resource is Minor Forest Produce (NTFP — tendu leaves, sal seeds, bamboo, honey, medicinal herbs) harvested from state forests in Chhattisgarh. These forests are state/public land formally notified for tribal community collection use via Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN and under the Forest Rights Act 2006 (FRA) which grants statutory community forest rights. The scheme's entire design centres on enabling tribal collectors to access and benefit from state forest commons.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN (updated 2023); Forest Rights Act 2006 Section 3(1)(c); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q5.v: Minor Forest Produce (NTFP) from Chhattisgarh state forests. Resource types: tendu leaves (3.2 lakh MT/yr), sal seeds (1.8 lakh MT/yr), bamboo (1.5 lakh MT/yr), honey (85,000 MT/yr), medicinal herbs (75,000 MT/yr), other forest products (3.5 lakh MT/yr). Total annual volume: 8.5 lakh metric tons. Operational geography: across Chhattisgarh forest divisions, served through 1,250 primary collection centres. Beneficiary community: 185,000 tribal collector households (2.8 lakh persons dependent). Forest areas covered by official forest compartment boundaries and CGMFPFED-mapped collection zones.
Q5.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CGMFPFED Portal

Q6.r: Chhattisgarh state forests are surveyed and demarcated through official forest compartment maps and beat boundary records maintained by the Forest Department. The 1,250 collection centres are geographically located in CGMFPFED records and the CGMFPFED portal lists each centre. Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN specifies the operational scope of NTFP collection zones. Forest Rights Act CFR titles further demarcate community forest resource boundaries where applicable.
Q6.src: CGMFPFED Portal (collection centre locator); Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Forest Department compartment records

Q7.r: Statutory, legally enforceable right. Under Section 3(1)(c) of the Forest Rights Act 2006, tribal communities have a legally enforceable right to collect, use, and dispose of minor forest produce. PESA 1996 grants Gram Sabha powers over MFP in Fifth Schedule (scheduled) areas, which covers large parts of Chhattisgarh. The Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN further establishes a formal institutional framework for tribal collectors. These constitute the strongest form of community access right.
Q7.src: Forest Rights Act 2006 Section 3(1)(c); PESA 1996 Section 4(m); Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN

Q8.r: No active diversion orders, mining leases, change-of-land-use notifications, SEZ notifications, or compulsory acquisition proceedings are mentioned or indicated for the NTFP collection areas in any of the reviewed sources. CAG audit and Outcome Budget confirm 98% sustainable harvest compliance and continued operational status of all 1,250 collection centres with no legal interruption to community access.
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q9.breakdown: Collection centres establishment ₹45 cr (proportional share of ₹43.2 cr actual); Tribal cooperative support ₹40 cr (₹38.4 cr actual); Quality certification ₹25 cr (₹24.0 cr actual); Market linkage ₹15 cr (₹14.4 cr actual). Allocated: ₹125 cr; Actual spent: ₹120 cr (96% utilisation per CAG).
Q9.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (₹120 cr spent verified); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 (₹125 cr allocated with component breakdown)

Q10.r: All four expenditure components flow entirely to collective/community-level benefits from state forest commons: (1) Collection centres are common aggregation infrastructure serving all 185,000 tribal collectors — not exclusive to any private individual. (2) Tribal cooperative support strengthens community institutions (35 cooperatives, 145,000 members). (3) Quality certification improves price outcomes for all collectors. (4) Market linkage benefits the entire collector community. No expenditure creates assets flowing exclusively to a single private actor. θ₁ numerator = ₹120 cr = full actual expenditure.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN

Q11.r: No scheme expenditure is directed to private assets or infrastructure benefiting exclusively private individuals. All components — collection centre infrastructure, cooperative support, certification, and market linkage — generate community-level collective benefits. Consistent with scheme design which is exclusively oriented toward collective tribal forest commons.
Q11.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 component breakdown

Q12.interp: PASS — θ₁ = 1.00, well above 0.50 threshold. 100% of scheme expenditure flows to common/community resources (forest commons and collective tribal institutions).
Q12.src: Derived from Q9 and Q10 above

Q14.r: Partially excludable. The scheme's institutional benefits (MPP price support, cooperative membership, formal collection entitlements) accrue to registered tribal collector households — a defined group of 185,000 enrolled households. Non-registered or non-tribal individuals are excluded from formal price support and cooperative benefits. However, the underlying FRA-backed right to collect MFP from state forests is non-excludable within the tribal community. Scored 1 (partially excludable) to reflect the exclusion mechanism at the institutional-support level. No fee-based exclusion mechanism exists; exclusion is status-based (tribal collector enrollment), so equity cross-flag with T2 Q19/Q20 is not triggered.
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 6-MFPT/2000/CGN; Forest Rights Act 2006; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (185,000 enrolled collectors)
