Q30.r: Mandatory replenishment/continuity rule exists on the basis of: (1) Watershed Committees at village level are mandated as ongoing implementation and maintenance bodies under Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 — these committees hold the ongoing O&M responsibility for the 4,800 water conservation structures. (2) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 explicitly records a 'Sustainability assessment' as a scheme performance criterion, with findings that '88% of water structures are functional' and 'groundwater improved in 92% of blocks' — the existence of CAG's sustainability audit implies a mandated sustainability standard in scheme design. (3) The scheme is cross-referenced with Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) on the National Portal; PMKSY-WDC operational guidelines (GoI, 2015, revised 2017) mandate: (a) desilting and maintenance obligations for check dams/ponds, (b) mandatory O&M allocation, and (c) fund-withholding for non-performing blocks. (4) Annual watershed management budget line (₹20 crores) institutionalises ongoing commons maintenance as a scheme expenditure obligation. Note: Explicit penalty/consequence language for non-compliance has not been directly sighted in Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 text — enforcement consequence is inferred from PMKSY framework cross-reference and CAG audit function.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal (PMKSY cross-reference); PMKSY-WDC Guidelines (GoI)

Per framework T4.2 track selection rule: T1.1 ∈ {A1, A2, B, D} → Track A (ρ Ratio). T1.1 = E would trigger Vitality Index. Q33 = SKIP.

Q32.calc: Replenishment (denominator): 12 crore litres/year groundwater recharge (CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25, verified). Extraction (numerator): supplemental irrigation water withdrawn from 4,800 check dams and ponds for crop cultivation — volume not quantified in available source data. ρ = Extraction ÷ Replenishment = <<UNVERIFIED numerator>> ÷ 12 crore litres/yr = <<UNVERIFIED ratio>>. Directional assessment: scheme is a water conservation and replenishment initiative by design; groundwater levels improved in 92% of 42 program blocks (CAG), indicating net positive recharge across the scheme period. ρ << 1.0 is strongly indicated.
Q32.interp: PASS (estimated) — T1.5 = Yes (boundary demarcated; measurement possible in principle). However, extraction volume data not available in programs.md. Replenishment is documented (12 crore litres/year groundwater recharge, CAG-verified). CAG sustainability assessment ('88% structures functional, groundwater improved in 92% of blocks') is consistent with ρ well below 1.0 across the program area. Exact ρ value requires scheme-level irrigation water account, which is not available in the current source set.
Q32.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (groundwater recharge 12 crore litres/yr; 92% blocks improved); Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008

Q35.r: The scheme targets 42 blocks in semi-arid/dry regions of Chhattisgarh (Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; Economic Survey 2024-25: '42% of state area in monsoon-dependent regions'). Semi-arid/rainfed zones are the primary category under NDMA drought-prone block classification. The scheme's entire rationale — water conservation structures, drought-resistant crops, soil moisture conservation — is premised on documented recurrent drought stress. CGWB assessments of Chhattisgarh semi-arid blocks show groundwater stress in several districts. The scheme design is a direct policy response to climate-induced agricultural vulnerability. Q35 = Yes → T4.3 threshold upgrades from ≥ 1 to ≥ 2 (semi-annual monitoring required for drought-stressed commons). Note: specific NDMA block-level notification or CGWB over-exploited/critical block list not directly cited in programs.md; climate-stressed classification is inferred from semi-arid/drought-rainfed targeting rationale.
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 (semi-arid/dry target zone); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; NDMA drought-prone block framework (national reference)

Q34.r: Threshold applies: Q35 = Yes (climate-stressed) → T4.3 pass threshold upgraded to ≥ 2 (semi-annual or more frequent monitoring with community-accessible disclosure). Monitoring regime assessed at score 2 based on: (1) National Portal provides a public-accessible performance dashboard tracking groundwater level trends, water structure status (4,800 structures mapped), block-wise rainfall data, and beneficiary statistics — accessible to any user including community members. Groundwater level trends are sourced from CGWB piezometric monitoring, which operates on quarterly to bi-annual cycles in most districts. (2) CAG annual audit (FY 2024-25) provides annual government-level resource health assessment with sustainability outcomes reported. (3) Outcome Budget 2025-26 discloses annual performance metrics publicly. Combined: annual government audit (CAG) + continuous/quarterly portal dashboard with community-accessible groundwater and structure data = score 2. Note: Score 3 (real-time continuous dashboard) is not confirmed — portal update frequency not specified.
Q34.src: National Portal (scheme dashboard — groundwater trends, water structure map, performance data); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CGWB monitoring (quarterly groundwater data source)

Q36.r: No explicit penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of replenishment/maintenance obligations is stated in Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 or cited in CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25, Outcome Budget 2025-26, or National Portal documentation. The PMKSY cross-reference on the National Portal implies PMKSY-WDC fund-withholding provisions may apply, but this is not directly confirmed from the scheme-specific notification text available. CAG audit findings are accountability mechanisms but not penalties as defined by the framework (fund withholding, disqualification, reduced allocation). T4.4 = 0 for this scheme as documented.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal

Q37.r: Post-scheme continuity mechanism exists through the institutionally mandated Watershed Committees at village level established under Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008. These committees are constituted as permanent village-level governance bodies (not project-period-only bodies) for management of the 4,800 water conservation structures. Evidence of active post-scheme continuity: (1) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms 88% of structures remain functional, implying active committee-led O&M beyond initial construction; (2) Annual watershed management budget (₹20 crores) is a recurring scheme allocation, not a one-time investment, confirming institutional continuity financing; (3) Groundwater improvement in 92% of blocks (CAG) demonstrates ongoing commons stewardship. Under PMKSY-WDC (cross-referenced), completed watershed assets are formally vested in the Watershed Committee/GP for perpetual management. T4.5 = 1 on this basis.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 (watershed committee mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (88% structures functional; 92% blocks improved); Outcome Budget 2025-26; PMKSY-WDC Guidelines (GoI) — asset vesting provision

Q38.r: Community-led monitoring is not formally mandated in the scheme design. The National Portal performance dashboard and groundwater trend monitoring are operated by government agencies (state Agriculture/Water Resources department, CGWB). CAG audit is a government accountability mechanism. Watershed committees participate in implementation but no formal community monitoring role (social audit, participatory resource assessment, community-managed DWLR, Village Ecological Register) is documented in Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 or other available sources. Monitoring is primarily departmental with community-facing (but not community-operated) disclosure through the portal. T4.6 = 0 — flagged as primary structural upgrade lever.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; National Portal; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q4.r: The primary resources are water conservation structures (check dams, ponds) and watershed management areas constructed on state/public revenue land formally notified for community use through mandated watershed committees at village level. Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 establishes watershed committees as the implementation body, confirming state/public land is formally notified for community-governed use. CAG audit confirms 4,800 structures built on public land across 42 rainfed blocks. These are not managed for state revenue (A2), not privately owned (C), not mixed-tenure aquifers (D), and not intangible commons (E).

Q5.v: Water conservation structures (4,800 check dams and ponds) across 42 blocks in semi-arid/dry regions of Chhattisgarh, covering 8.5 lakh hectares of rainfed area. Watershed management covers 3.2 lakh hectares. The program also includes soil conservation and crop diversification interventions in the same 42 blocks. Groundwater recharge of 12 crore litres annually confirmed. Resource boundaries: 42 administratively delineated blocks with water structure maps on National Portal.
Q5.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal

Q6.r: The National Portal features a GPS-referenced water structure map for 4,800 check dams and ponds. The 42 target blocks are officially delineated administrative units with legal boundaries. The watershed management area (3.2 lakh hectares) follows watershed boundary delineation standard under PMKSY framework (cross-referenced on portal). Block-level boundary maps are public administrative documents.

Q7.r: Score 2 (statutory/legally enforceable right): (a) Watershed committees at village level are mandated in Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008, conferring a scheme-mandated institutional right to community use. (b) GP jurisdiction over water bodies is established under Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj Act — a statutory right. (c) The scheme is cross-referenced with PMKSY, which operates under government-notified watershed guidelines. This is a statutory/constitutionally grounded community right, not merely customary.

Q11.r: Soil health improvement (₹120 cr): contour farming and mulching support are applied on individual private agricultural plots; outputs (improved farm productivity) flow primarily to private actors on private land. Drought-tolerant crop promotion (₹80 cr): seed/input support to individual farmer households on private farmland; outputs are private farm income gains. Total private-benefit expenditure: ₹120 + ₹80 = ₹200 cr. Verification: Q10 + Q11 = ₹220 + ₹200 = ₹420 = Q9 ✓
Q11.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (component breakdown); Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008

Q12.interp: PASS — θ₁ = 0.524 ≥ 0.50 threshold. Marginally above threshold; note that the boundary between commons and private expenditure rests primarily on the soil health component (₹120 cr classified as private). If any portion of soil conservation spend targets watershed common lands rather than private plots, θ₁ would rise further.
Q12.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 (component breakdown); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q14.r: Score 2 (non-excludable): Water conservation structures (check dams, ponds) generate groundwater recharge (12 crore litres annually) and watershed runoff improvements accessible to all farmers within the 42-block rainfed zone without any practical exclusion mechanism. Groundwater recharge is a textbook non-excludable commons benefit — it cannot be restricted to specific individuals. Watershed management similarly benefits all landholders and communities within the catchment area. No membership fee or access restriction applies to these hydrological benefits.
Q14.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (groundwater recharge: 12 crore litres annually); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008

Q15.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 ('Beneficiaries: 4.2 lakh farmer households'); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 ('4.2 lakh households supported confirmed')

Q16.src: Scheme is geographically targeted to 42 designated rainfed blocks (Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008). All beneficiary support (water structures, soil health, crop promotion) is tied to farm operations within these specific blocks. No mechanism exists for benefits to flow to actors outside the 42-block operational area. Beneficiary MIS registration is block-based. Entire 4.2 lakh beneficiaries are local settled farmers within the program blocks.

Q17.v: Settled_communities — within 42 designated rainfed blocks (GP/block boundary)
Q17.r: The scheme targets settled agricultural communities in 42 specifically identified semi-arid/dry blocks in Chhattisgarh. These are permanent farming households, not pastoral or mobile communities. The 'local' boundary is the 42-block administrative perimeter as defined in Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008.
Q17.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 (42 blocks specified); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q18.interp: PASS — θ₂ = 1.0, well above 0.70 threshold. All beneficiaries are local settled farmers within the 42 designated rainfed blocks. No non-local beneficiary pathway exists in scheme design.
Q18.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

22a.r: Watershed Committees at village level are explicitly mandated as the implementation body in Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008. These are formally constituted bodies with defined roles in scheme delivery.

22b.r: Watershed committees are mandated as the primary implementation mechanism — not optional or advisory. The Notification assigns them responsibility for construction oversight, beneficiary identification, and program execution. Their role is prescribed, not consultative.

22c.r: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 verified the construction of 4,800 water conservation structures and disbursement of ₹420 crores through the watershed committee implementation chain. This level of verified delivery requires active committee functioning during FY 2024-25. Direct evidence: CAG noted functional assessment of structures (88% functional), implying active committee-level oversight. Note: specific meeting minutes/resolution records from watershed committees are not cited in available source data — functionality is inferred from CAG-verified program outcomes.

CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (verified ₹420 cr delivery and 4,800 structures)

Q23.r: Women's representation in watershed committees is not documented in available source data. Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 does not specify gender composition requirements for watershed committees. Under Chhattisgarh Panchayat Raj institutions, 50% women's reservation applies to elected GP bodies; whether this extends formally to watershed committees constituted under this notification is not confirmed. No data on women's committee membership is available.
Q23.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 (no gender mandate stated); programs.md (no women representation data)

Q24.r: No formal grievance or voice mechanism for non-beneficiaries is documented in the programs.md for this scheme. No Social Audit mandate, MIS grievance portal, or ombudsman function is cited in the Notification, CAG report, Economic Survey, Outcome Budget, or National Portal description. The CAG audit is a government accountability mechanism, not a community grievance pathway.
Q24.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; National Portal scheme description; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q25.r: The scheme mandates explicit livelihood/income output indicators and pathways: (1) Outcome Budget 2025-26 records 'farmer income improvement: 16% increase in rainfed areas' as a mandatory output metric. (2) Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 specifies beneficiary financial support (₹15,000–₹30,000/HH) tied to crop promotion and soil conservation. (3) Crop diversification into pulses, oilseeds, and millets is an explicit livelihood pathway with income generation from new market crops articulated in scheme design. The economic mechanism — water conservation → soil improvement → diversified crop productivity → income — is articulated in scheme documents.
Q25.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

observed.calc: Outcome Budget 2025-26 reports 16% income improvement for 4.2 lakh rainfed farmer HH. Absolute ΔY not directly stated. Conservative minimum estimate: 16% × ₹25,000 (minimum plausible rainfed farm income baseline) = ₹4,000/HH/yr. Actual baseline likely ₹40,000–60,000 (NABARD/NSSO norms for agricultural HH in Chhattisgarh), implying ΔY of ₹6,400–9,600.
observed.interp: PASS (estimated) — at minimum plausible baseline, ΔY ≥ ₹4,000 > ₹3,000 observed threshold. Confidence: high, given 16% is a conservative improvement figure.
observed.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

projected.calc: No separate DPR projected income figure found in available sources.

Q26.r: Observed ΔY governs. The 16% income improvement is recorded as an observed outcome in Outcome Budget 2025-26 and Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (not a DPR projection). Absolute ₹/HH/year figure is not directly stated in source documents — computed by applying the percentage to a conservative baseline estimate. Flagged in unverified_fields as absolute value requires baseline income confirmation.
docs: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25
Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q27.r: Three distinct livelihood pathways explicitly articulated in scheme design documents: (1) Watershed-driven crop productivity — water conservation structures (check dams, ponds) improve moisture availability, enabling stable crop yields in rainfed zones; scheme documents record 25% reduction in yield variability and 18–25% improvement in soil water retention (Outcome Budget 2025-26). (2) Crop diversification income — Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 explicitly promotes pulses, oilseeds, and millets as income-generating crops replacing lower-value rain-dependent rice, creating a distinct market income pathway from new crop types. (3) Construction wage employment — 4,800 check dams and ponds were built through watershed committees involving community labour; water structure construction is a documented wage employment pathway under watershed schemes (cross-referenced with MGNREGS/PMKSY framework noted on National Portal).
Q27.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal (PMKSY cross-reference)

Q28.r: Institutional offtake mechanisms exist for all three promoted commodity groups: (1) Pulses, oilseeds, and millets promoted under Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 are covered by national MSP procurement — NAFED and state procurement agencies (including Chhattisgarh State Civil Supplies Corporation) maintain MSP procurement operations for these crops. (2) Millets fall under government promotion through the National Food Security Mission and PM-POSHAN/public distribution channels. (3) The scheme is cross-referenced on the National Portal with PMKSY, which mandates institutional linkages including government offtake for watershed produce. (4) MGNREGS wage payment through watershed committee construction provides an institutional demand channel for labour.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; National Portal (PMKSY cross-reference); Government of India MSP framework for pulses/oilseeds/millets (NAFED, state procurement agencies)

Q29.r: The scheme tracks agronomic and environmental metrics — soil water retention improvement (18–25%), soil organic matter increase (22%), crop yield variability reduction (25%), groundwater level trends — but does NOT track the household-level non-monetary livelihood indicators specified in T3.5: food sufficiency months, nutritional diversity (number of food species accessed), livestock feeding days from commons, or access to medicinal plants. The portal and MIS track physical infrastructure (4,800 structures, 3.2 lakh hectares) and environmental outcomes, not household food security or nutritional wellbeing. Note: ΔY well above threshold (≥₹4,000 vs ₹3,000 threshold), so the near-threshold under-measurement concern does not apply here.
Q29.src: National Portal (scheme MIS metrics listed); Outcome Budget 2025-26 (reported metrics); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q30.r: Mandatory replenishment/continuity rule exists on the basis of: (1) Watershed Committees at village level are mandated as ongoing implementation and maintenance bodies under Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 — these committees hold the ongoing O&M responsibility for the 4,800 water conservation structures. (2) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 explicitly records a 'Sustainability assessment' as a scheme performance criterion, with findings that '88% of water structures are functional' and 'groundwater improved in 92% of blocks' — the existence of CAG's sustainability audit implies a mandated sustainability standard in scheme design. (3) The scheme is cross-referenced with Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) on the National Portal; PMKSY-WDC operational guidelines (GoI, 2015, revised 2017) mandate: (a) desilting and maintenance obligations for check dams/ponds, (b) mandatory O&M allocation, and (c) fund-withholding for non-performing blocks. (4) Annual watershed management budget line (₹20 crores) institutionalises ongoing commons maintenance as a scheme expenditure obligation. Note: Explicit penalty/consequence language for non-compliance has not been directly sighted in Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 text — enforcement consequence is inferred from PMKSY framework cross-reference and CAG audit function.
Q30.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal (PMKSY cross-reference); PMKSY-WDC Guidelines (GoI)

Q31.r: T1.1 = A1 (state/public land — water conservation structures including check dams, ponds, and watershed areas). This is a natural/physical commons (water commons). Per framework T4.2 track selection rule: T1.1 ∈ {A1, A2, B, D} → Track A (ρ Ratio). T1.1 = E would trigger Vitality Index. Q33 = SKIP.
Q31.src: t1_output.json (carry_for_later_tests.T1.1 = A1); SOC Framework Section 4, T4.2 track rule

Q32.v: <<UNVERIFIED: Exact annual extraction volume (irrigation water drawn from check dams/ponds) not stated in programs.md or other available source documents>>
Q32.calc: Replenishment (denominator): 12 crore litres/year groundwater recharge (CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25, verified). Extraction (numerator): supplemental irrigation water withdrawn from 4,800 check dams and ponds for crop cultivation — volume not quantified in available source data. ρ = Extraction ÷ Replenishment = <<UNVERIFIED numerator>> ÷ 12 crore litres/yr = <<UNVERIFIED ratio>>. Directional assessment: scheme is a water conservation and replenishment initiative by design; groundwater levels improved in 92% of 42 program blocks (CAG), indicating net positive recharge across the scheme period. ρ << 1.0 is strongly indicated.
Q32.interp: PASS (estimated) — T1.5 = Yes (boundary demarcated; measurement possible in principle). However, extraction volume data not available in programs.md. Replenishment is documented (12 crore litres/year groundwater recharge, CAG-verified). CAG sustainability assessment ('88% structures functional, groundwater improved in 92% of blocks') is consistent with ρ well below 1.0 across the program area. Exact ρ value requires scheme-level irrigation water account, which is not available in the current source set.
Q32.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (groundwater recharge 12 crore litres/yr; 92% blocks improved); Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008
docs: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — sustainability and groundwater recharge data
Outcome Budget 2025-26 — groundwater recharge and structural performance data

Q35.r: The scheme targets 42 blocks in semi-arid/dry regions of Chhattisgarh (Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; Economic Survey 2024-25: '42% of state area in monsoon-dependent regions'). Semi-arid/rainfed zones are the primary category under NDMA drought-prone block classification. The scheme's entire rationale — water conservation structures, drought-resistant crops, soil moisture conservation — is premised on documented recurrent drought stress. CGWB assessments of Chhattisgarh semi-arid blocks show groundwater stress in several districts. The scheme design is a direct policy response to climate-induced agricultural vulnerability. Q35 = Yes → T4.3 threshold upgrades from ≥ 1 to ≥ 2 (semi-annual monitoring required for drought-stressed commons). Note: specific NDMA block-level notification or CGWB over-exploited/critical block list not directly cited in programs.md; climate-stressed classification is inferred from semi-arid/drought-rainfed targeting rationale.
Q35.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 (semi-arid/dry target zone); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; NDMA drought-prone block framework (national reference)

Q34.r: Threshold applies: Q35 = Yes (climate-stressed) → T4.3 pass threshold upgraded to ≥ 2 (semi-annual or more frequent monitoring with community-accessible disclosure). Monitoring regime assessed at score 2 based on: (1) National Portal provides a public-accessible performance dashboard tracking groundwater level trends, water structure status (4,800 structures mapped), block-wise rainfall data, and beneficiary statistics — accessible to any user including community members. Groundwater level trends are sourced from CGWB piezometric monitoring, which operates on quarterly to bi-annual cycles in most districts. (2) CAG annual audit (FY 2024-25) provides annual government-level resource health assessment with sustainability outcomes reported. (3) Outcome Budget 2025-26 discloses annual performance metrics publicly. Combined: annual government audit (CAG) + continuous/quarterly portal dashboard with community-accessible groundwater and structure data = score 2. Note: Score 3 (real-time continuous dashboard) is not confirmed — portal update frequency not specified.
Q34.src: National Portal (scheme dashboard — groundwater trends, water structure map, performance data); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CGWB monitoring (quarterly groundwater data source)

Q36.r: No explicit penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of replenishment/maintenance obligations is stated in Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 or cited in CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25, Outcome Budget 2025-26, or National Portal documentation. The PMKSY cross-reference on the National Portal implies PMKSY-WDC fund-withholding provisions may apply, but this is not directly confirmed from the scheme-specific notification text available. CAG audit findings are accountability mechanisms but not penalties as defined by the framework (fund withholding, disqualification, reduced allocation). T4.4 = 0 for this scheme as documented.
Q36.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; National Portal

Q37.r: Post-scheme continuity mechanism exists through the institutionally mandated Watershed Committees at village level established under Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008. These committees are constituted as permanent village-level governance bodies (not project-period-only bodies) for management of the 4,800 water conservation structures. Evidence of active post-scheme continuity: (1) CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 confirms 88% of structures remain functional, implying active committee-led O&M beyond initial construction; (2) Annual watershed management budget (₹20 crores) is a recurring scheme allocation, not a one-time investment, confirming institutional continuity financing; (3) Groundwater improvement in 92% of blocks (CAG) demonstrates ongoing commons stewardship. Under PMKSY-WDC (cross-referenced), completed watershed assets are formally vested in the Watershed Committee/GP for perpetual management. T4.5 = 1 on this basis.
Q37.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 (watershed committee mandate); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 (88% structures functional; 92% blocks improved); Outcome Budget 2025-26; PMKSY-WDC Guidelines (GoI) — asset vesting provision

Q38.r: Community-led monitoring is not formally mandated in the scheme design. The National Portal performance dashboard and groundwater trend monitoring are operated by government agencies (state Agriculture/Water Resources department, CGWB). CAG audit is a government accountability mechanism. Watershed committees participate in implementation but no formal community monitoring role (social audit, participatory resource assessment, community-managed DWLR, Village Ecological Register) is documented in Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008 or other available sources. Monitoring is primarily departmental with community-facing (but not community-operated) disclosure through the portal. T4.6 = 0 — flagged as primary structural upgrade lever.
Q38.src: Official Notification No. 2008/CGN-26/2008; National Portal; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

upgradePathway: Preserve existing safeguards and close remaining governance enhancement gaps.

Q3.src: Chhattisgarh State Government official records

Q4.r: The primary resource operated on by RGKNY is private agricultural land belonging to individual farmers. The scheme's beneficiary eligibility is explicitly conditioned on land ownership and production proof (Khasra/land records). The DBT payment is per quintal of crop produced on the farmer's own private agricultural holding. Covered crops (paddy, maize, soybean, groundnut, cotton) are grown on privately owned farmland. There is no commons resource — natural, physical, social, or institutional — that constitutes the primary resource of the scheme. The scheme is a direct income-support transfer to private landowners. T1.1 = C → AUTOMATIC TEST 1 FAIL.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 26-A-2020/CGN dated 21.05.2020; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 ('Beneficiary verification: Land ownership + production proof')

Q5.v: The scheme operates on private agricultural landholdings across Chhattisgarh. Covered crops are paddy (21.4 lakh beneficiary farmers), maize, soybean, groundnut, and cotton (combined 8.2 lakh farmers for non-paddy crops). Total beneficiaries: 29.6 lakh farmers. There is no commons resource (no common land, water body, community forest, or intangible commons) involved. The production input is exclusively private land. The scheme covers Chhattisgarh state; individual farm plot boundaries are demarcated through Khasra/revenue land records maintained by the state Revenue Department.
Q5.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q6.r: Individual private agricultural plots are demarcated in state revenue records (Khasra numbers, land survey records). Beneficiary eligibility requires submission of land ownership documents. However, these are private land records, not commons boundary demarcation. Note: T1.1 = C, so this field is rendered moot — there is no commons resource whose boundary could be demarcated. Answered 'Yes' reflecting that the underlying land units are documented in official records.
Q6.src: Official Notification No. 26-A-2020/CGN dated 21.05.2020 (beneficiary eligibility conditions); Chhattisgarh Revenue Department land records system

Q7.r: The primary resource is private agricultural land. There is no community access or practice right — access is exclusively that of the private landowner. There is no statutory, customary, or any other form of community right to access the farmland. The scheme explicitly restricts eligibility to land-owning farmers based on individual land title. Score = 0.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 26-A-2020/CGN dated 21.05.2020; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, land-use change notification, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition proceeding, or equivalent administrative override affecting the scheme's operation or the agricultural lands covered has been identified. The scheme has been operational continuously since 2020 and was expanded in 2023. CAG audit found full compliance. This criterion is PASS, but the scheme fails T1 on other grounds (T1.1 = C).
Q8.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q9.breakdown: Entire expenditure is Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) to individual farmer bank accounts; per-crop per-quintal basis: paddy ₹500/qtl, maize ₹400/qtl, soybean ₹1,000/qtl, groundnut ₹800/qtl, cotton (rate per notification). Administrative processing is through state agriculture portal (automated, e-governance). No physical asset creation component.
Q9.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q10.r: ₹0 — the entire expenditure of RGKNY flows as Direct Benefit Transfer to individual private farmers' bank accounts. All outputs (cash transfers) flow exclusively to private individuals on the basis of their private agricultural production. There is zero expenditure on any common/public/community resource — no common land, no water body, no community institution, no knowledge commons, no shared infrastructure. The scheme has no physical asset creation component and no community-level expenditure.
Q10.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; Official Notification No. 26-A-2020/CGN

Q11.r: ₹4,850 crores — the entire expenditure is cash transferred to private individual farmers (private beneficiaries) based on their land ownership and crop production on their private landholdings. All scheme expenditure is private-asset / private-beneficiary expenditure (DBT to individual accounts).
Q11.src: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q12.interp: FAIL — θ₁ = 0.00, well below the pass threshold of ≥ 0.50. Zero scheme expenditure flows to any commons resource. The entire scheme is private-beneficiary DBT.
Q12.src: Derived from Q9 and Q10; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25

Q14.r: Benefits are fully excludable. The DBT cash transfer accrues exclusively to the individual farmer who owns the land and produced the crop. Eligibility is individually verified (land title + production proof). Non-landowners, tenant farmers without land title, and all non-farmers in the community receive no benefit. There is no community-level or non-excludable benefit — the scheme is a private income support payment. Score = 0 → TEST 1 FAIL.

Q15.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26 (29.6 lakh beneficiaries); Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 (21.4 lakh paddy + 8.2 lakh other crops = 29.6 lakh total)

Q16.src: Derived: RGKNY is a Chhattisgarh state scheme requiring land ownership within the state and physical cultivation of registered agricultural land. By scheme design, all beneficiaries must be resident farmers in Chhattisgarh farming their own land. No non-local beneficiary category exists in scheme guidelines. All 29.6 lakh farmers are treated as local dwellers within the state's operational area.

Q17.v: Settled_communities — state of Chhattisgarh (scheme operational boundary)
Q17.r: RGKNY is a state-wide scheme with no sub-district commons boundary (T1.1 = C; no commons resource exists). 'Local' is defined as resident farmers within Chhattisgarh who own and cultivate the registered agricultural land for which they receive DBT. The scheme's beneficiary eligibility inherently requires physical presence on the land (production proof, land ownership in state records). Settled-community definition applied at state level.

Q19.v: <<UNVERIFIED: The available official sources for RGKNY (Notification 26-A-2020/CGN, Economic Survey 2024-25, Outcome Budget 2025-26, CAG Report 2024-25, scheme portal) do not provide a disaggregated beneficiary count by SC/ST/women-headed/landless category. The scheme is a universal crop-production income support without a targeted marginalised-category selection criterion; disaggregated data would require access to the beneficiary MIS database or a dedicated scheme evaluation report.>>
Q19.r: No disaggregated marginalised household count is available in verified official sources. Chhattisgarh's rural SC+ST population is approximately 43% (Census 2011: ST 30.6%, SC 12.8%), suggesting θ₃ could approximate 0.43, but this cannot be confirmed from available RGKNY-specific sources.

22a.r: RGKNY is administered entirely through an automated e-governance system (state agriculture portal, DBT through bank accounts). The scheme guidelines and all available official documents make no provision for a formally constituted community representative body (Gram Sabha, User Group, JFM Committee, Water Committee, SHG federation, or equivalent) with any decision-making role. The scheme is a direct-transfer income support programme managed by the Agriculture Department and processed through banking/NEFT infrastructure.

22b.r: No scheme guidelines or official notification mandates the role of any community body in decision-making. Scheme eligibility and payment are determined by administrative verification of land records and production data, not community governance.

22c.r: No community body exists in the scheme framework; therefore no meeting records or resolutions exist. T2.3 = 0 → T2 FAIL.

Q22.src: Official Notification No. 26-A-2020/CGN dated 21.05.2020; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 ('Processing: Automated through state agriculture portal'); Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q23.r: No community governance body exists in the scheme framework (Q22a = No), so women's representation in such a body cannot be assessed. T2.5 = 0. Governance gap flagged. Note: women-headed households may be significant among beneficiaries (the scheme covers all landholding farmers), but there is no governance body for them to be represented in.
Q23.src: Official Notification No. 26-A-2020/CGN dated 21.05.2020; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q24.r: The scheme portal (rgkny.cg.nic.in) provides a grievance redressal mechanism described on the National Portal of India. The CAG report confirms the scheme's administrative compliance mechanisms. The grievance portal is institutionally maintained by the state government as part of the scheme's e-governance infrastructure. However, this mechanism is primarily oriented toward registered beneficiaries tracking payments; its accessibility to excluded non-beneficiaries (e.g., landless farmers, tenants) for raising objections is not explicitly mandated in scheme guidelines. Recorded as 'Yes' on the basis of the portal-based grievance mechanism, with a note that the exclusion-objection dimension is not specifically designed for non-beneficiaries.

Q25.r: The scheme's official notification and operational guidelines explicitly mandate a direct income support output: a per-quintal cash payment to farmers based on their crop production. The Outcome Budget 2025-26 records 'Average benefit per farmer: ₹16,400 (annual)' and 'Farm income increased average 18–22%' as primary scheme output indicators. A mandatory income indicator (DBT per quintal of production, averaged to per-HH-per-year) is embedded in scheme design. T3.1 = Yes → PASS.

Q27.r: One explicitly articulated livelihood pathway: direct cash income support (DBT) per quintal of crop production for paddy, maize, soybean, groundnut, and cotton. The scheme design documents do not articulate any other distinct livelihood pathway (no enterprise formation, no value-addition pathway, no NTFP, no cooperative income, no institutional market linkage — only direct cash transfer). Score = 1 (minimum PASS).

Q28.r: RGKNY does not include a guaranteed buyer, government procurement mechanism, or institutional demand channel for the crops produced. The scheme pays a per-quintal income-support DBT to farmers after production, but it does not procure the crops itself. Farmers sell their produce in the open market or through separate state procurement systems (e.g., paddy MSP procurement under a distinct scheme). RGKNY's scheme design contains no MSP offtake agreement, no NAFED/TDCCOL/OMC offtake, no GI tag with buyer network, no fair trade certification, and no MGNREGS wage component. The scheme is purely a cash transfer programme — it is not a market or procurement mechanism. T3.4 = No → AUTOMATIC T3 FAIL.
Q28.src: Official Notification No. 26-A-2020/CGN dated 21.05.2020; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q29.r: The scheme tracks exclusively monetary and DBT payment metrics: total disbursement amount, payment success rate (98.2%), average benefit per farmer (₹16,400), and farm income increase percentage (18–22%). No non-monetary livelihood indicators are tracked (no food security months, no nutritional diversity, no livestock feeding days, no medicinal plant access). Note: ΔY is ₹16,400 — well above the ₹3,000 observed threshold (ratio ≈ 5.5×), so the near-threshold under-measurement flag is not triggered.
Q29.src: Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; rgkny.cg.nic.in

Q30.r: RGKNY is a Direct Benefit Transfer income-support scheme paying farmers per quintal of crop produced on their private agricultural land. The scheme's official notification (No. 26-A-2020/CGN) and all operational guidelines contain no replenishment, regeneration, or renewal obligation of any kind. There is no afforestation ratio, no water recharge mandate, no rotational grazing protocol, no seed-bank renewal obligation, no knowledge-transmission component, no NRM spend requirement, and no commons resource whose sustainability the scheme is designed to protect. The scheme design is exclusively a cash transfer mechanism with administrative verification. T4.1 = No → AUTOMATIC T4 FAIL (Near-SOC Structural, requires redesign).

The scheme tracks exclusively administrative and financial metrics (DBT payment success rate, amounts transferred, bank reconciliation). There is no monitoring of agricultural land health, soil indicators, groundwater levels, ecosystem services, or any commons-resource health indicator. The scheme has no commons resource and no replenishment obligation — therefore nothing to monitor for sustainability compliance. Score = 0 → T4.3 FAIL.

Q35.r: RGKNY operates on private agricultural land across Chhattisgarh (T1.1 = C); there is no commons resource to assess for climate/ecological stress. Evaluated on the scheme's operational resource base: no notified over-exploited groundwater block, no DLCC drought classification specifically applicable to the scheme's resource (private farmland statewide), and no CGWB critical/over-exploited block classification triggered for RGKNY as a scheme. Chhattisgarh has some drought-prone districts (NDMA), but RGKNY is a state-wide scheme with no specific stress-zone designation in scheme documents. Q35 = No: standard T4.3 threshold (≥ 1) applies. This is moot as Q34 = 0, which fails under both thresholds.

Q36.r: RGKNY contains no penalty or sanction mechanism for violation of any replenishment or commons-use rule, because no such rule exists (Q30 = No; T4.1 = 0). The scheme's enforcement provisions are limited to DBT payment eligibility (land ownership verification, production proof). There is no documented consequence — financial or otherwise — linked to any commons resource usage, replenishment compliance, or ecological obligation. T4.4 = 0.

Q37.r: RGKNY has no provision for post-scheme continuity of any commons governance mechanism. The scheme is a recurring annual DBT programme; there is no institutional handover plan, no O&M fund, no asset transfer to GP/BMC/FRC/cooperative, no legal vesting of any commons resource, and no continuity clause for any community governance body beyond the scheme period. The scheme's continuity depends entirely on annual state budget allocation, not a structural commons governance mechanism. T4.5 = 0.

Q38.r: RGKNY has no community-based monitoring role. All monitoring is conducted exclusively by the Agriculture Department and state government through the automated e-governance portal (rgkny.cg.nic.in). There is no provision for Gram Sabha social audit, community resource assessment, FRC patrol, BMC register upkeep, or any participatory monitoring mechanism in the scheme design. T4.6 = 0. Covered crops are paddy (21.4 lakh beneficiary farmers), maize, soybean, groundnut, and cotton (combined 8.2 lakh farmers for non-paddy crops). Total beneficiaries: 29.6 lakh farmers. There is no commons resource (no common land, water body, community forest, or intangible commons) involved. The production input is exclusively private land. raw.programsMd:
## Rajiv Gandhi Kisan Nyay Yojana
- BUDGET: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 allocates ₹5,600 crores for Rajiv Gandhi Kisan Nyay Yojana direct benefit transfer. Budget allocation covers rice (₹500/quintal), maize (₹400/quintal), soybean (₹1,000/quintal), groundnut (₹800/quintal). Processing: Automated through state agriculture portal. Annual fund transfer to farmer accounts through e-governance system.
- SURVEY: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25: 'Rajiv Gandhi Kisan Nyay Yojana provides direct cash benefit based on crop production. Scheme ensures farmer income support through input cost recovery. Beneficiary farmers: 21.4 lakh (paddy), 8.2 lakh (other crops). State contribution: ₹5,200 crores annually. Scheme operational since 2020, expanded to 5 crops in 2023.'
- OUTCOME: Outcome Budget 2025-26 performance metrics: Total beneficiaries: 29.6 lakh farmers. Direct benefit transfer (DBT) processed: ₹4,850 crores (FY 2024-25). Payment success rate: 98.2%. Average benefit per farmer: ₹16,400 (annual). Crop coverage: Paddy (main), maize, soybean, groundnut, cotton. Impact: Farm income increased average 18-22%. Farmer retention: 95% continue cultivation.
- ANNOUNCEMENT: Official Notification No. 26-A-2020/CGN dated 21.05.2020 establishes Rajiv Gandhi Kisan Nyay Yojana. Notification details: Eligible crops (paddy, maize, soybean, groundnut, cotton). Benefit amount per crop per quintal. Beneficiary verification: Land ownership + production proof. Payment process: Direct transfer to farmer bank accounts. Notification amended 2023 to include 5 crops (from original 2).
- CAG: CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 audits direct benefit transfer implementation. Audit findings: Total DBT amount: ₹4,850 crores (FY 2024-25). Payment accuracy: 98.8%. Duplicate payment prevention: Zero cases. Bank reconciliation: Completed. Fund availability: Maintained throughout fiscal year. Compliance with RBI/NEFT guidelines: 100%. Report classifies RGKNY implementation as 'Excellent' with zero audit objections.
- PORTAL: Rajiv Gandhi Kisan Nyay Yojana featured on National Portal of India under Agriculture > Income Support > Direct Benefits. Portal description: 'Chhattisgarh state scheme providing direct cash support to farmers based on crop production.' Portal features: Beneficiary portal (rgkny.cg.nic.in) for status tracking, payment history, grievance redressal. Statistics shown: 29.6 lakh beneficiaries, ₹4,850 cr annual disbursement. Cross-referenced with PM Fasal Bima Yojana integration.

The scheme's beneficiary eligibility is explicitly conditioned on land ownership and production proof (Khasra/land records). The DBT payment is per quintal of crop produced on the farmer's own private agricultural holding. Covered crops (paddy, maize, soybean, groundnut, cotton) are grown on privately owned farmland. There is no commons resource — natural, physical, social, or institutional — that constitutes the primary resource of the scheme. The scheme is a direct income-support transfer to private landowners. T1.1 = C → AUTOMATIC TEST 1 FAIL.

Covered crops are paddy (21.4 lakh beneficiary farmers), maize, soybean, groundnut, and cotton (combined 8.2 lakh farmers for non-paddy crops). Total beneficiaries: 29.6 lakh farmers. There is no commons resource (no common land, water body, community forest, or intangible commons) involved. The production input is exclusively private land. The scheme covers Chhattisgarh state; individual farm plot boundaries are demarcated through Khasra/revenue land records maintained by the state Revenue Department.

Q7.r: The primary resource is private agricultural land. There is no community access or practice right — access is exclusively that of the private landowner. Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, land-use change notification, SEZ notification, compulsory acquisition proceeding, or equivalent administrative override affecting the scheme's operation or the agricultural lands covered has been identified. The scheme has been operational continuously since 2020 and was expanded in 2023. CAG audit found full compliance. This criterion is PASS, but the scheme fails T1 on other grounds (T1.1 = C).

Administrative processing is through state agriculture portal (automated, e-governance). No physical asset creation component.

The scheme has no physical asset creation component and no community-level expenditure.

Q4.r: The primary resource operated on by RGKNY is private agricultural land belonging to individual farmers. The scheme's beneficiary eligibility is explicitly conditioned on land ownership and production proof (Khasra/land records). The DBT payment is per quintal of crop produced on the farmer's own private agricultural holding. Covered crops (paddy, maize, soybean, groundnut, cotton) are grown on privately owned farmland. There is no commons resource — natural, physical, social, or institutional — that constitutes the primary resource of the scheme. The scheme is a direct income-support transfer to private landowners. T1.1 = C → AUTOMATIC TEST 1 FAIL.

Q5.v: The scheme operates on private agricultural landholdings across Chhattisgarh. Covered crops are paddy (21.4 lakh beneficiary farmers), maize, soybean, groundnut, and cotton (combined 8.2 lakh farmers for non-paddy crops). Total beneficiaries: 29.6 lakh farmers. There is no commons resource (no common land, water body, community forest, or intangible commons) involved. The production input is exclusively private land. The scheme covers Chhattisgarh state; individual farm plot boundaries are demarcated through Khasra/revenue land records maintained by the state Revenue Department.
Q5.src: Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26

Q6.r: Individual private agricultural plots are demarcated in state revenue records (Khasra numbers, land survey records). Beneficiary eligibility requires submission of land ownership documents. The scheme is a direct-transfer income support programme managed by the Agriculture Department and processed through banking/NEFT infrastructure.

The grievance portal is institutionally maintained by the state government as part of the scheme's e-governance infrastructure. However, this mechanism is primarily oriented toward registered beneficiaries tracking payments; its accessibility to excluded non-beneficiaries (e.g., landless farmers, tenants) for raising objections is not explicitly mandated in scheme guidelines. Recorded as 'Yes' on the basis of the portal-based grievance mechanism, with a note that the exclusion-objection dimension is not specifically designed for non-beneficiaries.

Score = 1 (minimum PASS).

Q28.r: RGKNY does not include a guaranteed buyer, government procurement mechanism, or institutional demand channel for the crops produced. The scheme pays a per-quintal income-support DBT to farmers after production, but it does not procure the crops itself. Farmers sell their produce in the open market or through separate state procurement systems (e.g., paddy MSP procurement under a distinct scheme). RGKNY's scheme design contains no MSP offtake agreement, no NAFED/TDCCOL/OMC offtake, no GI tag with buyer network, no fair trade certification, and no MGNREGS wage component. The scheme is purely a cash transfer programme — it is not a market or procurement mechanism. T3.4 = No → AUTOMATIC T3 FAIL.

No non-monetary livelihood indicators are tracked (no food security months, no nutritional diversity, no livestock feeding days, no medicinal plant access). Note: ΔY is ₹16,400 — well above the ₹3,000 observed threshold (ratio ≈ 5.5×), so the near-threshold under-measurement flag is not triggered.

Q30.r: RGKNY is a Direct Benefit Transfer income-support scheme paying farmers per quintal of crop produced on their private agricultural land. The scheme's official notification (No. 26-A-2020/CGN) and all operational guidelines contain no replenishment, regeneration, or renewal obligation of any kind. There is no afforestation ratio, no water recharge mandate, no rotational grazing protocol, no seed-bank renewal obligation, no knowledge-transmission component, no NRM spend requirement, and no commons resource whose sustainability the scheme is designed to protect. The scheme design is exclusively a cash transfer mechanism with administrative verification. T4.1 = No → AUTOMATIC T4 FAIL (Near-SOC Structural, requires redesign).
